title: Income Timing Strategies
description: Master income recognition timing techniques to minimize tax liability across periods

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Income timing is one of the most powerful tax planning tools. By controlling WHEN income is recognized, taxpayers can manage marginal tax rates, utilize lower brackets, defer taxes, and coordinate with deductions. CPAs must master both the strategies and the limitations.

sections.title: Core Timing Principles
sections.content: Income timing strategies work because of the time value of money and progressive tax rates. Deferring income delays tax payment (interest-free loan from government) and may shift income to years with lower rates. Accelerating income may be advantageous when rates are expected to increase or when income can offset expiring losses.

sections.title: Cash vs Accrual Method Impact
rows: When all events test met + amount determinable

sections.title: Cash Method Deferral Techniques
items: Delay billing: Send invoices after year-end for services rendered late in year
Defer receipt: Arrange for payments to be received in following year
Installment sales: Spread gain recognition over payment period
Deferred compensation: Negotiate payment timing with employers
BEWARE: Constructive receipt doctrine limits aggressive deferral

sections.title: Constructive Receipt Doctrine
sections.content: Income is constructively received when it's credited to your account, set apart for you, or made available without substantial limitation. You can't turn your back on income that's available. Example: December check mailed Dec 28—constructively received in current year even if not deposited until January.

sections.title: Constructive Receipt Exceptions
items: Substantial limitations or restrictions on access
Amount not yet determinable
Written deferral agreement BEFORE services performed
Employer-established non-qualified deferred compensation plans (§409A)
Installment sale elections properly structured

sections.title: Accrual Method Planning
rows: Long-term contracts—percentage completion vs. completed contract

sections.title: Income Acceleration Strategies
items: Accelerate into year with expiring NOLs or capital losses
Pull income into year before expected rate increase
Roth conversions in low-income years
Recognize gain before moving to high-tax state
Accelerate income to utilize expiring credits

sections.title: Example: Year-End Timing Decision
sections.content:
Sarah, a consultant (cash basis), completes a $50,000 project on December 20. She expects to be in the 32% bracket this year but 24% next year due to retirement.

Option A: Bill December 21, receive payment December 30
• Tax: $50,000 × 32% = $16,000 current year

Option B: Bill January 2, receive payment January 15
• Tax: $50,000 × 24% = $12,000 next year
• PV of deferral benefit (assuming 5% rate): ~$570
• Total benefit: $4,000 rate differential + $570 deferral = $4,570

Recommendation: Defer billing to next year, saving $4,570.

sections.title: Section 409A Compliance
sections.content: Deferred compensation arrangements must comply with §409A. Violations result in immediate income inclusion PLUS 20% penalty PLUS interest. Key rules: deferral elections made before services performed, distributions only on specified events (separation, disability, death, change in control, fixed date).

sections.title: Memory Aid: Income Timing
sections.content:
'DEFER or PULL':
D = Delay billing (cash method)
E = Elections (installment, deferral plans)
F = Future rate expectations
E = Earnings timing (accrual)
R = Restrictions (constructive receipt limits)

PULL = Pull income forward when rates rising or losses expiring

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Constructive receipt catches many candidates! A taxpayer cannot 'turn their back' on available income. The key is whether there are SUBSTANTIAL LIMITATIONS on access—not just inconvenience. Check is in the mail = constructively received when delivered, not when deposited.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Uncle Sam's Interest-Free Loan (Sort Of)
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Income timing isn't about finding loopholes; it's about playing the game smart. Think of deferring income as getting an interest-free loan from the government. You get to hold onto that cash longer, invest it, or just plain use it! But remember, it's not free money forever. The bill comes due eventually. The trick is figuring out when you'll be in a lower tax bracket. It's like a financial chess game, and you're always thinking a few moves ahead. Don't get greedy, just get strategic.

sections.title: Constructive Receipt Confusion!
sections.content: Students often think that simply not *wanting* income means they haven't constructively received it. This is a HUGE mistake! The key is *availability*. If the money is credited to your account, or a check is ready for you to pick up, you can't just ignore it and say, 'Nah, I'll take it next year.' Constructive receipt means you *could* have had the income. So, delaying cashing a check already in your possession doesn't defer income recognition. It's about control and accessibility, not just preference.

sections.title: Sarah's Bonus Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a high-performing consultant, is offered a $50,000 bonus in December. She knows her income will significantly decrease next year due to starting a family. If she takes the bonus in December, it's taxed at her higher current rate. However, if she can legitimately defer the bonus payment until January (with documentation showing it wasn't available to her in December), she'll pay significantly less in taxes due to the lower bracket next year. Deferral can be a powerful tool, but it must be done properly!

sections.title: Year-End Planning: Income Timing Everyday
sections.content: In practice, you'll use income timing strategies most often during year-end tax planning. Think about clients who are small business owners or independent contractors. They have more control over when they invoice clients or accept payments. You'll need to analyze their current and projected income, estimate tax brackets, and advise on whether to accelerate or defer income. Mastering constructive receipt is crucial here, as it often dictates the limits of what's possible. Document everything carefully!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Income timing exploits time value of money and rate differentials
• Cash method offers more flexibility; accrual follows all-events test
• Constructive receipt limits deferral—can't ignore available income
• §409A governs deferred compensation—severe penalties for violations
• Sometimes ACCELERATING income is optimal (expiring losses, rate increases)
• Always consider both current and future tax rates in timing decisions
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would LEAST likely support the deferral of income from Year 1 to Year 2 for tax planning purposes?
options: The taxpayer anticipates being in a lower marginal tax bracket in Year 2.
The taxpayer believes that tax rates will decrease significantly in Year 2.
The taxpayer has significant investment opportunities in Year 1 that require immediate capital.
The taxpayer anticipates being in a higher marginal tax bracket in Year 2.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Deferring income is generally not advantageous if the taxpayer anticipates being in a higher marginal tax bracket in the future. In that case, accelerating income into the current year would be more beneficial to take advantage of the lower rate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of progressive tax rates on the decision to defer income.

reveal.question: Define the 'Constructive Receipt' doctrine and explain its significance in income tax.
reveal.answer: The Constructive Receipt doctrine states that income is taxable when it is credited to your account, set apart for you, or otherwise made available so that you may draw upon it at any time, even if you have not physically received it. It's significant because taxpayers cannot intentionally postpone recognizing income by delaying its actual receipt if they have access to it.

rows.label: Tax Rate Expectation
values: Tax rates expected to increase in the future
Tax rates expected to decrease in the future

rows.label: Primary Benefit
values: Pay taxes at a lower current rate
Delay tax payment and potentially shift income to a lower tax bracket

rows.label: Time Value of Money Impact
values: Reduces future tax burden (if rates increase as expected)
Allows for current investment/use of funds that would otherwise be paid in taxes

rows.label: Risk
values: Tax rate expectations are incorrect
Tax rate expectations are incorrect

title: Income Shifting Strategies
description: Implement tax-efficient income shifting among family members and entities

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Income shifting can dramatically reduce family tax burdens by moving income to lower-bracket taxpayers. However, the IRS has developed extensive rules to prevent abuse. Understanding what works—and what doesn't—is essential for effective tax planning.

sections.title: Assignment of Income Doctrine
sections.content: The fruit-and-tree doctrine (Lucas v. Earl) holds that income is taxed to the person who earns it. You can't assign earned income to another taxpayer. However, you CAN transfer income-producing property, shifting future income to the property's new owner.

sections.title: Valid vs Invalid Income Shifting
rows: If reasonable compensation for real work
If valid business purpose, real services/capital

sections.title: Kiddie Tax Rules (§1(g))
sections.content: The kiddie tax limits income shifting to children by taxing their unearned income above a threshold at the parent's marginal rate. Applies to children under 19 (or under 24 if full-time student) who don't provide more than half their own support.

sections.title: Employing Family Members
items: Children under 18 in parent's sole proprietorship: No FICA
Must pay REASONABLE compensation for ACTUAL services
Document duties, hours, and comparable wages
Child's standard deduction shelters up to $14,600 (2024)
Child can contribute to Roth IRA with earned income
Shifts income from high-bracket parent to zero/low-bracket child

sections.title: Example: Employing a Child
sections.content:
Dr. Smith (35% bracket) employs her 16-year-old son in her medical practice for legitimate office work.

Son's compensation: $14,000/year (reasonable for duties)
Son's tax: $0 (within standard deduction)
Son's FICA: $0 (under 18, parent's sole prop)

Tax savings to Dr. Smith:
• Income tax: $14,000 × 35% = $4,900
• Self-employment tax: $14,000 × 15.3% = $2,142
• Total family tax savings: $7,042

Bonus: Son can contribute $7,000 to Roth IRA, building tax-free retirement savings!

sections.title: Family Partnerships
sections.content: Family members can be partners if they contribute capital or services. Special rules (§704(e)) require that capital be a material income-producing factor and allocations reflect reasonable compensation for services. IRS scrutinizes allocations disproportionate to contributions.

sections.title: Family Partnership Rules
items: Capital must be material income-producing factor
Donor must be adequately compensated for services first
Child's share limited to capital contribution portion
Allocations must have economic substance
Gift of partnership interest creates valid partner

sections.title: Trusts for Income Shifting
rows: Limited shift due to kiddie tax

sections.title: Shifting Investment Income
items: Gift appreciated stock: Done transfers to lower-bracket family member
Series I/EE Bonds: Register in child's name; defer/shift interest
Municipal bonds: Tax-exempt regardless of bracket
Consider kiddie tax before gifting to minors
Adult children (24+) not subject to kiddie tax

sections.title: S Corporation Salary/Distribution Planning
sections.content: S corp shareholders who work in the business must receive reasonable compensation (subject to FICA). Remaining profits pass through as distributions (no FICA). Strategy: Minimize salary to reasonable level, maximize distributions. But IRS challenges unreasonably low salaries!

sections.title: Memory Aid: Income Shifting
sections.content:
'SHIFT the TREE, not the FRUIT':
• Can't assign earned income (fruit)
• CAN transfer income-producing property (tree)
• Employ family for real work at reasonable pay
• Kiddie tax limits shifting to minors
• Trusts and partnerships must have substance

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The kiddie tax applies through age 23 for full-time students who don't provide over half their support! Many candidates think it ends at 18. Also, employment of children under 18 is only FICA-exempt in a sole proprietorship or spousal partnership—NOT in corporations or partnerships with non-parent partners.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Income Shifting: More Than Just a Theory!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Income shifting sounds fancy, but it's all about legally minimizing taxes for your family. Think of it like this: Uncle Sam wants his cut, but you're the quarterback calling the plays. The fruit-and-tree doctrine? That's the ref making sure you don't throw the ball to your dog. Transferring income-producing property is a legit play, but assigning your salary to your kids? Flag on the play! Understand the rules, and you can legally save a ton.

sections.title: Kiddie Tax Confusion: Unearned vs. Earned Income
sections.content: A common blunder is assuming the kiddie tax applies to ALL of a child's income. Wrong! It only applies to *unearned* income exceeding a certain threshold (around $2,500 in recent years). Earned income, like wages from a summer job, is taxed at the child's rate. Remembering this distinction is crucial. Don't accidentally overpay taxes by applying the parent's rate to income that's actually taxed at the child's lower rate. Read the instructions carefully!

sections.title: The Rental Property Shuffle
sections.content: Sarah wants to help her daughter, a college student, with expenses. Sarah owns a rental property generating $10,000 in annual net income. If Sarah gifts the property to her daughter, the $10,000 in income will now be taxed at the daughter's (presumably lower) tax bracket, potentially saving the family significant tax dollars. Remember to consider gift tax implications and the daughter's ability to manage the property.

sections.title: Client Meetings: Income Shifting Opportunities
sections.content: In practice, you'll often identify income shifting opportunities during annual tax planning meetings with clients. For example, clients with substantial investment portfolios and minor children are prime candidates. You'll need to analyze their current income, tax brackets, and assets to determine the best strategies, like gifting appreciated stock to a child's custodial account. Document your recommendations and be sure to clearly explain the potential tax benefits and associated risks.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Assignment of income doctrine: Can't assign earned income; CAN gift property
• Kiddie tax applies to unearned income of children under 19 (24 if student)
• Employing children: Real work, reasonable pay, no FICA if under 18 in sole prop
• Family partnerships require capital as material factor, economic substance
• Trusts can shift income but grantor trust rules may apply
• S corp: Balance salary (FICA) vs distributions (no FICA)—IRS watches!
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John, age 16 and a full-time student, receives $3,000 in unearned income from a trust established by his grandparents. John also earns $2,000 from a part-time job. His parents' marginal tax rate is 32%. Assuming the kiddie tax threshold for unearned income is $2,500, how will John's unearned income be taxed?
options: $500 will be taxed at John's tax rate, and $2,500 will be taxed at his parents' rate.
$3,000 will be taxed at John's tax rate.
$3,000 will be taxed at his parents' tax rate.
$500 will be taxed at his parents' tax rate, and $2,500 will be taxed at John's rate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The kiddie tax applies to unearned income exceeding the threshold ($2,500). In this case, $3,000 - $2,500 = $500 is taxed at the parent's rate (32%), and the first $2,500 is taxed at the child's rate. This is designed to prevent income shifting to lower-bracket children.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the kiddie tax threshold and which income is subject to the parent's rate.

reveal.question: Explain the 'fruit-and-tree' metaphor in the context of the assignment of income doctrine.
reveal.answer: The 'fruit-and-tree' metaphor illustrates that income (the fruit) is taxed to the individual who owns the asset that generates the income (the tree). You can transfer the tree (the income-producing property) to shift future income, but you cannot simply assign the fruit (the income) without transferring the tree. The income will still be taxed to the original owner of the tree.

rows.label: Kiddie Tax Impact
values: Not directly affected.
Unearned income above a certain threshold is taxed at the parent's marginal tax rate for children meeting specific age and support requirements.

title: 🆕 H.R. 1: New Income Exclusions
description: Navigate the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act income exclusion changes and planning opportunities

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA/H.R. 1) significantly changed income exclusions. Many provisions sunset after 2025, creating critical planning windows. Understanding these changes helps clients maximize benefits while provisions remain available.

sections.title: TCJA Overview
sections.content: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, enacted December 2017, made sweeping changes to individual and business taxation. Many individual provisions expire after December 31, 2025, creating a 'sunset cliff.' Understanding which exclusions changed—and which remain permanent—is essential.

sections.title: Moving Expense Exclusion Suspension
sections.content: Before TCJA, employer-paid moving expenses were excluded from income, and employees could deduct unreimbursed moving expenses. TCJA suspended BOTH the exclusion and the deduction for 2018-2025. Exception: Active duty military moving pursuant to military orders retain both benefits.

sections.title: Qualified Fringe Benefits Retained
items: Health insurance premiums (employer-paid): Still excluded
HSA contributions by employer: Still excluded
Dependent care assistance: Still excluded up to $5,000
Educational assistance: Still excluded up to $5,250
Qualified transportation: Parking $315/month, transit $315/month (2024)
De minimis fringe benefits: Still excluded

sections.title: Alimony Planning Implications
sections.content: For divorces finalized after 2018, alimony is tax-neutral—no deduction for payor, no income for recipient. This shifts tax planning to property division. Pre-2019 divorces retain old treatment unless modified with election. Consider timing of divorce finalization for tax efficiency.

sections.title: Qualified Opportunity Zone Exclusions
items: New exclusion created by TCJA (§1400Z-2)
Invest capital gains in Qualified Opportunity Fund within 180 days
Defer original gain until 2026 (or earlier sale)
10% basis increase if held 5 years (by 12/31/2026)
15% basis increase if held 7 years (deadline passed)
Permanent exclusion of NEW gains if held 10+ years

sections.title: Section 529 Expansion
sections.content: TCJA expanded 529 plan distributions to include K-12 tuition (up to $10,000/year per beneficiary). Previously limited to higher education. Permanent change. State tax treatment varies—some states don't conform.

sections.title: Planning Before Sunset
items: Moving: If relocating for work, consider timing around 2026
Divorce: Property division more important post-2018
QOZ: Must invest by deadlines to get basis step-up
Monitor legislation: Provisions may be extended or modified
State conformity: Check state treatment of federal changes

sections.title: Memory Aid: TCJA Exclusion Changes
sections.content:
'TCJA: MOVE-BIKE-ALIMONY-QOZ':
• MOVing expenses suspended (except military)
• BIKE benefit suspended
• ALIMONY no longer income/deduction (post-2018)
• QOZ new exclusion for opportunity zone gains
Most return in 2026—sunset cliff!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Alimony rules depend on DIVORCE DATE, not payment date! A divorce finalized December 15, 2018, uses OLD rules forever (income to recipient, deduction to payor). Divorce finalized January 2, 2019, uses NEW rules (no income, no deduction). The cutoff is when the decree was entered.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: TCJA Sunset Cliff: Don't Get Clipped!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk TCJA. This whole 'sunset' thing is basically Congress saying, 'Hey, we'll give you tax breaks...for now!' After 2025, a bunch of stuff reverts back. So, when you're prepping taxes, especially for individuals, keep an eye on those expiration dates. I've seen seasoned pros forget and apply old rules, leading to amended returns and unhappy clients. It's like a tax time bomb waiting to go off if you're not careful. Double-check everything!

sections.title: Moving Expenses: Still Think It's Deductible?
sections.content: A common mistake students make is assuming moving expenses are still deductible for regular employees. TCJA suspended this deduction from 2018-2025. Students often remember the 'moving expenses' keyword but forget the suspension, leading them to incorrectly reduce taxable income. Remember, it's GONE for most folks until further notice! Focus on the active-duty military exception – that’s the only one remaining in the current tax environment.

sections.title: Sergeant Miller's PCS: A Taxing Move
sections.content: Sergeant Miller receives Permanent Change of Station (PCS) orders and incurs $8,000 in moving expenses. His employer reimburses him $6,000. The learning point here is that the reimbursement is excluded from Sergeant Miller's income, and he can exclude the full $8,000 worth of expenses, even if the employer only reimbursed $6,000. This is because active-duty military are exempt from the TCJA's moving expense changes. This is a common scenario in military tax practices.

sections.title: Client Intake: Uncover Moving Expense Details
sections.content: In practice, during client intake, always ask specifically about moving expenses. Don't just assume they know the rules. Dig deeper to determine if they're active-duty military. If they are, document their orders and reimbursements carefully. This one question can significantly impact their tax liability, and a mistake here can lead to penalties and interest. Being thorough upfront saves time and headaches later in the tax preparation process.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Moving expense exclusion suspended 2018-2025 (except military)
• Bicycle commuting benefit suspended 2018-2025
• Alimony: Post-2018 divorces = no income/no deduction (permanent)
• QOZ provides deferral + potential permanent exclusion of new gains
• 529 expanded to K-12 tuition ($10k/year) permanently
• Many provisions sunset after 2025—monitor legislation
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During 2024, which of the following taxpayers can exclude employer-reimbursed moving expenses from their gross income?
options: A single taxpayer who moved to accept a new job offer in a different state.
A married couple who both work and moved to be closer to their aging parents.
An active duty member of the U.S. Army who moved pursuant to permanent change of station orders.
A self-employed individual who moved their business to a new location.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The TCJA suspended the moving expense exclusion for most taxpayers from 2018-2025. The exception is for active duty military personnel moving pursuant to military orders. Therefore, only the active duty member can exclude employer-reimbursed moving expenses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the specific exception to the moving expense exclusion suspension.

reveal.question: When do many of the individual tax provisions of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) expire, creating what is known as the 'sunset cliff'?
reveal.answer: Many individual tax provisions of the TCJA expire after December 31, 2025.

sections.title: Moving Expenses: Before TCJA vs. During Suspension

rows.label: Employer-Paid Moving Expenses
values: Excludable from employee's income
Generally taxable income to the employee (exception for active-duty military)

rows.label: Employee Unreimbursed Moving Expenses
values: Deductible by the employee (subject to certain requirements)
Not deductible

title: Deduction Timing & Bunching
description: Optimize deduction timing through strategic bunching and year-end planning

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: With the near-doubling of the standard deduction under TCJA, many taxpayers now alternate between itemizing and taking the standard deduction. Strategic 'bunching' of deductions can maximize tax benefits by concentrating deductible expenses into years when itemizing is advantageous.

sections.title: The Bunching Strategy
sections.content: Bunching involves timing discretionary deductible expenses to maximize itemized deductions in certain years while taking the standard deduction in others. Instead of spreading deductions evenly (potentially losing benefit), concentrate them to exceed the standard deduction threshold.

sections.title: Charitable Contribution Bunching
items: Donor-Advised Fund (DAF): Contribute large amount in one year, distribute over time
Bunch 2-3 years of donations into one year
Alternate: Itemize Year 1 (large donation), Standard Year 2
Appreciated stock: Avoid capital gains, get FMV deduction
Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD): Age 70½+, directly from IRA, up to $105k

sections.title: Example: Charitable Bunching
sections.content:
Tom and Mary (MFJ) typically donate $15,000/year. Their other itemized deductions total $20,000 (below $29,200 standard).

Without bunching (per year):
• Itemized: $35,000; Benefit over standard: $5,800
• Total 2-year benefit: $11,600

With bunching (every other year):
• Year 1: $30,000 donations + $20,000 other = $50,000 itemized
  Benefit: $50,000 - $29,200 = $20,800
• Year 2: $0 donations, take $29,200 standard
• Total 2-year benefit: $20,800

Bunching advantage: $9,200 additional deductions!

sections.title: Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs)
sections.content: DAFs allow immediate deduction for contribution, with grants to charities made over time. Perfect for bunching: Make large DAF contribution in high-income year, recommend grants to favorite charities over following years. Contribution is irrevocable once made.

sections.title: Medical Expense Timing
items: Deductible only to extent exceeding 7.5% of AGI
Bunch elective procedures (dental, vision, etc.) into one year
If close to threshold, accelerate December expenses
If under threshold, defer to following year
Consider FSA/HSA for expenses that won't meet threshold

sections.title: SALT Limitation Impact
sections.content: The $10,000 SALT cap ($5,000 MFS) limits bunching for state/local taxes. If SALT already exceeds cap, accelerating property tax payment provides no benefit. Bunching SALT only works if normally under the cap. Note: SALT cap sunsets after 2025.

sections.title: Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD)
sections.content: For taxpayers 70½+, QCDs from IRAs are excluded from income entirely (up to $105,000 in 2024). This beats itemizing because: (1) No AGI increase, (2) Satisfies RMD, (3) Benefits even standard deduction takers. QCDs are better than bunching for eligible taxpayers!

sections.title: Interaction with AMT
items: Bunching may trigger AMT in high-deduction years
SALT deduction disallowed for AMT
Medical threshold was 10% for AMT (now both 7.5%)
Calculate AMT impact before large bunching
Charitable contributions allowed for both regular and AMT

sections.title: Memory Aid: Bunching Candidates
sections.content:
'BUNCH when you CONTROL timing':
B = Big charitable gifts (DAF ideal)
U = Unreimbursed medical (elective procedures)
N = kNow your threshold (standard deduction amount)
C = Calculate 2-year impact
H = Hold/accelerate based on comparison

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Property tax prepayment is LIMITED! Under TCJA, you cannot deduct prepaid property taxes for a future year if assessment hasn't been made. And even if you can prepay, the $10,000 SALT cap limits the benefit. Don't advise prepaying property taxes without checking both limitations.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Bunching: It's Like Tax Tetris!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, tax law can feel like a giant game of Tetris. Bunching deductions? That's just strategically fitting those expense blocks to get the highest score (aka, the lowest tax bill!). I've seen clients leave money on the table because they didn't plan ahead. Think about it: if your itemized deductions are usually around $8,000, and the standard deduction is $13,850 (single, 2023), you're basically wasting those deductions. Bunching lets you concentrate expenses to actually *exceed* that threshold in certain years. Don't just let those deductions dribble away! Play smart.

sections.title: Forgetting State & Local Tax (SALT) Cap Impact
sections.content: A common mistake is ignoring the SALT deduction limit ($10,000). Students sometimes think 'I'll just bunch ALL my property taxes and state income tax withholding!', but if those *already* exceed $10,000, bunching more doesn't help for federal purposes! You're still capped. The key is to consider the *total* itemized deductions, including SALT, and determine if additional bunching (medical expenses, charitable contributions) will actually push you over the standard deduction *after* accounting for the SALT limitation. Don't spin your wheels bunching something that won't lower your tax liability.

sections.title: Dr. Emily's Donation Dilemma
sections.content: Dr. Emily consistently donates $5,000 to her alma mater each year. Her other itemized deductions hover around $9,000 annually. By strategically donating $10,000 in alternating years, she can itemize in those years (totaling $19,000, exceeding the standard deduction) and take the standard deduction in the other years. This simple shift maximizes her tax savings over time, compared to consistently itemizing below the standard deduction threshold every year. The key is planning!

sections.title: Year-End Tax Projections: Bunching's Playground
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter bunching during year-end tax planning meetings with clients. This is where you analyze their income and potential deductions to project their tax liability. Clients often have discretionary expenses, like large charitable donations or elective medical procedures. By discussing the potential tax benefits of accelerating or deferring these expenses, you can help them optimize their tax situation. This proactive approach demonstrates your value as a tax professional and strengthens client relationships. Mastering bunching is a crucial tool for providing effective tax advice.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Bunching concentrates deductions to exceed standard deduction threshold
• Donor-Advised Funds enable large one-time deduction with distributions over time
• Charitable contributions offer highest bunching flexibility
• Medical expense bunching works when near 7.5% AGI threshold
• SALT cap limits property/state tax bunching benefit
• QCDs (age 70½+) may beat bunching for charitable giving
• Always calculate 2-year tax impact before bunching
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST illustrates the effective use of a bunching strategy for itemized deductions?
options: A taxpayer consistently claims itemized deductions slightly above the standard deduction each year.
A taxpayer alternates between claiming the standard deduction and itemized deductions, exceeding the standard deduction amount in one year and claiming the standard deduction the next.
A taxpayer always takes the standard deduction regardless of potential itemized deductions.
A taxpayer evenly distributes deductible expenses across multiple years to minimize fluctuations in taxable income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The bunching strategy aims to concentrate deductible expenses in specific years to exceed the standard deduction, allowing for itemization in those years. Alternating between itemizing and taking the standard deduction maximizes tax benefits compared to spreading deductions evenly or consistently taking the standard deduction.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the goal of the bunching strategy: exceeding the standard deduction in some years.

reveal.question: Define the 'bunching' strategy in the context of tax planning for itemized deductions.
reveal.answer: Bunching is a tax planning strategy where taxpayers strategically time discretionary deductible expenses to concentrate them in a specific year. The goal is to exceed the standard deduction amount in that year, allowing them to itemize deductions and potentially reduce their taxable income. In other years, they would take the standard deduction.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah anticipates having $7,000 in itemized deductions in 2024 and projects a similar amount for 2025. The standard deduction for her filing status is $14,600. To implement a bunching strategy, what is the MOST appropriate course of action?
options: Accelerate deductible expenses into 2024 to exceed $14,600, taking the standard deduction in 2025.
Defer deductible expenses from 2024 to 2025 to exceed $14,600, taking the standard deduction in 2024.
Distribute deductible expenses evenly between 2024 and 2025.
It's impossible to use the bunching strategy in this scenario.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To use the bunching strategy, Sarah should try to accelerate deductible expenses into one year (e.g., 2024) so that her total itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction ($14,600). Then, she would take the standard deduction in 2025. Options B and C are incorrect because they don't maximize the potential tax benefit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how you can concentrate deductions to exceed the standard deduction.

title: 🆕 H.R. 1: Updated Deduction Limits
description: Master the TCJA deduction limit changes and planning strategies before sunset

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: TCJA dramatically altered itemized deductions—eliminating some, capping others, and expanding the standard deduction. With most provisions sunsetting after 2025, understanding current rules and planning for potential changes is critical.

sections.title: SALT Cap Deep Dive
sections.content: The $10,000 SALT cap covers state/local income taxes OR sales taxes (election), PLUS property taxes combined. High-tax state residents are significantly impacted. The cap applies regardless of filing status (except $5,000 for MFS). No inflation adjustment.

sections.title: Pass-Through Entity Tax (PTE) Workaround
sections.content: Many states now allow PTEs (S corps, partnerships) to pay state income tax at the entity level. This tax is deductible as a business expense, bypassing the individual SALT cap. Owners receive credit or exclusion for their share. Available in 30+ states.

sections.title: Mortgage Interest Changes
items: New limit: $750,000 acquisition indebtedness (down from $1M)
Grandfathered: Mortgages originated before 12/15/2017 retain $1M limit
Refinancing: Grandfathered debt retains old limit if principal doesn't increase
Home equity: Interest only deductible if used to buy, build, or improve home
Second homes: Included in limit (combined with primary)

sections.title: Example: Mortgage Interest Limitation
sections.content:
John purchased a home in 2020 with an $850,000 mortgage.

Mortgage interest paid: $42,000
Deductible portion: $750,000 / $850,000 = 88.2%
Deductible interest: $42,000 × 88.2% = $37,044
Non-deductible: $4,956

If John had purchased before 12/15/2017 with same mortgage, 100% would be deductible under the $1M grandfathered limit.

sections.title: Suspended Deductions
items: Miscellaneous itemized deductions (2% floor): Investment expenses, tax prep, unreimbursed employee expenses—ALL GONE
Moving expenses: Suspended (except active military)
Hobby losses: Cannot offset hobby income (was limited to hobby income)
Casualty/theft losses: Only federally declared disasters
Alimony deduction: Post-2018 divorces (permanent change)

sections.title: Planning Before 2026 Sunset
items: SALT: Use PTE election if available in your state
Mortgage: Grandfathered debt retains $1M limit—don't refinance above
Maximize bunching while standard deduction is high
Accelerate charitable contributions before potential limit reduction
Monitor legislation for extension or modification
Personal exemptions return in 2026—may help large families

sections.title: Memory Aid: TCJA Deduction Changes
sections.content:
'TCJA LIMITS': Think 'STAMP':
S = SALT capped at $10k
T = Ten thousand dollar SALT limit
A = Acquisition debt reduced to $750k
M = Miscellaneous itemized GONE
P = Personal exemptions GONE

All sunset 2026 (except alimony change = permanent)

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The mortgage interest limit depends on ORIGINATION DATE! Pre-12/15/2017 mortgages use $1M limit; later mortgages use $750K. Refinancing grandfathered debt retains old limit ONLY if you don't increase principal. Know both thresholds and the date!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: SALT Cap: The Taxpayer's Nemesis!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. The $10,000 SALT cap? It's a pain. I've had clients in New Jersey practically weeping over this. Remember, it's combined - property taxes, state income OR sales tax (your choice!). And yeah, $10,000 sounds like a lot until you own a house in the suburbs. When discussing SALT planning, always ask about property taxes FIRST. That usually eats up most of the cap, leaving little room for state income tax strategies. Don't forget that married filing separately only gets $5,000. Brutal!

sections.title: SALT vs. Business Expense Deduction Confusion
sections.content: Students often forget that the $10,000 SALT cap only applies to itemized deductions on Schedule A. They incorrectly assume that the cap also restricts business expense deductions. For example, if a sole proprietor pays $15,000 in state income tax *through* their business (as a PTE workaround), only $10,000 is limited on Schedule A (if the taxpayer itemizes). The business deduction is separate and not subject to the individual SALT cap. Double-check the context: is the tax being paid personally or through a business entity?

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Shuffle
sections.content: Sarah owns an S Corp in California and faces a hefty state income tax bill. Her CPA recommends the PTE election. By paying the state income tax at the S Corp level, the $20,000 state tax payment becomes a deductible business expense, completely bypassing her personal SALT cap. Sarah then receives a credit on her personal return for her share of the S Corp's state tax payment, effectively eliminating her individual state income tax liability. The PTE workaround saved Sarah thousands!

sections.title: SALT Cap: Real-World Client Scenarios
sections.content: In practice, you'll constantly be evaluating the SALT cap's impact on clients, especially those in high-tax states. You'll need to analyze whether itemizing deductions is even beneficial, given the cap and the increased standard deduction. Moreover, understanding the PTE election is crucial for S Corp and partnership clients. It is imperative to model different scenarios (standard deduction vs. itemizing, PTE election vs. not) to determine the most tax-efficient strategy for each client. This analysis is a core component of tax planning services.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• SALT capped at $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)—PTE election may bypass
• Mortgage interest: $750K limit for post-12/14/2017 loans; $1M grandfathered
• Miscellaneous itemized deductions (2% floor) suspended
• Personal exemptions suspended (offset by higher standard deduction)
• Charitable AGI limit increased from 50% to 60% for cash
• Most provisions sunset after 2025—significant planning implications
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Mr. and Mrs. Smith, filing jointly, reside in a high-tax state. Their state income tax is $8,000, their property taxes are $4,000, and their state sales tax is $6,000. Assuming they elect to deduct state income tax instead of sales tax, what is the amount of SALT deduction they can claim on Schedule A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SALT deduction is capped at $10,000. Their state income tax ($8,000) plus property taxes ($4,000) equals $12,000. However, due to the cap, they can only deduct $10,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the $10,000 cap applies to the *total* of state and local taxes.

reveal.question: Explain the purpose of the Pass-Through Entity (PTE) tax workaround.
reveal.answer: The PTE workaround allows pass-through entities (like S corps and partnerships) to pay state income tax at the entity level. This entity-level tax is deductible as a business expense, bypassing the individual SALT cap. The owners then receive a credit or exclusion for their share of the tax paid by the entity, effectively allowing a full state income tax deduction that would otherwise be limited at the individual level.

knowledgeCheck.question: What is the SALT deduction limit for taxpayers who are married filing separately?
options: No Limit
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SALT deduction limit for taxpayers who are married filing separately is $5,000. This is half of the $10,000 limit for other filing statuses.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how the overall SALT limit is affected by filing status.

title: Capital Gains Strategies
description: Optimize capital gains recognition through timing, harvesting, and rate management

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Capital gains planning offers significant tax savings through preferential rates, loss harvesting, and strategic timing. Understanding the interplay between holding periods, rate brackets, and the 3.8% NIIT helps maximize after-tax returns for investment clients.

sections.title: Capital Gains Rate Planning
sections.content: The 0% LTCG bracket is a powerful planning tool. Taxpayers in lower brackets can recognize gains tax-free up to the threshold. Strategy: 'Fill up' the 0% bracket each year with gain recognition, effectively stepping up basis for free.

sections.title: Gain Harvesting Strategy
sections.content:
Sarah, single, has taxable income of $35,000 before capital gains. She has stock with $50,000 unrealized LTCG.

Room in 0% bracket: $47,025 - $35,000 = $12,025

Strategy: Sell shares to recognize $12,025 gain
• Tax on gain: $0 (within 0% bracket)
• Repurchase immediately (no wash sale for gains)
• New basis: FMV at repurchase
• Result: Stepped-up basis with zero tax!

Repeat annually to systematically harvest gains at 0%.

sections.title: Tax Loss Harvesting
items: Sell investments at a loss to offset gains
Net STCL first offsets STCG (saves up to 37%)
Excess offsets LTCG (saves 15-23.8%)
$3,000 annual deduction against ordinary income
Unlimited carryforward of excess losses
WATCH: Wash sale rule (30-day window)

sections.title: Wash Sale Rule
sections.content: Loss is disallowed if you purchase substantially identical securities within 30 days before or after the sale. Disallowed loss is added to basis of replacement shares. Rule applies across all accounts (IRA, spouse, etc.). Does NOT apply to gains—you can harvest gains and immediately repurchase.

sections.title: Avoiding Wash Sales
rows: Buy now, sell original after 31 days
Sell in taxable, buy in IRA

sections.title: Specific Identification
sections.content: When selling partial positions, specific identification allows you to choose WHICH shares to sell. This lets you control gain/loss recognition. Must identify shares before sale and receive confirmation. Default method is FIFO (first in, first out).

sections.title: Charitable Giving of Appreciated Stock
items: Donate LTCG stock: Deduction = FMV, no capital gains tax
Better than selling and donating cash (avoids embedded gain)
Must hold >1 year for FMV deduction
30% AGI limit for appreciated property to public charities
Donate highest-gain shares for maximum benefit

sections.title: Example: Stock Donation vs Cash
sections.content:
Mark (24% bracket, 15% LTCG) wants to donate $10,000. He owns stock worth $10,000 with $6,000 basis.

Option 1: Sell stock, donate cash
• Capital gain: $4,000 × 15% = $600 tax
• Charitable deduction: $10,000 × 24% = $2,400 savings
• Net benefit: $2,400 - $600 = $1,800

Option 2: Donate stock directly
• No capital gain recognized
• Charitable deduction: $10,000 × 24% = $2,400 savings
• Net benefit: $2,400

Donating stock saves additional $600!

sections.title: Step-Up in Basis at Death
sections.content: Assets receive stepped-up basis to FMV at death (§1014). Strategy: Hold highly appreciated assets until death rather than selling. Heirs inherit with FMV basis, eliminating all embedded gain. But: Don't let tax tail wag the investment dog!

sections.title: Installment Sales
items: Spread gain recognition over payment period
Each payment = return of basis + interest + gain portion
Defers tax and may spread across lower brackets
Not available for publicly traded securities
Depreciation recapture recognized in year of sale

sections.title: Memory Aid: Capital Gains Planning
sections.content:
'HARVEST both ways':
• Harvest LOSSES to offset gains (mind wash sale!)
• Harvest GAINS at 0% bracket (step up basis free!)
• GIVE appreciated stock (skip the gain, get deduction)
• HOLD until death (step-up erases gain)
• IDENTIFY specific shares (control recognition)

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Wash sale applies across ALL accounts including IRAs! Selling at a loss in a brokerage account and buying the same stock in your IRA within 30 days disallows the loss AND you don't get the basis added to IRA shares. This is worse than a regular wash sale!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The 0% LTCG Bracket: Your Secret Weapon
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk turkey. The 0% long-term capital gains bracket? It's basically the tax code giving you free money. Seriously. I've seen folks leave thousands on the table because they didn't understand how to use it. Don't be that person! Think of it like this: you're getting a free basis step-up. Recognize those gains while you're in the 0% bracket, and you're essentially resetting the clock for future appreciation. It's a no-brainer...if you know about it. Now you do. Go forth and conquer!

sections.title: Forgetting State Taxes on Capital Gains
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing solely on the federal 0% long-term capital gains bracket and forgetting about state income taxes. While federally you might be in the 0% bracket, your state may still tax those gains. This can significantly impact the overall benefit of harvesting gains. Always consider the combined federal and state tax implications when planning capital gains strategies. A quick look at your client's state tax rates can save you (and them) a nasty surprise come tax season.

sections.title: The Case of Mrs. Henderson's Stock Portfolio
sections.content: Mrs. Henderson, a recent widow with a taxable income of $45,000, owns stock with a low basis and substantial unrealized gains. Her CPA, noticing she's well within the 0% LTCG bracket, advises her to sell a portion of the stock to recognize gains up to the bracket limit. This allows her to diversify her portfolio without incurring federal tax, providing increased financial flexibility and a stepped-up basis for the remaining shares.

sections.title: Client Meetings: The 'Harvesting' Conversation
sections.content: In practice, you'll often encounter clients who are hesitant to sell assets, even if it's tax-advantageous. This is where your communication skills come in. Frame capital gains harvesting as a proactive strategy to minimize future tax liabilities and potentially improve their investment portfolio. Use clear examples and demonstrate the potential tax savings in dollars and cents. Show them how 'filling up' the 0% bracket now can prevent a larger tax bill later. This approach builds trust and reinforces your value as a tax advisor.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• LTCG rates: 0%, 15%, 20% (+3.8% NIIT if applicable)
• Harvest gains at 0% to step up basis tax-free
• Harvest losses to offset gains ($3,000 vs ordinary income)
• Wash sale: 30-day rule for losses, applies across all accounts
• Donate appreciated stock: FMV deduction, no gain recognized
• Specific identification controls which shares are sold
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is a single taxpayer with taxable income of $35,000 in 2024. She also has a long-term capital gain of $15,000 from the sale of stock. Assuming the 2024 single filer 0% LTCG threshold is $47,025, what amount of her long-term capital gain will be taxed at 0%?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah's taxable income is $35,000. The 0% LTCG bracket extends up to $47,025. The amount of the bracket remaining is $47,025 - $35,000 = $12,025. Since her LTCG is $15,000, only $12,025 could be sheltered. However, the question asks what amount of her $15,000 LTCG is taxed at 0%. The correct answer is $15,000 because her income combined with the LTCG is still below the $47,025 threshold ($35,000+$15,000=$50,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider the taxpayer's existing taxable income before applying the capital gains.

reveal.question: Describe the tax planning strategy of 'filling up' the 0% long-term capital gains bracket.
reveal.answer: The strategy involves intentionally recognizing long-term capital gains in years when the taxpayer's income is low enough to fall within the 0% LTCG bracket. By realizing gains up to the threshold, the taxpayer effectively steps up the basis of the asset without paying any capital gains tax. This can be particularly beneficial for assets expected to appreciate further.

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a single taxpayer, has $30,000 in taxable income. He owns stock with a basis of $5,000 and a fair market value of $15,000. Knowing the 0% LTCG rate applies up to $47,025 for single filers, he sells the stock. Later, he re-purchases the same stock. What is John's new basis in the stock after the sale and repurchase?
options: Cannot be determined without more information.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John recognized a $10,000 gain ($15,000 - $5,000). His total income is now $40,000 ($30,000 + $10,000), still below the $47,025 threshold. Therefore, the gain is taxed at 0%. Since he re-purchased the stock, his new basis is the purchase price, which is the fair market value at the time of sale, $15,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the impact on the basis of the re-purchased asset.

title: Qualified Opportunity Zones
description: Leverage Qualified Opportunity Zone investments for capital gains deferral and exclusion

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Qualified Opportunity Zones (QOZs) offer powerful tax benefits: deferral of capital gains, basis step-up, and potential permanent exclusion of new appreciation. Created by TCJA, understanding QOZ rules helps clients maximize benefits before key deadlines pass.

sections.title: How QOZ Investment Works
sections.content: Taxpayer realizes capital gain from any source → Invests gain amount in Qualified Opportunity Fund (QOF) within 180 days → QOF invests in qualified opportunity zone property → Taxpayer defers original gain, may step up basis, and excludes appreciation if held 10+ years.

sections.title: 180-Day Investment Window
items: Clock starts on date gain would be recognized
For partnership/S corp gains: Start from K-1 year-end (unless elect earlier)
Section 1231 gains: 180 days from end of tax year (netting period)
Investment must be in a Qualified Opportunity Fund (QOF)
Only the GAIN amount needs to be invested (not gross proceeds)

sections.title: Qualified Opportunity Fund Requirements
items: Corporation or partnership organized for QOZ investment
Self-certifies on Form 8996 with tax return
90% of assets must be QOZ property (tested semi-annually)
Cannot exceed 5% non-qualified financial property
Can be self-created (taxpayer forms own QOF)

sections.title: Qualified Opportunity Zone Property
rows: Acquired after 12/31/2017, domestic corp, 70%+ tangible QOZ property
Acquired after 12/31/2017, domestic partnership, 70%+ tangible QOZ property
Tangible property, original use in QOZ or substantially improved

sections.title: Original Use vs Substantial Improvement
sections.content: Land cannot be original use (always previously existed). Buildings can be original use if never placed in service. For existing buildings, must substantially improve: additions to basis must equal or exceed original building basis within 30 months. Land is excluded from substantial improvement calculation.

sections.title: Example: QOZ Investment Timeline
sections.content:
June 1, 2024: Mike sells stock, realizes $500,000 LTCG
October 1, 2024: Invests $500,000 in QOF (within 180 days)

December 31, 2026: Must recognize deferred gain (or sale triggers earlier)
• If still holding: 10% basis step-up ($50,000) applies (held 5+ years by 12/31/2026)
• Taxable gain: $500,000 - $50,000 = $450,000

October 1, 2034: Sells QOF investment for $1,200,000 (held 10+ years)
• New appreciation: $1,200,000 - $500,000 = $700,000
• Tax on new appreciation: $0 (10-year exclusion)
• Total benefit: Deferred original gain + $50k basis step-up + $700k excluded

sections.title: Inclusion Events
items: Sale or exchange of QOF investment
December 31, 2026 (mandatory inclusion)
Gift of QOF interest (gain recognized, no exclusion)
Death does NOT trigger inclusion (passes to heirs)
Distributions: May be inclusion events depending on structure

sections.title: Practical Planning Considerations
items: Must invest within 180 days—identify QOF quickly
Can form your own QOF if investing in specific property
10-year hold required for maximum benefit (no new gain)
7-year deadline passed—only 10% step-up still available
Gains from ANY source qualify (stocks, real estate, crypto, etc.)
State tax treatment varies—some don't conform

sections.title: Memory Aid: QOZ Benefits
sections.content:
'5-7-10 Rule' (now just 5-10!):
• 5 years = 10% basis step-up on deferred gain
• 7 years = 15% basis step-up (deadline PASSED!)
• 10 years = NEW appreciation excluded permanently

'180 days to DEFER, 10 years to DISAPPEAR'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The 10-year exclusion only applies to APPRECIATION on the QOF investment, not the original deferred gain! The deferred gain must be recognized by December 31, 2026, regardless of holding period. The 10-year benefit is for NEW gains earned while in the QOZ.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: QOZ: The Government's Way of Saying 'Invest, Please'
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, Qualified Opportunity Zones sound like something out of a superhero movie. In reality, it's the government's attempt to incentivize investment in underserved areas. Think of it as a tax break for doing good (and maybe making some money along the way). The key? Nail the 180-day investment window. Miss that, and your capital gains are going straight to Uncle Sam. Don't be that guy. It's like missing the last train home and having to explain it to your spouse. Not fun.

sections.title: Mixing Up Gain Deferral and Exclusion
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking all the capital gain disappears with a QOZ investment. Nope! The *original* gain is only *deferred* until the earlier of the date the QOF investment is sold or December 31, 2026. The *appreciation* of the QOF investment, held for 10+ years, is what gets excluded. So, if you invest $100k gain, and it grows to $500k in 10 years, you still pay tax on the original $100k (eventually), but the $400k gain is tax-free. Don't promise clients complete tax elimination without explaining this nuance!

sections.title: Sarah's Missed Opportunity
sections.content: Sarah sold her company stock for a $500,000 gain. Excited about QOZs, she immediately started researching properties. She found a great project *200* days later and invested the funds. Ouch! Because she missed the 180-day window, she can't defer the gain. The lesson? Due diligence is important, but speed is your friend. Have potential QOFs researched *before* the capital gain event if possible to avoid timeline issues.

sections.title: QOZs in Real-World Tax Planning
sections.content: In practice, you'll often encounter QOZs when working with clients who've had a significant capital gain event – sale of a business, real estate, or even cryptocurrency. They'll ask about options to minimize taxes. That's when you bring up QOZs. Be prepared to explain the complex rules around eligible gains, qualified funds, and the 90% asset test. Also, be sure to document your advice thoroughly, especially regarding the inherent risks of investing in distressed areas. Clients need to be aware of the potential downsides alongside the tax benefits.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• QOZ allows deferral of capital gains + potential permanent exclusion
• Must invest in QOF within 180 days of gain recognition
• 5-year hold: 10% basis step-up (by 12/31/2026)
• 10-year hold: NEW appreciation excluded from tax
• Deferred gain recognized by December 31, 2026
• Any capital gain qualifies; only gain amount (not proceeds) must be invested
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Barry realized a $200,000 capital gain on the sale of stock on March 1, 2024. To qualify for the maximum QOZ benefits, what is the LATEST date Barry must invest in a Qualified Opportunity Fund (QOF)?
options: August 28, 2024
August 29, 2024
September 1, 2024
September 2, 2024
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The investment in a QOF must occur within 180 days of realizing the capital gain. March 1 + 180 days = August 28. Remember to accurately calculate the 180-day window.
knowledgeCheck.hint: How many days are in each month?

reveal.question: What are the three potential tax benefits associated with investing in a Qualified Opportunity Zone?
reveal.answer: 1. Deferral of the original capital gain. 2. Potential step-up in basis of the QOF investment. 3. Exclusion of capital gains on the QOF investment if held for at least 10 years.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following investments would NOT qualify as a QOF investment?
options: Purchase of a building in a designated QOZ for renovation.
Purchase of land in a designated QOZ for future development.
Investment in a publicly traded company that operates a factory in a designated QOZ.
Investment in a startup business located in a designated QOZ.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While QOFs invest in QOZ property, investing in a publicly traded company is generally not considered a direct investment in QOZ property. QOF investments typically involve direct investment in businesses or real estate located within the QOZ.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the directness of the investment in QOZ property.

title: Net Investment Income Tax Planning
description: Minimize the 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax through strategic income classification and timing

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) affects higher-income taxpayers with investment income. Strategic planning can reduce or eliminate NIIT through income reclassification, timing, and threshold management. This is often overlooked in tax planning.

sections.title: NIIT Basics
sections.content: The 3.8% NIIT applies to the LESSER of: (1) Net Investment Income, or (2) Modified AGI exceeding the threshold. It's an additional tax on top of regular income tax and capital gains tax. Thresholds are NOT indexed for inflation.

sections.title: Net Investment Income Includes
items: Interest income (taxable and tax-exempt)
Dividends (qualified and non-qualified)
Capital gains (short-term and long-term)
Rental and royalty income (from passive activities)
Passive activity income from partnerships/S corps
Annuity income (non-qualified)

sections.title: NOT Subject to NIIT
items: Wages and self-employment income (subject to payroll taxes instead)
Active trade or business income
Distributions from qualified retirement plans (401k, IRA)
Social Security benefits
Tax-exempt interest (technically NII but reduces threshold impact)
Gain on sale of principal residence (excluded portion)

sections.title: Material Participation Exception
sections.content: Income from a trade or business is NOT NII if the taxpayer materially participates. This includes S corporation and partnership income where the owner is actively involved. Material participation requires meeting one of seven tests (500 hours, substantially all, etc.).

sections.title: Example: NIIT Calculation
sections.content:
John and Mary (MFJ) have:
• Wages: $200,000
• Dividends: $30,000
• Capital gains: $50,000
• Rental income (passive): $40,000

MAGI: $320,000
Net Investment Income: $30,000 + $50,000 + $40,000 = $120,000
Excess over threshold: $320,000 - $250,000 = $70,000

NIIT Base: Lesser of $120,000 or $70,000 = $70,000
NIIT: $70,000 × 3.8% = $2,660

sections.title: NIIT Planning Strategies
rows: Max retirement contributions, HSA, defer income
Spread gains across years below threshold
Interest not subject to NIIT (but still in MAGI)

sections.title: Real Estate Professional Exception
items: Rental income NOT subject to NIIT if real estate professional status
Requirements: 750+ hours in real property trades/businesses
More than half of personal services in real estate
Must materially participate in each rental activity (or group)
Significant benefit for active real estate investors

sections.title: Grouping Elections
sections.content: Taxpayers can elect to group activities for material participation testing. Once made, grouping is generally irrevocable. Proper grouping can help meet the 500-hour threshold by aggregating hours across related activities. Consider grouping implications for NIIT planning.

sections.title: Timing Strategies
items: Defer capital gains to years with lower MAGI
Accelerate losses to offset NII
Time Roth conversions in lower-income years
Harvest capital losses in high-MAGI years
Bunch income/deductions across years strategically

sections.title: Trust and Estate NIIT
sections.content: Trusts and estates face NIIT at much lower threshold: MAGI over $14,450 (2024). The 3.8% applies quickly. Strategy: Distribute income to beneficiaries (uses their higher thresholds). Simple trusts automatically distribute; complex trusts have flexibility.

sections.title: Memory Aid: NIIT Planning
sections.content:
'NIIT hits PASSIVE PIGS':
P = Passive rental income
I = Interest and dividends
G = Gains (capital)
S = S corps/partnerships (passive only)

Active business income escapes! Material participation is the key escape hatch.

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Retirement plan distributions are NOT subject to NIIT even though they're investment-related! The 3.8% only applies to Net Investment Income, which specifically excludes qualified plan distributions. Don't confuse NII with all investment-related income.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: NIIT: My Least Favorite Tax (and Yours Soon!)
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. The Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) is nobody's favorite. It's like the IRS decided regular taxes weren't enough fun. Figuring out if it applies and how to minimize it is a constant battle. The thresholds haven't budged in years, which means more and more people are getting caught in its web. So buckle up, because mastering this stuff now will save you (and your clients) a lot of headaches later. Think of it as becoming a NIIT ninja – stealthily avoiding this extra tax burden!

sections.title: NIIT Threshold Amnesia: A Costly Error
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming the NIIT thresholds are indexed for inflation like many other tax items. They AREN'T! The $250,000 threshold for married filing jointly, $200,000 for single, and $125,000 for married filing separately have remained stagnant since the NIIT's inception. Students often overestimate the modified AGI limit before NIIT kicks in, leading to incorrect tax projections and potentially missed planning opportunities. Always double-check those thresholds; they're the unchanging gatekeepers of NIIT liability.

sections.title: The Case of Bob's Rental Income
sections.content: Bob, a retired engineer, actively manages his rental properties, generating $60,000 in rental income. He initially assumed it was all subject to NIIT, but after a deeper dive, it was determined that his rental activity constituted a trade or business. Since it was considered a trade or business, it was NOT subject to NIIT. The lesson? Don't automatically assume all investment-related income is subject to NIIT. The level of activity can make all the difference.

sections.title: Client Meetings & NIIT: A Frequent Flyer
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter NIIT when discussing investment strategies with high-income clients. During annual tax planning meetings, proactively analyze their investment income and modified AGI to project potential NIIT liability. Then, explore strategies like Roth conversions, tax-loss harvesting, or timing the sale of investments to minimize the impact. Being proactive about NIIT shows your clients you're thinking ahead and looking out for their best financial interests, solidifying your value as a tax professional.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• 3.8% NIIT on lesser of NII or MAGI over threshold ($250k MFJ)
• NII includes interest, dividends, capital gains, passive income
• Active business income and wages NOT subject to NIIT
• Material participation converts passive to active (escapes NIIT)
• Retirement distributions exempt from NIIT
• Trusts hit 3.8% above only $14,450—distribute to beneficiaries
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah has Net Investment Income (NII) of $60,000 and Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) of $230,000. The NIIT threshold for her filing status is $200,000. What amount is subject to the 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NIIT is applied to the LESSER of NII or the amount by which MAGI exceeds the threshold. Here, MAGI exceeds the threshold by $30,000 ($230,000 - $200,000). Since $30,000 is less than Sarah's NII of $60,000, the NIIT applies to $30,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to compare BOTH NII and the excess of MAGI over the threshold.

reveal.question: Are the NIIT thresholds indexed for inflation?
reveal.answer: No, the NIIT thresholds are NOT indexed for inflation. This means more taxpayers may become subject to the tax over time as their income increases.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)?
options: The NIIT replaces regular income tax on investment income.
The NIIT is in addition to regular income tax and capital gains tax.
The NIIT only applies to taxpayers with capital losses.
The NIIT is calculated based on gross investment income, not net investment income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NIIT is an ADDITIONAL tax on top of regular income tax and capital gains tax. It does not replace any other tax. It's calculated on Net Investment Income (NII), not gross.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the NIIT is an *additional* tax.

title: Retirement Planning: Roth vs Traditional
description: Analyze Roth versus Traditional retirement account decisions for optimal tax efficiency

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The Roth vs. Traditional decision is one of the most common and impactful tax planning questions. The answer depends on current vs. future tax rates, time horizon, and individual circumstances. CPAs must understand the analysis framework to provide proper guidance.

sections.title: The Core Analysis
sections.content: If tax rates are EQUAL at contribution and withdrawal, Roth and Traditional produce identical after-tax results! The key question: Will your marginal rate be higher or lower in retirement? Most people assume lower rates in retirement, but this isn't always true.

sections.title: Mathematical Proof: Equal Rates
sections.content:
$6,000 contribution, 25% tax rate both periods, 7% return, 30 years

Traditional:
• Invest $6,000 pre-tax → Grows to $45,675
• Withdrawal taxed at 25%: $45,675 × 0.75 = $34,256

Roth:
• Pay 25% tax first: $6,000 × 0.75 = $4,500 invested
• Grows to: $4,500 × 7.61 = $34,256 (tax-free!)

Same result! The deciding factor is rate CHANGE.

sections.title: When Traditional Wins
items: High current income, lower expected retirement income
Currently in peak earning years
Expect to retire in low/no income tax state
Planning significant charitable giving in retirement (QCDs)
Need the upfront deduction for cash flow
Employer match is always pre-tax (capture it!)

sections.title: When Roth Wins
items: Currently in low tax bracket (early career, gap year)
Expect higher rates in retirement (career growth, tax law changes)
Large traditional balances already (RMDs will push up bracket)
Want to leave tax-free inheritance
Value of no RMDs (more flexibility)
Estate planning benefits (heirs inherit tax-free)

sections.title: Roth Conversion Analysis
sections.content: Converting Traditional to Roth triggers current income (taxable event). Best done in low-income years: sabbatical, early retirement before Social Security/RMDs, market downturns (convert at reduced value). Pay conversion tax from outside funds if possible.

sections.title: Roth Conversion Checklist
items: Is current bracket lower than expected future bracket?
Can you pay tax from non-retirement funds? (Better outcome)
Will conversion bump you into higher bracket or trigger IRMAA?
How long until you need the funds? (Need time to recover tax cost)
Are you subject to pro-rata rule? (Non-deductible basis)

sections.title: Backdoor Roth IRA
sections.content: High earners exceeding Roth IRA income limits can use 'backdoor': Contribute to non-deductible Traditional IRA, then convert to Roth. Conversion is tax-free if no pre-tax IRA balance. BEWARE: Pro-rata rule applies if you have existing Traditional IRA funds.

sections.title: Pro-Rata Rule Example
sections.content:
Sarah has $90,000 in Traditional IRA (all pre-tax) and wants backdoor Roth.

Step 1: Contributes $7,000 non-deductible to Traditional IRA
Total IRA: $97,000 ($90,000 pre-tax + $7,000 after-tax)

Step 2: Converts $7,000 to Roth
Taxable portion: $7,000 × ($90,000/$97,000) = $6,495
Tax-free portion: $7,000 × ($7,000/$97,000) = $505

The pro-rata rule makes backdoor Roth less effective when pre-tax IRA balances exist. Consider rolling Traditional IRA into 401(k) first!

sections.title: SECURE Act 2.0 Changes
items: Roth 401(k): No longer subject to RMDs (starting 2024)
Higher catch-up for ages 60-63: $10,000 (starting 2025)
Employer match can go to Roth (employee's choice)
529 to Roth IRA rollovers (limits apply)
RMD age increased to 73 (2023), then 75 (2033)

sections.title: Memory Aid: Roth vs Traditional
sections.content:
'Pay taxes when CHEAPER':
• Low bracket now? → Roth (pay tax now at low rate)
• High bracket now? → Traditional (defer to lower rate)
• Equal rates? → Roth (flexibility, no RMDs, estate benefits)
• Uncertain? → Diversify between both (tax diversification)

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The pro-rata rule for Roth conversions considers ALL Traditional IRAs (including SEP and SIMPLE after 2 years), not just the one you're converting. You can't isolate non-deductible contributions for tax-free conversion if you have ANY pre-tax IRA funds!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Roth vs Traditional: My Gut Feeling (and the Math)
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Everyone *thinks* they want a Roth, right? Pay the taxes now and be done with it! But I've seen too many folks blindly jump in without running the numbers. Don't be swayed by the hype. The 'tax-free growth' sounds amazing, but if your tax bracket tanks in retirement, you might be kicking yourself for not taking the deduction now. It's all about projecting your future tax situation. Spend the time doing the projections. Trust me, your future self will thank you (or curse you, if you don't!).

sections.title: Forgetting the Time Value of Money with Roth vs Trad
sections.content: A common mistake is ignoring the immediate tax benefit of a traditional IRA. Students focus solely on the tax-free withdrawals from a Roth, overlooking the potential for investing the tax savings from a traditional IRA contribution. Let's say you're in the 22% bracket. Contributing $6,500 to a traditional IRA saves you $1,430 in taxes *today*. Invest that $1,430! It's not just about the final withdrawal amount; it's about the growth potential of those immediate tax savings. Ignoring this skews the comparison.

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise: Higher Taxes in Retirement?
sections.content: Sarah, a high-earning consultant, always maxed out her Roth 401(k). She assumed retirement would mean lower taxes. However, she inherited a substantial rental property portfolio shortly before retiring. The rental income, combined with required minimum distributions, pushed her into an even *higher* tax bracket than during her working years. Had she utilized a traditional 401(k), she could have significantly reduced her tax burden during her peak earning years.

sections.title: Client Scenarios: Roth vs Traditional in the Real World
sections.content: In practice, you'll use this analysis constantly. Think about a young professional just starting out – a Roth IRA is often a great choice, as their income (and tax bracket) will likely increase. On the other hand, a late-career professional facing high current taxes might benefit more from the immediate deduction of a traditional contribution. You'll need to gather information about their current income, expected retirement income, other assets, and risk tolerance to make the best recommendation. This is a core skill for any tax advisor.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Equal tax rates = equal outcomes; rate CHANGE determines winner
• Traditional: Deduction now, taxed at withdrawal (higher rate now favors)
• Roth: No deduction, tax-free withdrawal (lower rate now favors)
• Roth conversion best in low-income years; pay tax from outside funds
• Backdoor Roth for high earners; watch pro-rata rule
• Tax diversification (some in each) provides flexibility
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Roth vs. Traditional IRA: Tax Rate Sensitivity

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual is deciding between contributing $6,500 to a Roth IRA or a Traditional IRA. They anticipate being in a lower marginal tax bracket during retirement than they are currently. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the after-tax outcome, assuming the same investment growth in both accounts?
options: The Roth IRA will always result in a higher after-tax amount due to tax-free withdrawals.
The Traditional IRA will always result in a higher after-tax amount due to the immediate tax deduction.
The Roth IRA and Traditional IRA will result in the same after-tax amount if the tax rate is the same at contribution and withdrawal.
The Traditional IRA will likely result in a higher after-tax amount because the individual expects to be in a lower tax bracket during retirement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: If the individual expects to be in a lower tax bracket during retirement, the Traditional IRA is generally more advantageous. They receive a tax deduction now at a higher rate and pay taxes later at a lower rate. The Roth IRA offers tax-free withdrawals, but the contributions are made with after-tax dollars at the higher current rate. If tax rates were equal, the after-tax outcome would be the same (except for the timing difference of the tax benefit).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of tax deductions and withdrawals at different tax rates.

sections.title: Key Concept Recall: Tax Rate Parity

reveal.question: What is the key conclusion regarding Roth vs. Traditional retirement accounts if the tax rate at the time of contribution is equal to the tax rate at the time of withdrawal?
reveal.answer: If the tax rate at contribution equals the tax rate at withdrawal, the Roth IRA and the Traditional IRA will produce the same after-tax result. This is a fundamental concept in retirement planning and highlights the importance of estimating future tax rates.

sections.title: Side-by-Side Comparison: Roth vs Traditional IRA

rows.label: Contribution Tax Treatment
values: After-tax
Pre-tax (deductible) or After-tax (non-deductible)

rows.label: Withdrawal Tax Treatment
values: Tax-free (if qualified)
Taxable (in retirement)

rows.label: Best For (Tax Rate Perspective)
values: Expecting higher tax rate in retirement
Expecting lower tax rate in retirement

rows.label: Tax Rate at Contribution (Advantageous)
values: Lower
Higher

title: Charitable Giving Strategies
description: Maximize tax benefits through strategic charitable contribution planning

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Charitable giving offers significant tax planning opportunities, but the rules are complex. Choosing the right asset to donate, timing contributions effectively, and using advanced vehicles can dramatically increase both the tax benefit and philanthropic impact.

sections.title: Appreciated Property Strategy
sections.content: Donating long-term appreciated property provides a double benefit: (1) Deduction for full fair market value, and (2) No capital gains tax on appreciation. This is almost always better than selling and donating cash. Must hold property >1 year for FMV deduction.

sections.title: Example: Appreciated Stock Donation
sections.content:
Alice (32% bracket, 15% LTCG) wants to donate $50,000 to charity. She owns stock worth $50,000 with $10,000 basis.

Option 1: Sell stock, donate cash
• Capital gain: $40,000 × 15% = $6,000 tax
• NIIT (3.8% if applicable): $40,000 × 3.8% = $1,520
• Deduction value: $50,000 × 32% = $16,000
• Net benefit: $16,000 - $7,520 = $8,480

Option 2: Donate stock directly
• No capital gain recognized
• Deduction value: $50,000 × 32% = $16,000
• Net benefit: $16,000

Stock donation saves $7,520 in avoided taxes!

sections.title: Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs)
items: Immediate deduction in year of contribution
Distributions to charities made over time at donor's recommendation
