Ideal for bunching strategy (large deduction in high-income year)
Can contribute cash, securities, or other assets
No minimum distribution requirement
Contribution is irrevocable—cannot take back funds

sections.title: Qualified Charitable Distributions (QCDs)
sections.content: Age 70½+ can transfer up to $105,000 (2024) directly from IRA to charity. QCD is excluded from gross income entirely—better than deduction! Counts toward RMD. Not available from 401(k) or employer plans.

sections.title: QCD vs Itemized Deduction
sections.content:
Bob, age 72, wants to give $10,000 to charity. Takes standard deduction.

Option 1: Withdraw IRA, donate cash
• IRA withdrawal: $10,000 income
• No deduction benefit (standard deduction)
• Tax at 22%: $2,200
• Net cost: $2,200

Option 2: QCD from IRA
• $10,000 never included in income
• Tax: $0
• Also satisfies RMD!

QCD saves $2,200 AND reduces AGI (helps Medicare premiums, Social Security taxation, etc.)

sections.title: CRT Benefits
items: Immediate partial charitable deduction (present value of remainder)
Avoid capital gains on contributed appreciated property
Income stream for life or term of years (max 20)
Remainder goes to charity at termination
Can diversify concentrated stock position without immediate tax

sections.title: Charitable Lead Trusts (CLTs)
sections.content: Opposite of CRT: Charity receives income stream first, remainder goes to non-charitable beneficiaries (family). Used for estate planning—freeze gift/estate value while passing future growth to heirs. Grantor CLT gives income tax deduction; non-grantor gives gift/estate tax deduction.

sections.title: Private Foundations
items: Family controls charitable giving permanently
5% minimum annual distribution requirement
Lower AGI limits (30% cash, 20% LTCG property)
Excise tax on investment income (1.39%)
Self-dealing rules restrict transactions with insiders
More administrative burden than DAF

sections.title: Memory Aid: Charitable Vehicles
sections.content:
'DAF = Donate And Forget (charity waits)'
'CRT = Charity Remainder Trust (YOU get income now, charity later)'
'CLT = Charity Leads Trust (CHARITY gets income now, heirs later)'
'QCD = Quietly Contributes Directly (skips your return entirely)'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Appreciated property held ≤1 year only gets BASIS deduction, not FMV! The FMV deduction requires long-term holding (>1 year). Also, tangible personal property (art, collectibles) only gets FMV deduction if the charity's use is related to its exempt purpose.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Don't Leave Money on the Table!
sections.content: Okay, real talk. I've seen clients donate cash when they could have donated stock. It's like using a hammer to swat a fly...it works, but you're missing out on a HUGE tax benefit! Seriously, if your client has appreciated stock they've held for over a year, gently suggest they donate that instead of writing a check. You'll look like a rockstar, and they'll save a ton. Think of it as a win-win-win: charity wins, client wins, you win (because they'll refer you more!).

sections.title: Short-Term Property = No FMV Deduction
sections.content: A really common mistake is trying to deduct the full fair market value of property held for less than a year. Students often remember the general rule about appreciated property but forget the holding period requirement. If the client held the property for a year or less, the deduction is limited to the taxpayer's basis, not the fair market value. So, double-check that holding period! Trust me, the IRS will catch this, and your client won't be happy with the penalties and interest.

sections.title: The Smith Family's Stock Donation
sections.content: The Smith family wants to donate $10,000 to their local food bank. They have stock they purchased for $2,000 five years ago, now worth $10,000. If they sell the stock and donate the cash, they'll pay capital gains tax on the $8,000 gain. By donating the stock directly, they avoid the capital gains tax AND get a $10,000 deduction. That's tax planning gold!

sections.title: Charitable Donation Optimization
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this scenario most often when working with high-net-worth individuals or business owners. They frequently have appreciated assets (stocks, real estate, artwork) that can be strategically donated to maximize tax benefits. When reviewing their portfolio, proactively identify these opportunities. It's not just about filling out a form; it's about adding significant value through proactive tax planning. Use software to model different scenarios and illustrate the tax savings to your client.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Donate LTCG appreciated property for FMV deduction + avoid capital gains
• DAFs enable bunching (big deduction now, give over time)
• QCDs (70½+) excluded from income—better than deduction for non-itemizers
• CRTs provide income + deduction + capital gain avoidance
• 60% AGI limit for cash; 30% for LTCG property to public charities
• Substantiation requirements vary by amount and property type
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Barry owns stock purchased 5 years ago for $10,000. The stock is now worth $30,000. He is in the 20% long-term capital gains tax bracket and the 37% ordinary income tax bracket. If Barry donates the stock directly to a qualified charity, what is the combined tax benefit (deduction and avoided capital gains tax) assuming he can deduct the full amount of the contribution?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The tax benefit consists of two parts: the deduction for the fair market value of the stock ($30,000), and the avoided capital gains tax on the appreciation. The avoided capital gains tax is $20,000 * 20% = $4,000. The deduction of $30,000 reduces his ordinary income, resulting in a tax savings of $30,000 * 37% = $11,100. Therefore, the combined tax benefit is $11,100 + $4,000 + $11,000 = $22,100, but the question asks for the *benefit* of the donation in terms of deduction and avoided capital gains tax, not the resulting tax savings. The FMV deduction is $30,000. The capital gains tax avoided is 20% of the $20,000 gain = $4,000. $30,000 + $4,000 = $34,000. The deduction amount of $30,000 multiplied by Barry's 37% tax bracket equals $11,100. The capital gains tax avoided is $20,000 * 20% = $4,000. $11,100 + $4,000 = $15,100. This is not an answer choice, so the best answer is $26,000, which represents the FMV less the capital gains tax that would have been paid.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the deduction *and* the avoided capital gains.

reveal.question: What is the minimum holding period required to deduct the fair market value of donated appreciated property?
reveal.answer: The property must be held for more than one year (long-term capital gain property) to be eligible for a deduction based on its fair market value. If held for one year or less, the deduction is limited to the property's basis.

knowledgeCheck.question: Carol donates stock held for 10 months to a qualified charity. The stock's basis is $5,000, and its fair market value on the date of donation is $8,000. What is the maximum amount Carol can deduct as a charitable contribution?
options: There is no deduction allowed
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the stock was held for less than one year, it is considered short-term property. The deduction is limited to the lesser of the property's fair market value or its basis. In this case, the basis ($5,000) is less than the fair market value ($8,000), so the deduction is limited to $5,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the holding period requirement for deducting fair market value versus basis.

title: Entity Selection: Comprehensive Comparison
description: Master entity selection analysis considering tax, liability, and operational factors

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Entity selection is a foundational business decision with significant and often irreversible tax consequences. CPAs must analyze multiple factors—tax rates, liability protection, operational flexibility, exit planning—to recommend the optimal structure.

sections.title: Self-Employment Tax Savings
sections.content: S corporations can reduce SE/payroll taxes by splitting income between 'reasonable compensation' (subject to FICA) and distributions (not subject to FICA). But compensation must be reasonable—IRS challenges unreasonably low salaries.

sections.title: Example: S Corp SE Tax Savings
sections.content:
Sarah's consulting LLC earns $200,000 net profit.

As Sole Proprietor:
• SE tax: $200,000 × 92.35% × 15.3% = $28,260

As S Corporation (reasonable salary $100,000):
• FICA on salary: $100,000 × 7.65% = $7,650 (employee share)
• Employer share: $7,650 (deductible)
• Total: $15,300 (vs $28,260)
• Distribution: $100,000 (no FICA)
• Savings: ~$13,000 annually!

But: Payroll compliance costs, reasonable comp risk.

sections.title: Section 199A Deduction
sections.content: Pass-through entities may qualify for 20% deduction on Qualified Business Income. S corps, partnerships, and sole props benefit; C corps don't (but have 21% rate). Limits apply based on: wages paid, qualified property, specified service businesses, and taxable income.

sections.title: C Corporation Considerations
items: 21% flat rate (lower than top individual rate of 37%)
Double taxation on distributions (corp tax + dividend tax)
Accumulated earnings can defer second level of tax
QSBS exclusion potential (§1202): Up to 100% gain exclusion
Preferred for venture capital/IPO (unlimited shareholders, classes)
Foreign tax credit stacking opportunities

sections.title: S Corporation Limitations
items: Maximum 100 shareholders
Only individuals, estates, certain trusts (no corps/partnerships)
One class of stock only
Must be domestic corporation
No nonresident alien shareholders
Limited basis from debt (shareholder loans only)

sections.title: Partnership Advantages
items: Flexible allocations (§704(b) substantial economic effect)
Basis includes share of all partnership debt
Special allocations of specific items (depreciation, gains)
No double taxation; no entity-level tax
Can have corporate, foreign, tax-exempt partners
Profits interests for employees (tax-advantaged compensation)

sections.title: Liability Considerations
sections.content: All corporations and LLCs provide liability protection, but: personal guarantees override protection; professional malpractice still personal; veil piercing risks if formalities not followed. Sole props and general partners have unlimited personal liability.

sections.title: Exit/Sale Considerations
rows: Interest sale, buyer gets carryover basis

sections.title: Memory Aid: Entity Selection
sections.content:
'SELF-EMPLOYED? Think SPIT':
S = S corp saves SE tax (with reasonable comp)
P = Partnership for flexibility & debt basis
I = Individual/sole prop for simplicity
T = Tax integration: C corp 21% + dividend vs pass-through 37%

Remember: No perfect answer—it depends on facts!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: S corporation distributions above basis create CAPITAL GAIN, not ordinary income. C corporation distributions are dividends (qualified or non-qualified). Know the character difference! Also: S corp losses limited to stock + debt basis; partnerships include share of ALL debt.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: S Corp Salary Shenanigans: A Tax Pro's Take
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. The S corp 'reasonable compensation' game is a tightrope walk. I've seen folks try to pay themselves peanuts, like $30,000 when they're clearly working 60 hours a week and generating $200,000 in revenue. The IRS isn't blind! They'll reclassify those distributions as wages, and suddenly you're facing back taxes, penalties, and interest. Don't be greedy. Aim for a salary that reflects your role and contribution. Think, 'What would I pay someone else to do this job?' That's your benchmark.

sections.title: Forgetting the Double Tax of C Corps
sections.content: A common mistake is overlooking the double taxation inherent in C corporations. Students often focus solely on the lower corporate tax rate (21%) without considering that dividends paid to shareholders are taxed again at the individual level. This double taxation can significantly erode profits, especially for small businesses where the owners are also actively involved. Always factor in both corporate and individual tax implications when comparing C corps to pass-through entities like S corps or LLCs. Run the numbers! Don't just assume lower corporate tax is always better.

sections.title: Lisa's LLC Liability Lesson
sections.content: Lisa started a graphic design business as a sole proprietorship. A disgruntled client sued her for $500,000, alleging copyright infringement. Because Lisa didn't have the liability protection of an LLC or corporation, her personal assets – her house and savings – were at risk. Choosing the right entity structure isn't just about taxes; it's about shielding yourself from potential business liabilities. Lisa learned this the hard (and expensive) way.

sections.title: Entity Selection in the Real World
sections.content: In practice, entity selection is rarely a straightforward decision. You'll often be working with clients who are already operating under a certain structure, and you'll need to assess whether it's still the optimal choice. This involves projecting future income, considering the client's risk tolerance, and weighing the administrative burdens of different entities. You'll also need to be able to explain the pros and cons in plain English, not just tax jargon. Being able to articulate these differences clearly is what separates a good tax advisor from a great one.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• S corp can reduce SE tax by splitting wages/distributions (reasonable comp required)
• C corp: 21% rate but double taxation on distributions
• Partnership: Flexible allocations, basis from all debt
• §199A: 20% deduction for pass-throughs (limitations apply)
• QSBS (§1202) can exclude 100% of C corp gain
• Exit strategy matters: Asset vs equity sale implications differ by entity
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John, the sole shareholder of an S corporation, performs significant services for the business. The S corporation generates $200,000 in net income before any payments to John. John takes a salary of $40,000 and a distribution of $160,000. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the IRS's potential challenge to John's compensation?
options: The IRS will likely accept John's compensation as reasonable, as S corporations have significant flexibility in determining owner compensation.
The IRS will likely challenge John's compensation as unreasonably low if $40,000 does not reflect the fair market value of the services he provided.
The IRS will only challenge John's compensation if the distribution exceeds the accumulated adjustments account (AAA).
The IRS will automatically reclassify the distribution as salary, subject to FICA taxes, regardless of the reasonableness of the $40,000 salary.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS scrutinizes S corporation owner compensation to ensure that a 'reasonable salary' is paid before distributions are taken. If the salary is deemed unreasonably low compared to the services provided, the IRS can reclassify distributions as wages, subjecting them to FICA taxes. The AAA and automatic reclassification are not the primary determining factors.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the IRS's goal: to collect FICA taxes.

reveal.question: Explain how an S corporation can potentially reduce self-employment taxes for its owner.
reveal.answer: An S corporation allows the owner to split income between salary (subject to FICA taxes) and distributions (not subject to FICA taxes). By taking a 'reasonable salary' and the remaining income as distributions, the owner can potentially reduce their overall self-employment tax burden compared to a sole proprietorship or partnership where all profits are subject to SE tax.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following entity types are generally subject to self-employment tax on their profits (before considering any S-election)?
options: C Corporation
S Corporation
Limited Liability Company (LLC) taxed as a partnership
All of the above
knowledgeCheck.explanation: LLCs taxed as partnerships (and partnerships themselves) generally pass through profits to their owners, who are then subject to self-employment tax on their share. C corporations are subject to corporate income tax, not self-employment tax at the owner level. S corporations pass through income, but the owner's salary is subject to payroll taxes, and distributions are generally not subject to self-employment tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which entity types have income directly attributed to their owners without being considered employees.

title: Check-the-Box Regulations
description: Navigate entity classification elections under the check-the-box regulations

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The check-the-box regulations give taxpayers flexibility to choose how eligible entities are taxed. Understanding default classifications and election options is essential for entity planning, restructuring, and optimizing tax treatment.

sections.title: Check-the-Box Overview
sections.content: Treasury Regulations §301.7701 allow 'eligible entities' to elect their federal tax classification. An LLC can be taxed as a sole proprietorship, partnership, S corporation, or C corporation. The election is made on Form 8832.

sections.title: Default Classifications
items: Single-member LLC: Disregarded entity (Schedule C or branch of owner)
Multi-member LLC: Partnership (Form 1065)
Business trusts: Generally taxed as corporations
State law corporations: Always corporations (no choice)
Publicly traded partnerships: Treated as corporations

sections.title: Making the Election
rows: Effective up to 75 days before OR 12 months after filing
Can't change again for 60 months (exceptions apply)

sections.title: S Corporation Election
sections.content: To be taxed as an S corp, an LLC must: (1) Be classified as a corporation (Form 8832 or formed as Inc.), then (2) Elect S status (Form 2553). Shortcut: Filing Form 2553 for an LLC is treated as electing both corp status and S status.

sections.title: Example: LLC to S Corp
sections.content:
Tech Solutions LLC (2 members) wants S corp treatment starting January 1, 2025.

Option 1: Two-step process
• Form 8832 to elect corporation (effective 1/1/2025)
• Form 2553 to elect S corp (due by 3/15/2025)

Option 2: Shortcut
• Form 2553 only (with late election relief if needed)
• IRS treats this as electing corp AND S corp

Result: LLC taxed as S corporation starting 1/1/2025

sections.title: Conversion to Corporation
sections.content: When a partnership elects corporation status, it's treated as if the partnership contributed all assets to a new corporation in exchange for stock under §351. Generally tax-free if requirements met. Built-in gains may be subject to later recognition.

sections.title: Conversion FROM Corporation
sections.content: Converting FROM corporation to partnership is TAXABLE! Treated as a corporate liquidation followed by contribution to partnership. Corporation recognizes gain on appreciated assets; shareholders recognize gain/loss on deemed distribution. Avoid this—plan entity choice upfront!

sections.title: 60-Month Limitation
items: After electing, can't change classification for 60 months
Exceptions: Change in ownership (50%+), IRS approval
Applies to the specific entity, not successors
Purpose: Prevent whipsaw planning (frequent changes)
New entity can choose any classification immediately

sections.title: Disregarded Entity Uses
items: Single-member LLC owned by individual: Schedule C, E, or F
Single-member LLC owned by corporation: Branch of parent
QSub (Qualified S Subsidiary): Disregarded for income tax
Employment taxes: May be treated as separate for FICA purposes
State law: May still require separate state filings

sections.title: Memory Aid: Check-the-Box
sections.content:
'LLC = Lots of Classification Choices':
• 1 member: Disregarded (Schedule C) or Corporation
• 2+ members: Partnership or Corporation
• Corporation: Add S election for pass-through

'Into corp = free; Out of corp = fee (taxable!)'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: An LLC cannot directly elect S corporation status. It must first be classified as a corporation, then elect S status. However, filing Form 2553 alone is treated as making BOTH elections. Don't confuse Form 8832 (entity classification) with Form 2553 (S election).
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: CTB: It's All About the Choices!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Check-the-box regulations are like choosing your own adventure, but with taxes. An LLC? It can morph into practically anything! Sole prop, partnership, S-corp, C-corp... it's all on the table. The key is *knowing* you have these options. Too many folks just assume default status and potentially miss out on some serious tax savings. Don't be that person! Form 8832 is your golden ticket. Use it wisely!

sections.title: Forgetting the Default Classification!
sections.content: Students often forget the default classification rules. If you *don't* file Form 8832, the IRS will classify your entity based on the number of owners: single-member LLC becomes a disregarded entity (sole proprietorship for tax purposes), and multi-member LLC becomes a partnership. This is crucial! Imagine accidentally operating as a partnership when you intended to be taxed as an S-corp. That's a mess of K-1s and potential penalties. Always double-check the default rules and compare them to your desired tax outcome.

sections.title: Sarah's S-Corp Surprise
sections.content: Sarah and her business partner, Tom, formed an LLC with the intention of electing S-corp status. They diligently operated their business but forgot to file Form 8832. Two years later, during an audit, the IRS classified them as a partnership for those two years, resulting in unexpected self-employment tax for Sarah and Tom. The lesson? Don't leave your entity classification to chance; file that Form 8832!

sections.title: Client Intake: CTB is Your Opening Move
sections.content: In practice, discussing check-the-box regulations is often one of the *first* things you'll do with a new business client. During client intake, you need to determine their business structure and anticipated tax impact. Asking about their desired tax classification (sole prop, partnership, S-corp, C-corp) based on the CTB rules is essential. This early conversation helps you advise them on the best entity structure and avoids headaches down the road. Missing this step can lead to costly restructuring and amended returns.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Check-the-box allows eligible entities to choose tax classification
• Single-member LLC default: Disregarded; Multi-member: Partnership
• Form 8832 for entity classification; Form 2553 for S election
• Partnership → Corp: Generally tax-free (§351)
• Corp → Partnership: Taxable liquidation (avoid!)
• 60-month rule limits frequent changes
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Under Treasury Regulations §301.7701, which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the 'check-the-box' regulations?
options: All entities, regardless of their legal structure, can elect their federal tax classification.
Only corporations can elect their federal tax classification using Form 8832.
Eligible entities, such as LLCs, can elect their federal tax classification, allowing them to be taxed as a sole proprietorship, partnership, S corporation, or C corporation.
The 'check-the-box' regulations only apply to state tax classifications, not federal.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 'check-the-box' regulations allow 'eligible entities,' primarily LLCs, the flexibility to choose their federal tax classification. They can elect to be taxed as a sole proprietorship (if single-member LLC), partnership, S corporation, or C corporation, providing significant tax planning opportunities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which entity types are most commonly discussed in the context of 'check-the-box' regulations.

reveal.question: What form is used to make the 'check-the-box' election with the IRS?
reveal.answer: Form 8832, Entity Classification Election, is the form used to elect the entity's federal tax classification under the 'check-the-box' regulations.

rows.label: Tax Rate
values: Individual tax rates (flow-through)
Corporate tax rate (currently 21%)

rows.label: Tax Liability
values: Owners pay tax on their share of the partnership income, regardless of distribution
Corporation pays tax on its profits; shareholders pay tax on dividends

rows.label: Losses
values: Losses generally flow through to the owners, subject to certain limitations
Losses generally stay within the corporation, unless consolidated return is filed

rows.label: Form Used for Election (Assuming an LLC)
values: Form 8832
Form 8832

title: State Tax Considerations: Nexus
description: Understand state tax nexus rules and multistate taxation triggers

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: State taxes can significantly impact total tax burden and business structure decisions. Understanding nexus—the connection requiring a business to pay taxes in a state—is essential for compliance and planning in our increasingly mobile, multistate economy.

sections.title: What Is Nexus?
sections.content: Nexus is a sufficient connection between a taxpayer and a state that allows the state to impose a tax obligation. The threshold varies by state and tax type (income, sales, franchise). Constitutional limits (Due Process and Commerce Clause) require minimum contacts.

sections.title: Types of Nexus
rows: Sales, payroll, or property in state

sections.title: South Dakota v. Wayfair (2018)
sections.content: The Supreme Court overturned Quill, allowing states to require sales tax collection based on economic nexus alone—no physical presence required. Most states adopted $100,000 sales OR 200 transactions threshold. This revolutionized multistate tax compliance.

sections.title: Income Tax Nexus
items: P.L. 86-272: Federal protection for solicitation of tangible goods only
Does NOT protect: Services, intangibles, digital goods
Many states interpret P.L. 86-272 narrowly
Economic nexus for income tax expanding post-Wayfair
Factor presence nexus: Property, payroll, or sales in state

sections.title: P.L. 86-272 Protection
sections.content: Public Law 86-272 prohibits states from taxing income derived from interstate commerce if the only activity is solicitation of orders for tangible personal property. Orders must be approved and fulfilled outside the state. ANY activity beyond solicitation voids protection.

sections.title: Activities That Void P.L. 86-272
items: Maintaining inventory in state (beyond samples)
Service or repair activities
Installation of products
Technical assistance or training
Investigating creditworthiness
Collecting delinquent accounts
Website interactivity (MTC interpretation)

sections.title: Franchise/Gross Receipts Taxes
rows: Economic nexus similar to sales tax

sections.title: Pass-Through Entity Nexus
sections.content: Partners and S corp shareholders may have nexus in states where the entity operates, regardless of the owner's home state. Many states impose withholding on pass-through income to nonresidents. Composite returns may be available.

sections.title: Planning Strategies
items: Monitor economic nexus thresholds in all states with sales
Evaluate P.L. 86-272 protection carefully (activities matter)
Consider entity structure to isolate state exposure
Review employee work locations (especially remote work)
Use nexus questionnaires/studies before expanding
Implement sales tax automation for multi-state sellers

sections.title: Remote Work Implications
sections.content: Remote workers can create nexus! An employee working from home in State X may create income tax nexus, sales tax nexus, and withholding obligations for the employer. Post-COVID, this is a major compliance issue. Track where employees work and for how long.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Nexus Types
sections.content:
'PEACE creates nexus':
P = Physical presence (property, people)
E = Economic activity (Wayfair thresholds)
A = Agency relationships (representatives)
C = Click-through (affiliate links)
E = Employee presence (including remote)

Any of these can trigger state tax obligations!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: P.L. 86-272 only protects against INCOME tax—not sales tax, franchise tax, or other taxes. And it only covers solicitation of TANGIBLE personal property, not services or intangibles. Many candidates over-apply this protection!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Nexus: It's Like a Taxing Tinder Profile
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Nexus sounds boring, but it's basically a state checking if you're 'compatible' enough to tax you. Before Wayfair, it was all about physical presence – like, did you even bother showing up? Now, states are swiping right based on economic activity. $100k in sales? 200 transactions? Boom, you're a match! Don't get catfished by state tax laws; know your thresholds or you'll be paying for dinner... for the state.

sections.title: Confusing Sales Tax & Income Tax Nexus
sections.content: Students often think the Wayfair decision and economic nexus apply equally to both sales tax AND income tax. While Wayfair significantly expanded sales tax nexus, income tax nexus still often requires a higher level of connection, including potential physical presence for some states. Don't assume a $100,000 sales threshold automatically subjects you to state income tax. Always research the specific nexus rules for EACH tax type in EACH state. One size does NOT fit all!

sections.title: Sarah's Shopify Snafu
sections.content: Sarah runs a successful online store through Shopify, selling handmade jewelry. She ships nationwide and hit $120,000 in sales to California last year, but only made 50 transactions. She's always lived in and operated from Oregon. Even though she has no physical presence in California, the $120,000 in sales creates economic nexus. Sarah now needs to register for and collect California sales tax, a nasty surprise she hadn't budgeted for.

sections.title: Nexus: Your Due Diligence Checklist Item
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter nexus issues primarily during entity planning and business expansion. When advising a client considering selling online or expanding into new states, a nexus study is crucial. This involves analyzing their sales volume, transaction counts, and physical presence (if any) in each state. Document your findings and advise on registration requirements. Failing to identify nexus early can result in significant penalties and back taxes, a headache no one wants.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Nexus is the connection allowing a state to impose tax
• Economic nexus (Wayfair): $100k sales or 200 transactions for sales tax
• P.L. 86-272: Limited protection for solicitation of tangible goods
• Remote workers can create nexus in their work state
• Pass-through owners may have nexus where entity operates
• Different nexus standards for income, sales, franchise taxes
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: After the South Dakota v. Wayfair decision, which of the following is the MOST important factor in determining if a company has sales tax nexus in a state?
options: The company's physical presence in the state.
The number of employees the company has in the state.
The company's economic activity in the state, exceeding a certain threshold.
Whether the company's headquarters are located in the state.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The South Dakota v. Wayfair decision established that physical presence is no longer the sole determining factor for sales tax nexus. Economic activity, such as exceeding a certain sales or transaction threshold, is now sufficient to create nexus.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the impact of Wayfair on remote sales.

reveal.question: Define 'nexus' in the context of state taxation.
reveal.answer: Nexus is a sufficient connection between a taxpayer and a state that allows the state to impose a tax obligation. The level of connection required varies by state and tax type (e.g., income tax, sales tax, franchise tax).

rows.label: Basis
values: Physical presence in the state (e.g., office, warehouse, employees).
Economic activity in the state, such as sales or transactions, exceeding a specified threshold (e.g., $100,000 in sales or 200 transactions).

rows.label: Legal Precedent (Pre-2018)
values: Quill Corp. v. North Dakota (Limited states ability to tax without physical presence)
Generally not applicable before Wayfair decision.

rows.label: Legal Precedent (Post-2018)
values: Still relevant for certain tax types and situations, but less critical for sales tax.
South Dakota v. Wayfair (Established economic nexus as sufficient for sales tax).

rows.label: Example
values: Having a retail store in California.
Selling $150,000 worth of goods to customers in Texas without any physical presence there.

title: State Apportionment Formulas
description: Apply state income apportionment formulas to allocate multistate business income

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: When a business operates in multiple states, each state taxes only its portion of the business income. Apportionment formulas determine that portion. Understanding these formulas enables tax planning through strategic location of sales, property, and payroll.

sections.title: Apportionment vs Allocation
rows: Dividing business income among states by formula
Assigning specific items to specific states
Real estate gains to property state

sections.title: Traditional Three-Factor Formula
sections.content:
The traditional UDITPA formula equally weights three factors: Property, Payroll, and Sales. Each factor compares in-state activity to everywhere activity. The average determines the apportionment percentage.

Apportionment % = (Property% + Payroll% + Sales%) ÷ 3

sections.title: Factor Calculations
rows: Average value of real/tangible property in state
Compensation paid to employees in state

sections.title: Modern Trend: Single Sales Factor
sections.content: Most states have moved to single sales factor (SSF) apportionment, weighting sales at 100%. This benefits companies with in-state property and payroll selling out-of-state. It disadvantages companies selling INTO the state from elsewhere.

sections.title: State Formula Variations
rows: CA, NY, TX, IL, PA, and most states

sections.title: Example: Three-Factor vs Single Sales
sections.content:
TechCorp has:
• Property: 80% in State A, 20% in State B
• Payroll: 90% in State A, 10% in State B
• Sales: 30% in State A, 70% in State B

Three-Factor (State A):
(80% + 90% + 30%) ÷ 3 = 66.7% apportioned to A

Single Sales Factor (State A):
30% apportioned to A

If State A has higher rates, single sales factor is advantageous—more income apportioned to State B where TechCorp has lower physical presence.

sections.title: Market-Based Sourcing
sections.content: Most states now use market-based sourcing for services: Sales are sourced to where the customer receives benefit, not where services are performed. This shifts tax burden to states with customers rather than states with operations.

sections.title: Throwback and Throwout Rules
items: Throwback: Sales to states where company has no nexus are 'thrown back' to origination state
Throwout: Sales with no nexus are excluded from both numerator AND denominator
Purpose: Prevent 'nowhere income' that escapes all state tax
Planning: Establish nexus strategically to minimize throwback
Approximately half of states have throwback rules

sections.title: Planning Strategies
items: Locate property and payroll in states without income tax or with low rates
In single-sales-factor states, in-state operations don't increase tax
Evaluate market-based sourcing impact on service businesses
Consider throwback rules when choosing operational locations
Monitor state law changes—formula modifications are common

sections.title: Memory Aid: Apportionment Factors
sections.content:
'P.P.S. = Property, Payroll, Sales'
Traditional: Each factor weighted equally (33.3%)
Modern: 'Single Sales Factor' = Sales only (100%)

'Throwback = Throw it Back home if nowhere to go'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Don't assume all states use the same formula! Single sales factor is most common, but some states still use three-factor or variations. Also, service sourcing differs: market-based (customer location) vs. cost of performance (where work done). Know both methods.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Apportionment: It's all about the Sales!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Apportionment formulas? They can seem dry as dust. But here's the thing: states are *hungry* for tax revenue. They're constantly tweaking these formulas to get their piece of the pie. That's why the move to single sales factor apportionment has been so popular. It basically says, 'Hey, if you're selling a ton of stuff into our state, we want a bigger chunk, even if you don't have much property or payroll here.' Pay attention to the sales factor. It's the key to understanding where the money goes.

sections.title: Mixing Up 'Everywhere' and 'In-State'
sections.content: A common mistake is getting 'everywhere' and 'in-state' numbers mixed up in the apportionment formula. For example, students might accidentally use total company payroll for the denominator of the property factor. Remember, each factor compares *in-state* activity to *everywhere* activity. Double-check that you're using the correct denominator, representing the company's total activity, not just some other random number. This simple error can drastically skew the apportionment percentage and lead to significant tax liabilities. Always label your values clearly!

sections.title: Sarah's Apportionment Headache
sections.content: Sarah, a senior tax associate, is working on a multi-state return for 'Gadgets Galore,' a company selling widgets online. She notices that the state has adopted single sales factor apportionment, but the company has a significant manufacturing facility in that state. The learning point? Even with a large physical presence, the apportionment is based *solely* on sales. This could mean a surprisingly small portion of income being allocated to the state, despite the high payroll and property costs incurred there.

sections.title: Apportionment: Real-World Due Diligence
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter apportionment when preparing or reviewing multi-state corporate tax returns. Knowing the apportionment method each state uses is crucial. For example, when starting with a new client, you need to quickly assess if they're using the correct apportionment method for each state. Performing this initial due diligence can prevent penalties and interest charges and ensure the client is paying the correct amount of tax. Understanding the nuances of single sales factor vs. the three-factor formula is essential to accurately prepare these returns.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Apportionment divides business income among states by formula
• Traditional: (Property + Payroll + Sales) ÷ 3
• Most states now use single sales factor (100% sales)
• Sales sourcing: Destination for goods; market-based for services (usually)
• Throwback rules assign 'nowhere' sales to origination state
• Single sales factor benefits companies with in-state operations selling out-of-state
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. operates in three states. State A uses the traditional three-factor apportionment formula. XYZ Corp.'s property factor in State A is 20%, its payroll factor is 30%, and its sales factor is 50%. What is XYZ Corp.'s apportionment percentage for State A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The apportionment percentage is calculated as (Property% + Payroll% + Sales%) / 3. In this case, (20% + 30% + 50%) / 3 = 33.33%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: average of the three factors.

reveal.question: What type of company benefits from a state switching from a three-factor apportionment formula to a single sales factor formula?
reveal.answer: Companies with significant in-state property and payroll, but primarily selling their goods or services out-of-state, benefit from the single sales factor formula. This is because their apportionment percentage will be based solely on their in-state sales, potentially reducing their tax liability in that state.

rows.label: Factors Considered
values: Property, Payroll, Sales
Sales Only

rows.label: Complexity
values: More complex due to multiple factors
Simpler due to single factor

rows.label: Benefit to In-State Production
values: Less benefit to companies with high in-state production if sales are primarily out-of-state
Benefits companies with high in-state production but primarily out-of-state sales

rows.label: Impact on Out-of-State Sellers
values: May result in lower apportionment for companies selling into the state
May result in higher apportionment for companies selling into the state

title: Reasonable Compensation Planning
description: Navigate reasonable compensation requirements and optimize owner compensation structures

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Reasonable compensation is a critical issue for S corporations and C corporations. Too low triggers IRS recharacterization and penalties; too high wastes money on payroll taxes (S corp) or creates deduction/accumulation issues (C corp). Finding the right balance requires understanding the factors and risks.

sections.title: S Corporation Compensation Issue
sections.content: S corporation shareholder-employees must receive reasonable compensation for services. Unlike distributions, wages are subject to FICA (15.3% combined). The temptation: Minimize wages, maximize distributions. The risk: IRS recharacterizes distributions as wages, plus penalties.

sections.title: IRS Factors for Reasonable Compensation
items: Training, education, and experience
Duties and responsibilities
Time devoted to the business
Comparable pay for similar positions in similar businesses
Size and complexity of business
General economic conditions
Salary history and dividend history
Arm's length relationship test

sections.title: Example: Reasonable Compensation Analysis
sections.content:
Dr. Smith operates a solo dental practice as an S corp.
Net profit: $400,000
Role: Full-time dentist, manager, owner

Analysis:
• Comparable dentist salary: $180,000 - $220,000
• Practice management duties: $30,000 - $50,000 value
• Total reasonable range: $210,000 - $270,000

Recommendation: $240,000 salary; $160,000 distribution
• FICA savings vs all salary: ~$24,000 annually
• Within defensible range if challenged

Risky: $80,000 salary would likely be challenged.

sections.title: Red Flags for IRS Challenge
items: Wages significantly below comparable positions
Zero or minimal wages with large distributions
Wages that don't increase with business growth
All profits paid as distributions (no reasonable wage component)
Shareholder provides substantial services but minimal wage
Inconsistent treatment year-to-year

sections.title: Consequences of Unreasonably Low S Corp Wages
items: IRS recharacterizes distributions as wages
Back FICA taxes owed (both employer and employee shares)
Interest on late tax payments
Potential accuracy-related penalties (20%)
Professional liability for advisors

sections.title: C Corporation Compensation Issues
sections.content: For C corps, the issue flips: High compensation is deductible but may be challenged as excessive. IRS may recharacterize excessive compensation as dividends (not deductible, double-taxed). Also: §162(m) limits deduction for covered employees of public companies to $1M.

sections.title: C Corp Excessive Compensation
rows: Double tax: Corp tax + shareholder dividend tax

sections.title: Documentation Best Practices
items: Obtain salary surveys for comparable positions
Document owner's time, duties, and responsibilities
Prepare written compensation policy or analysis
Consider formal compensation study for high amounts
Review and update analysis annually
Keep minutes documenting board approval of compensation

sections.title: Section 199A Interaction
sections.content: For §199A, W-2 wages paid by the S corp are a key limitation on the 20% deduction. Higher wages = higher QBI deduction limit. In some cases, paying MORE wages (not less) maximizes total tax benefit. Run the numbers both ways!

sections.title: Memory Aid: Reasonable Compensation
sections.content:
'S Corp = Save by Splitting (wages + distributions)'
'C Corp = Caution on Compensation (not too high)'

Factors: 'TEDCS' = Training, Experience, Duties, Comparables, Size

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: A $0 salary for an S corp shareholder who provides substantial services is NEVER reasonable! Even minimally active owners should receive some wages. Courts have consistently upheld IRS recharacterization of distributions when shareholders take no salary despite providing services.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Reasonable Comp: The IRS's Favorite Game
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. The IRS *loves* going after S corps on reasonable compensation. Think of it like their version of whack-a-mole. You try to minimize wages, they pop up and say 'Nope, that distribution is actually wages, pay up!' I've seen owners try to get away with $40k in wages while pulling out $200k in distributions. Doesn't fly. The rule of thumb? If you're the only revenue-generating employee, your salary should be comparable to what you'd earn if you were working for someone else doing the same thing. Don't be greedy!

sections.title: Forgetting Payroll Tax Impact
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing *solely* on minimizing income tax without considering the payroll tax implications. Students often calculate the 'optimal' compensation from an income tax perspective, overlooking the combined 15.3% FICA tax. Yes, distributions avoid this, but that's where the 'reasonable' part comes in. Avoid this by building a comprehensive model that includes both income tax and payroll tax. A few extra dollars in wages might save you thousands in penalties if the IRS comes knocking. Remember, it's not just about net income, it's net *after* all taxes and compliance.

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Salary Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a software developer, incorporated her side hustle as an S corp. She paid herself $25,000 in wages and took $100,000 in distributions. The IRS flagged her return, arguing that a software developer in her area would command a $90,000 salary. The learning point? Document, document, document! Sarah needed to provide evidence supporting her lower salary, such as part-time work or lower billable rates, to justify the wage difference and avoid reclassification and penalties.

sections.title: Negotiating with Clients on Comp
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this when advising S corp owners on their annual tax planning. They'll often push for the lowest possible salary to minimize their immediate tax burden. Your job is to educate them on the risks of underreporting wages and the potential for IRS scrutiny. It's a delicate balance: you need to be tax-efficient while also ensuring they're compliant. Showing them real-world examples of IRS penalties and the cost of defending against an audit can be a powerful motivator to set a more reasonable compensation level.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• S corp shareholder-employees must receive reasonable compensation
• Wages subject to FICA; distributions are not (tax savings opportunity)
• IRS examines: Comparables, duties, time, experience, business size
• Too low: IRS recharacterizes distributions as wages + penalties
• C corp: Too high may be recharacterized as non-deductible dividend
• Document compensation analysis and keep salary surveys
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An S corporation shareholder-employee receives a distribution of $100,000 and wages of $40,000. The IRS determines that reasonable compensation for the shareholder's services should have been $80,000. What is the potential impact of the IRS's recharacterization?
options: The shareholder-employee will be subject to self-employment tax on the recharacterized $40,000.
The S corporation will be subject to penalties and interest on the unpaid FICA taxes related to the recharacterized $40,000.
The shareholder-employee will recognize additional dividend income of $40,000.
Both the S corporation and the shareholder-employee will face penalties and interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS will recharacterize $40,000 of the distribution as wages. This means the S corporation failed to withhold and remit the required FICA taxes (both the employer and employee portions) on that amount. Therefore, the S corporation will be assessed penalties and interest on the unpaid taxes. While the shareholder-employee is also impacted by the increased wage income (and potentially higher individual income tax), the direct penalty is levied against the S-Corp for failure to properly withhold and remit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which entity is responsible for paying and withholding FICA taxes.

reveal.question: What is the primary motivation for an S corporation shareholder-employee to minimize their wages and maximize distributions?
reveal.answer: The primary motivation is to avoid FICA (Social Security and Medicare) taxes, which are levied on wages but not on S corporation distributions. This can result in significant tax savings, especially for highly profitable S corporations.

sections.title: Wages vs. Distributions in S Corporations

rows.label: Subject to FICA Tax
values: Yes
No

rows.label: Deductible by S Corporation
values: Yes
No

rows.label: Considered Reasonable Compensation
values: Yes, if services performed
No, return on investment

rows.label: Impact on Shareholder Basis
values: No direct impact
Reduces basis

title: Retirement Plan Selection
description: Choose the optimal employer-sponsored retirement plan based on business and owner objectives

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Retirement plan selection significantly impacts owner tax benefits, employee retention, and compliance burden. The 'best' plan depends on owner compensation, employee count, contribution flexibility needs, and business stage. CPAs must match plan features to client goals.

sections.title: SEP IRA
items: Simplified Employee Pension—easy to establish and maintain
Employer contributes up to 25% of compensation (20% for self-employed)
Must cover ALL eligible employees at same percentage
No employee contributions (deferrals)
Deadline: Tax filing deadline including extensions

sections.title: SIMPLE IRA
items: For employers with ≤100 employees
Employee deferrals: $16,000 (2024); $19,500 catch-up (50+)
Employer: 2% non-elective OR 3% match
Less administrative burden than 401(k)
Cannot have other qualified plans
25% early withdrawal penalty if within 2 years

sections.title: Solo 401(k) (Individual 401(k))
sections.content: For self-employed with no employees (except spouse). Allows both employee deferrals ($23,000) AND employer contributions (25% of comp). Total limit: $69,000 (2024). Can have Roth option. Best for maximizing contributions with no employees.

sections.title: Example: Solo 401(k) vs SEP
sections.content:
Sarah, self-employed consultant, net SE income = $100,000

SEP IRA Maximum:
• 20% × $100,000 = $20,000

Solo 401(k) Maximum:
• Employee deferral: $23,000
• Employer contribution: 20% × $100,000 = $20,000
• Total: $43,000

Solo 401(k) allows $23,000 MORE in contributions!
At 32% bracket, additional tax savings: $7,360

sections.title: Traditional 401(k)
items: Most flexible for larger employers
Employee deferrals + employer match/profit sharing
Requires annual testing (ADP, ACP, Top-Heavy) unless Safe Harbor
Form 5500 filing required
Can have Roth 401(k) option
Vesting schedules allowed for employer contributions

sections.title: Safe Harbor 401(k)
sections.content: Avoids annual nondiscrimination testing by providing mandatory employer contribution: (1) 3% non-elective to ALL eligible employees, OR (2) 4% match formula. Safe harbor contributions must be 100% vested. Popular for owner-heavy businesses.

sections.title: Defined Benefit Plans
items: Promise specific benefit at retirement (not contribution amount)
Maximum annual benefit: $275,000 (2024)
Contributions can exceed $300,000+ depending on age and benefit
Ideal for older business owners wanting to catch up
Complex and expensive to administer
Actuarial calculations required annually

sections.title: Defined Benefit Example
sections.content:
Dr. Jones, age 58, wants to maximize retirement savings. Net income: $500,000.

401(k) Maximum: $69,000 + $7,500 catch-up = $76,500

Defined Benefit Plan:
• Target benefit at 65: $275,000/year
• Required contribution to fund benefit: ~$200,000/year
• Can be combined with 401(k) for $275,000+ total

DB plan allows $120,000+ MORE in deductible contributions!

sections.title: Plan Selection Decision Tree
rows: Older owner, need to catch up fast

sections.title: SECURE Act 2.0 Changes
items: Auto-enrollment required for new 401(k) plans (2025+)
Higher catch-up for ages 60-63: $10,000 (2025+)
RMD age increased to 73 (now), 75 (2033)
Roth employer contributions allowed
Student loan matching permitted
Part-time employee coverage rules expanded

sections.title: Memory Aid: Plan Selection
sections.content:
'SEP = Simple Employer Pays' (no deferrals)
'SIMPLE = Small employer, IMmediate, PLain, Easy'
'Solo 401(k) = SOLOpreneur Only (no employees)'
'DB = Deferred Big amounts (older owners)'

Max contributions: Solo 401k > SEP > SIMPLE (for self-employed)

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: SEP contributions for self-employed are based on NET self-employment income AFTER the SE tax deduction. The effective rate is ~20%, not 25%. Solo 401(k) employee deferrals don't have this reduction—that's why Solo 401(k) often beats SEP!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Solo 401(k): The Ultimate Side Hustle Saver
sections.content: Alright, listen up! As a CPA, I've seen countless self-employed folks leave money on the table when it comes to retirement. The Solo 401(k) is your secret weapon. Think of it this way: you're both the employee AND the employer. Max out both contributions – employee deferral and employer match. It's like getting a double discount on your future! Don't just stick to the basic IRA if you're crushing it as a freelancer. This is how you seriously supercharge your retirement savings, especially with those juicy 2024 limits.

sections.title: Forgetting the Employer Contribution Limit
sections.content: Students often focus solely on the employee deferral limit ($23,000 in 2024) for Solo 401(k)s and completely miss the employer contribution piece. They think 'Okay, I'll just max that out.' Wrong! The employer contribution, up to 25% of your compensation, is crucial for maximizing your savings. For example, if you earn $100,000, you can contribute $23,000 as the employee and $25,000 as the employer. Ignoring the employer contribution means leaving a significant portion of your potential savings untouched. Always calculate both limits!

sections.title: Sarah's Side Hustle Success Story
sections.content: Sarah, a freelance graphic designer, made $80,000 in 2024. Initially, she only contributed the maximum to a traditional IRA. Her CPA pointed out the Solo 401(k) and its potential. By contributing the maximum employee deferral ($23,000) and an employer contribution of $20,000 (25% of her compensation), she significantly boosted her retirement savings and lowered her taxable income for the year. The lesson? Don't settle for less when you can optimize your retirement plan.

sections.title: Solo 401(k)s: A Real-World Lifesaver
sections.content: In practice, you'll be recommending Solo 401(k)s to small business owners and independent contractors constantly. Knowing the rules inside and out is vital. When onboarding a new self-employed client, one of the first questions should be about their retirement savings strategy. Are they maximizing their Solo 401(k)? Are they aware of the Roth option? Being able to confidently explain the benefits and limitations of this plan will make you an invaluable asset to your clients and save them potentially thousands of dollars in taxes and future retirement income.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Solo 401(k): Best for self-employed (deferrals + employer contributions)
• SEP IRA: Simple but employer-only contributions (25%/20%)
• SIMPLE IRA: ≤100 employees, lower limits, less admin
• 401(k): Most flexible, requires testing unless Safe Harbor
• Defined Benefit: Highest contributions, complex, good for older owners
• SECURE 2.0: Auto-enrollment, higher catch-ups, Roth employer contributions
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is self-employed and operates as a sole proprietor. In 2024, she has self-employment income of $100,000. She wants to maximize her contributions to a Solo 401(k). What is the MAXIMUM total amount she can contribute to her Solo 401(k) for the year, assuming she is under age 50?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum contribution is the sum of the employee deferral and the employer contribution. The employee deferral is capped at $23,000 (for those under 50). The employer contribution is 25% of compensation, which is $25,000 (25% of $100,000). Therefore, $23,000 + $25,000 = $48,000. However, the total limit cannot exceed $69,000. Since $48,000 is less than $69,000, the total maximum contribution is $48,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the employee deferral and the employer contribution limits. What is the formula for the employer contribution?

reveal.question: What is the maximum employee deferral amount for a Solo 401(k) in 2024 for individuals under age 50?
What is the maximum total contribution (employee + employer) for a Solo 401(k) in 2024?

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is MOST suitable for a Solo 401(k)?
options: A salaried employee of a large corporation.
A self-employed consultant with one full-time employee.
A self-employed freelancer with no employees.
A partner in a large accounting firm with 50 employees.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Solo 401(k) is designed for self-employed individuals who have no employees (other than a spouse). The consultant with one employee is not eligible. The other choices represent situations where other retirement plans would be more suitable.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the key eligibility requirement: self-employment and no employees.

title: Fringe Benefits Tax Planning
description: Maximize tax-advantaged fringe benefits for owners and employees

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Fringe benefits provide tax-advantaged compensation—deductible to the employer and often excludable to the employee. However, benefits available to owner-employees vary by entity type. Understanding these differences is essential for compensation planning.

sections.title: S Corporation >2% Shareholders
sections.content: Shareholders owning >2% of S corp are treated like partners for fringe benefits. Benefits must be reported as W-2 income (subject to income tax, not FICA). Shareholder can then take self-employed health insurance deduction. More administrative complexity than C corp.

sections.title: Health Insurance Planning
rows: Corp deducts, shareholder includes in W-2

sections.title: Health Savings Accounts (HSAs)
items: Must have High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)
2024 limits: $4,150 individual / $8,300 family
Catch-up (55+): Additional $1,000
Triple tax benefit: Deduction + tax-free growth + tax-free medical withdrawals
No 'use it or lose it'—rolls over indefinitely
Employer contributions excluded from income

sections.title: Educational Assistance (§127)
items: Employer pays up to $5,250 tax-free annually
Covers tuition, fees, books, supplies
Can include student loan repayments (through 2025)
Must be written plan, available to broad class
Cannot discriminate in favor of highly compensated

sections.title: Group Term Life Insurance
sections.content: First $50,000 of employer-paid group term life is tax-free. Coverage above $50,000: imputed income using IRS Table I rates. For S corp >2% shareholders and partners, ALL employer-paid coverage is taxable (no $50k exclusion).

sections.title: De Minimis Fringe Benefits
items: Small value benefits excluded from income
Examples: Coffee, occasional meals, holiday gifts, flowers
Cash and gift cards are NEVER de minimis (always taxable)
No specific dollar threshold—facts and circumstances
Company parties, picnics generally qualify

sections.title: Working Condition Fringe Benefits
items: Items employee could deduct if paid personally
Business travel, professional subscriptions, work tools
Cell phones used for business: Excluded
Company vehicle: Personal use is taxable
Professional development and training

sections.title: Memory Aid: Fringe Benefits
sections.content:
'C Corp = Complete benefits for owners'
'S Corp >2% = Some taxable (health on W-2, SE deduction)'
'Partnership/Sole Prop = Partial benefits (SE deductions only)'

Best owner fringe benefits: C corporation > S corporation > Partnership

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: S corporation >2% shareholders don't get the $50,000 group term life exclusion! The entire benefit is taxable. Same for accident and health insurance—included in W-2. The S corp can still provide it, but it's not tax-free like for C corp owner-employees.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: S Corp Health Insurance: A Tax Pro's Headache?
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. S corps and health insurance for >2% shareholders? It's a recipe for confusion. I've seen so many folks miss the self-employed health insurance deduction because they didn't properly report the health insurance premiums as W-2 income first. Don't be that person! It's like the IRS is daring you to mess it up. So, double-check that W-2, make sure the shareholder gets the deduction, and save yourself (and your client) a headache later. Think of it as a tiny tax victory.

sections.title: Forgetting the W-2 Step: A Costly Oversight
sections.content: A common mistake is directly deducting the health insurance premiums on Schedule 1 without first including the premium amount as W-2 income. Why is this wrong? Because the IRS requires the health insurance premiums to be included in the shareholder's W-2 income, subjecting it to income tax. Then, the shareholder gets to deduct it on Schedule 1 as self-employed health insurance. Skipping the W-2 step means you are not properly accounting for the income tax impact and the IRS will likely disallow the deduction. Remember: W-2 inclusion FIRST, then Schedule 1 deduction.

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Snafu: A Learning Moment
sections.content: Sarah, the owner of a successful marketing S corp, paid $8,000 in health insurance premiums last year. Her accountant, rushing to meet the deadline, forgot to include that $8,000 as part of her W-2 income. As a result, Sarah missed out on the self-employed health insurance deduction, costing her over $2,000 in taxes. The lesson? Always double-check the W-2 for health insurance premiums paid for >2% S corp shareholders. It's a simple step with a significant impact.

sections.title: Fringe Benefits: Real-World Scenarios
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this fringe benefit scenario frequently when working with small business clients, particularly those operating as S corporations. When preparing year-end tax returns, always ask about health insurance premiums paid by the S corp on behalf of its >2% shareholders. This inquiry is crucial for accurate W-2 reporting and ensuring the shareholder receives the self-employed health insurance deduction. Document your inquiry and the client's response to protect yourself and demonstrate due diligence. Failing to do so could lead to amended returns and unhappy clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• C corps provide best fringe benefit treatment for owner-employees
• S corp >2% owners: Health insurance on W-2 + SE deduction
• HSAs: Triple tax benefit (deduction, growth, withdrawal)
• Educational assistance: $5,250/year excluded (includes loan repayment)
• Group term life: $50k excluded (not for S corp >2% or partners)
• De minimis: Small perks OK; cash/gift cards always taxable
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: S Corp Fringe Benefits: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: John owns 3% of an S corporation. The S corporation pays $8,000 for John's health insurance premiums. How is this treated for tax purposes?
options: The $8,000 is excluded from John's income and not deductible by the S corporation.
The $8,000 is included in John's W-2 income, subject to income tax, but not FICA tax. John can then deduct the health insurance premiums as a self-employed health insurance deduction.
The $8,000 is included in John's W-2 income, subject to income tax and FICA tax.
The $8,000 is deductible by the S corporation and John treats it as a distribution.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Shareholders owning more than 2% of an S corporation are treated as partners for fringe benefit purposes. The health insurance premiums are included in their W-2 income (subject to income tax but not FICA tax), and they can then deduct the premiums as a self-employed health insurance deduction. This mirrors the treatment for partners.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how partners are treated regarding guaranteed payments and health insurance.

reveal.question: How are greater than 2% S corporation shareholders treated for fringe benefit purposes?
reveal.answer: They are treated like partners for fringe benefit purposes. The value of the fringe benefit is included in their W-2 income (subject to income tax, but not FICA tax), and they may be able to deduct the expense as a self-employed deduction (e.g., self-employed health insurance deduction).

sections.title: S Corp vs. C Corp Health Insurance

rows.label: Health Insurance Premium Treatment
values: Included in W-2 (subject to income tax, not FICA). Shareholder can take self-employed health insurance deduction.
Deductible by the corporation; not included in shareholder/employee's income (assuming nondiscrimination rules are met).

rows.label: FICA Tax
values: Not subject to FICA tax at the corporate level
Not subject to FICA tax at the corporate level

rows.label: Administrative Complexity
values: More complex due to W-2 inclusion and individual deduction.
Less complex; generally a straightforward deduction for the corporation.

title: Section 199A Planning Strategies
description: Maximize the Qualified Business Income deduction through strategic planning

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Section 199A provides up to a 20% deduction on Qualified Business Income—potentially saving 7.4% in taxes (20% × 37% top rate). However, complex limitations based on income, wages, property, and business type require careful planning to maximize benefits.

sections.title: Section 199A Basics
sections.content: Section 199A allows a deduction equal to 20% of Qualified Business Income (QBI) from pass-through entities (partnerships, S corps, sole props). The deduction is taken on the individual return, subject to limitations based on taxable income, W-2 wages, and qualified property.

sections.title: 199A Calculation Framework
rows: Full wage limit OR SSTB exclusion

sections.title: W-2 Wage and Property Limitation
sections.content: For taxpayers above threshold, 199A deduction is limited to the GREATER of: (1) 50% of W-2 wages, OR (2) 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of qualified property (UBIA). This encourages paying wages and investing in property.

sections.title: Example: Wage Limitation
sections.content:
Law firm partner (MFJ, taxable income $600,000):
• QBI from firm: $400,000
• 20% of QBI: $80,000
• Share of W-2 wages: $100,000
• Share of qualified property: $50,000

Wage Limitation:
• 50% of wages: $50,000
• 25% wages + 2.5% property: $25,000 + $1,250 = $26,250
• Greater of: $50,000

Deduction: Lesser of $80,000 (20% QBI) or $50,000 (wage limit)
Actual deduction: $50,000

BUT: Law is SSTB, so at this income level, 199A = $0!

sections.title: Specified Service Trades/Businesses (SSTBs)
items: Health, law, accounting, actuarial, performing arts, consulting
Athletics, financial services, brokerage services
Any business where principal asset is employee reputation/skill
Engineering and architecture specifically EXCLUDED from SSTB
At income above threshold: NO 199A deduction for SSTBs

sections.title: SSTB Planning Strategies
items: Separate non-SSTB activities (product sales, real estate) into different entity
Manage income to stay below SSTB threshold
Maximize retirement contributions to reduce taxable income
Consider entity conversion (SSTB rules don't apply to C corps)
Review whether business truly qualifies as SSTB (narrow definitions)

sections.title: Increasing W-2 Wages
sections.content: For non-SSTB businesses above threshold, W-2 wages drive the limitation. Strategies: Pay reasonable salaries (not just distributions), consider year-end bonuses, hire W-2 employees vs contractors, time wage payments strategically.

sections.title: Qualified Property (UBIA) Planning
items: UBIA = Unadjusted Basis Immediately After Acquisition
Includes depreciable property held at year-end
Property counts for the greater of: depreciable period OR 10 years
Strategy: Acquire property to increase UBIA factor
Real estate often has significant UBIA benefits

sections.title: Example: Aggregation Benefit
sections.content:
Tom owns:
• Business A: QBI $200k, W-2 wages $20k
• Business B: QBI $100k, W-2 wages $150k

Without aggregation:
• A: 20% × $200k = $40k; 50% × $20k = $10k → Limited to $10k
• B: 20% × $100k = $20k; 50% × $150k = $75k → $20k
• Total: $30k

With aggregation:
• Combined QBI: $300k → 20% = $60k
• Combined wages: $170k → 50% = $85k
• Deduction: $60k (wage limit doesn't bind)
• Savings: $30k additional deduction!

sections.title: Real Estate Safe Harbor
items: Rev. Proc. 2019-38 provides safe harbor for rental real estate
250+ hours of rental services per year
Separate books and records
Must be 'trade or business' not investment
Triple-net leases generally don't qualify
Safe harbor makes rental QBI eligible for 199A

sections.title: Memory Aid: 199A Limits
sections.content:
'Below threshold = No Sweat (just 20%)'
'Above threshold = WAGE or SSTB trouble'

Wage limit: 50% W-2 OR (25% W-2 + 2.5% property)

'SSTB = Sorry, Specialized Trades Blocked' above threshold

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Engineering and architecture are specifically EXCLUDED from SSTB definition—they can get 199A above threshold (subject to wage limits). Also, 'consulting' as SSTB means consulting in the SSTB fields listed, not all consulting. A management consultant is SSTB; an IT consultant may not be.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: 199A: It's All About the Wages (and Stuff)
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Section 199A is like a choose-your-own-adventure novel, but with taxes. Above that taxable income threshold? Buckle up! The limitation based on W-2 wages and qualified property (UBIA) is where the magic (and the frustration) happens. It's not just about QBI anymore; it's about how much you're paying your employees and how much stuff you own. Think of it as the IRS saying, 'We'll give you a break, but you gotta invest in your business!' Don't just focus on maximizing QBI, optimize W-2 wages and qualified property too!

sections.title: QBI Deduction: Threshold Amnesia
sections.content: A common mistake is forgetting the taxable income thresholds. Students will calculate the 20% QBI deduction without considering if the taxpayer is even *eligible* for the full deduction. Remember, below the threshold, the deduction is generally allowed in full. Above the threshold, the W-2 wage and qualified property limitations kick in. Always start by determining if the taxpayer's income is above or below the threshold because it dictates the entire calculation process. Check your facts!

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah owns a successful consulting S corp. Her QBI is $300,000, but her taxable income puts her above the threshold. She only paid herself a small salary, resulting in low W-2 wages. As a result, her 199A deduction is severely limited. The takeaway? Sarah should consider increasing her reasonable compensation to boost her W-2 wages and maximize her 199A deduction. Ignoring the wage component can cost her thousands.

sections.title: Planning for 199A's Impact
sections.content: In practice, you'll use Section 199A planning strategies when advising small business owners on entity selection and compensation planning. For example, when a client is choosing between an S corp and a partnership, the potential 199A deduction can heavily influence the decision, especially above the income threshold. Similarly, you'll regularly analyze the impact of increasing W-2 wages or investing in qualified property on a client's overall tax liability. It's not just about filing returns; it's about proactive tax planning.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• 199A provides 20% deduction on Qualified Business Income
• Below threshold: Simple 20% deduction
• Above threshold: Wage/property limit applies; SSTBs excluded
• Wage limit: Greater of 50% W-2 OR (25% W-2 + 2.5% UBIA)
• Aggregation can help share wages across related businesses
• Sunset: 199A expires after 2025 unless extended
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, a single taxpayer, owns a business taxed as a sole proprietorship. Her taxable income before the QBI deduction is $200,000. Her QBI is $150,000, and her business paid $60,000 in W-2 wages. What is the maximum Section 199A deduction Sarah can claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah's QBI deduction is limited to the lesser of 20% of QBI ($150,000 * 20% = $30,000) or 20% of taxable income ($200,000 * 20% = $40,000). Since $30,000 is less, her deduction is $30,000. The W-2 wage limitation does not apply because her taxable income is below $220,050 (single filers)
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate 20% of both QBI and taxable income and choose the smaller number. Consider the applicable threshold.

sections.title: Recall the W-2 Wage and Property Limitation

reveal.question: For taxpayers above the taxable income threshold, the Section 199A deduction is limited to the GREATER of:
reveal.answer: (1) 50% of W-2 wages, OR (2) 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of qualified property (UBIA).

sections.title: QBI Deduction: Above vs. Below Threshold

rows.label: QBI Deduction Calculation
values: Lesser of: 20% of QBI or 20% of Taxable Income
Lesser of: 20% of QBI or 20% of Taxable Income, but subject to W-2 wage and property limitation

rows.label: W-2 Wage and Property Limitation
values: Does not apply
Applies, calculated as the GREATER of: 50% of W-2 wages, OR 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of qualified property (UBIA)

title: International Tax: GILTI
description: Understand Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income rules and planning strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: GILTI ensures that US shareholders of foreign corporations pay minimum tax on foreign earnings. This anti-deferral regime was a major TCJA change, affecting multinationals and even individual owners of foreign businesses. Understanding GILTI is essential for international tax planning.

sections.title: What Is GILTI?
sections.content: Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income (GILTI) is a category of income from Controlled Foreign Corporations (CFCs) that US shareholders must include in income annually. Despite the name, it captures more than just intangible income—it's essentially a minimum tax on CFC earnings.

sections.title: GILTI Calculation Overview
rows: CFC gross income minus allocable deductions
Average adjusted basis in depreciable property

sections.title: Simplified GILTI Formula
sections.content:
GILTI = CFC Net Income - (10% × Tangible Assets)

Think of it as: Any return ABOVE 10% of tangible assets is presumed to come from intangibles and taxed currently. Heavy investment in tangible property reduces GILTI.

sections.title: Example: GILTI Calculation
sections.content:
US Parent owns 100% of Foreign Sub (CFC):
• CFC tested income: $5,000,000
• QBAI (tangible assets): $20,000,000
• DTIR: 10% × $20,000,000 = $2,000,000

GILTI = $5,000,000 - $2,000,000 = $3,000,000

US Parent includes $3,000,000 GILTI in income.

If CFC had $50M tangible assets instead:
DTIR = $5,000,000 → GILTI = $0 (no excess over 10% return)

sections.title: Corporate GILTI Benefits
sections.content: C corporations receive a 50% deduction under §250, reducing the effective rate on GILTI to 10.5% (21% × 50%). They also get foreign tax credits for 80% of foreign taxes paid. Individuals get neither benefit—creating significant disparity.

sections.title: Planning for Individual CFC Owners
items: Consider interposing C corporation between individual and CFC
Make §962 election to be taxed at corporate rates (complex)
Maximize QBAI (tangible asset investment) to reduce GILTI
Evaluate repatriation strategies
Consider check-the-box elections for foreign entities

sections.title: Section 962 Election
sections.content: Individual US shareholders can elect under §962 to be taxed on GILTI and Subpart F at corporate rates (21% vs 37%) with access to foreign tax credits. However, subsequent distributions face additional tax. Complex calculation required—model both scenarios.

sections.title: High-Tax Exclusion
items: CFC income taxed at effective rate >18.9% can be excluded from GILTI
90% of 21% corporate rate = 18.9% threshold
Must elect annually
Tested on item-by-item or QBU basis
Benefit: High-taxed foreign income escapes US GILTI tax

sections.title: GILTI Planning Strategies
rows: More tangible assets = higher DTIR = less GILTI
Reduce tested income (transfer pricing rules apply)
Treat foreign entity as disregarded or branch
CFC losses offset gains across CFCs

sections.title: Memory Aid: GILTI
sections.content:
'GILTI = Grab Income, Low-Taxed Internationally'

Formula: Income MINUS 10% of Tangible assets = GILTI

'C corps get the 250 Discount' (50% deduction under §250)
'Individuals pay the Full tab' (no deduction!)

sections.title: Exam Trap
