sections.content: GILTI taxes US shareholders—not the foreign corporation! The CFC doesn't pay GILTI tax; the US shareholder includes GILTI in their US return. Also, individual shareholders do NOT get the §250 deduction—only C corporations do. This is a major planning consideration.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: GILTI: Not as Guilty as it Sounds (Maybe)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, GILTI sounds like a crime, right? It's not *that* bad, but it's definitely something you need to understand. Think of it as the IRS saying, 'Hey, you're making a lot of money offshore, and we want a piece of the pie *now*, even if you're reinvesting it.' That 10% tangible asset return is their way of figuring out if you're really investing or just hiding profits. Don't get caught off guard by this one; it’s a big deal for many businesses.

sections.title: Tangible Assets: Not Just Desks and Chairs!
sections.content: Students often underestimate what qualifies as tangible property for the 10% calculation. It's not just your office furniture! Think about the CFC's actual business. If they own a factory, that's a HUGE tangible asset. If they lease it, it doesn't count. The key is to look beyond the balance sheet and understand the operational assets. For example, a manufacturing plant in Ireland is a tangible asset, but the Apple logo it produces isn't (that's intangible!). Getting this wrong significantly impacts your GILTI calculation.

sections.title: Sarah's GILTI Gamble
sections.content: Sarah's company, USCo, has a CFC in the Bahamas. The CFC earned $2 million, but USCo only factored in the furniture and equipment at $50,000 for the tangible asset calculation. Big mistake! The CFC actually owns a shipping port valued at $5 million. By including the shipping port, Sarah significantly reduces USCo's GILTI liability and avoids potential penalties. Remember to dig deep and consider all qualifying tangible assets!

sections.title: GILTI in the Wild: Planning is Key
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter GILTI when working with multinational corporations or high-net-worth individuals with offshore investments. Understanding GILTI allows you to proactively plan for potential tax liabilities. For instance, a client might consider increasing their tangible asset investment in their CFC to reduce GILTI exposure. This could involve purchasing equipment or real estate. The goal is to minimize the tax burden while aligning with the client's overall business strategy. It's not just about compliance; it's about strategic tax planning.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• GILTI = CFC income exceeding 10% return on tangible assets
• US shareholders include GILTI annually (anti-deferral)
• C corps: 50% deduction (§250) → 10.5% effective rate + FTC
• Individuals: No §250 deduction—full ordinary rates (37%)
• Planning: Increase QBAI, use high-tax exclusion, consider C corp ownership
• §962 election allows individuals to elect corporate rates (complex)
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A U.S. corporation owns 100% of a CFC. The CFC has net income of $1,000,000 and tangible assets with a tax basis of $2,000,000. What is the amount of GILTI that the U.S. corporation must include in its taxable income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The GILTI calculation is: CFC Net Income - (10% * Tangible Assets). In this case: $1,000,000 - (10% * $2,000,000) = $1,000,000 - $200,000 = $800,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: GILTI = CFC Net Income - (10% × Tangible Assets)

reveal.question: What type of income does GILTI primarily target and what is the purpose of the GILTI tax?
reveal.answer: GILTI targets income from Controlled Foreign Corporations (CFCs). The purpose of GILTI is to establish a minimum tax on the earnings of CFCs held by U.S. shareholders, aiming to discourage the shifting of profits to low-tax jurisdictions.

sections.title: Impact of Tangible Assets on GILTI

knowledgeCheck.question: How does a CFC's investment in tangible assets affect the amount of GILTI that a U.S. shareholder must include in income?
options: Increased investment in tangible assets increases GILTI.
Increased investment in tangible assets decreases GILTI.
Investment in tangible assets has no impact on GILTI.
Investment in tangible assets only impacts GILTI if the CFC is located in a tax haven.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A higher investment in tangible assets reduces the amount of GILTI because the calculation subtracts 10% of tangible assets from CFC net income. This means a larger base of tangible assets results in a smaller GILTI amount.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how tangible assets factor into the GILTI formula.

title: International Tax: FDII & BEAT
description: Navigate Foreign-Derived Intangible Income and Base Erosion Anti-Abuse Tax rules

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: FDII incentivizes keeping intellectual property in the US by providing preferential rates on export income. BEAT ensures large multinationals don't erode the US tax base through deductible payments to foreign affiliates. Both are key TCJA international provisions.

sections.title: FDII Overview
sections.content: Foreign-Derived Intangible Income (FDII) provides a deduction for US corporations earning income from exports (goods, services, IP). The 37.5% deduction results in an effective rate of 13.125% (vs 21% standard). Think of it as the 'carrot' to GILTI's 'stick.'

sections.title: FDII Example
sections.content:
US Tech Corp has:
• Total DEI: $100 million
• Foreign-derived DEI: $40 million (exports)
• QBAI: $200 million

DII = $100M - (10% × $200M) = $80 million
Foreign ratio = $40M / $100M = 40%
FDII = $80M × 40% = $32 million
FDII Deduction = $32M × 37.5% = $12 million

Tax savings: $12M × 21% = $2.52 million
Effective rate on FDII: 13.125% (vs 21%)

sections.title: Qualifying for FDII
items: Sale of property: For foreign use, to foreign person
Services: Provided to person outside US, or with respect to foreign property
IP licensing: Foreign use of intangibles
Must document foreign use—reliable documentation required
Related party transactions subject to additional scrutiny

sections.title: FDII vs GILTI Comparison
rows: Incentive to keep IP in US

sections.title: BEAT Overview
sections.content: Base Erosion and Anti-Abuse Tax (BEAT) is a minimum tax on large corporations making deductible payments to foreign related parties. It recalculates tax by adding back certain 'base erosion payments,' then applies a minimum rate. If BEAT exceeds regular tax, pay the excess.

sections.title: BEAT Applicability
items: Applies to corporations with gross receipts ≥$500 million (3-year avg)
Base erosion percentage ≥3% of total deductions
Banks/dealers: 2% threshold
Excludes: Cost of goods sold, qualified derivative payments
Does NOT apply to individuals, S corps, or small corporations

sections.title: BEAT Calculation
rows: Excess of BEAT tax over regular tax

sections.title: Base Erosion Payments
items: Deductible payments to foreign related parties
Includes: Royalties, interest, management fees, services
Excludes: COGS, qualified derivative payments
Acquisition of depreciable/amortizable property from related party
Reinsurance premiums to foreign affiliates

sections.title: BEAT Example
sections.content:
Big Corp (US) has:
• Taxable income: $100 million
• Base erosion payments to foreign parent: $50 million

Regular tax: $100M × 21% = $21 million

BEAT calculation:
• Modified taxable income: $100M + $50M = $150 million
• BEAT tax: $150M × 10% = $15 million
• BEAT liability: $15M - $21M = ($6M) negative → No BEAT

In this case, regular tax exceeds BEAT, so no additional tax.
But if base erosion payments were $200M, BEAT would apply.

sections.title: BEAT Planning Strategies
items: Restructure intercompany payments to qualify as COGS
Use qualified derivative payments (excluded from BEAT)
Consider US-to-US structures instead of US-to-foreign
Monitor base erosion percentage (stay below 3%)
Evaluate cost-sharing arrangements

sections.title: Memory Aid: FDII & BEAT
sections.content:
'FDII = Foreign Dollars In Incentive' (37.5% deduction for exports)
'BEAT = Base Erosion Adds Tax' (minimum tax on payments to foreign affiliates)

FDII: Carrot for keeping IP in US
BEAT: Stick for shifting profits out of US

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: FDII is only available to C corporations—NOT pass-throughs or individuals! Also, FDII and GILTI deductions share a combined limitation with taxable income. If losses reduce taxable income, the deductions may be limited. Plan for both provisions together.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: FDII & BEAT: The Global Tax Tango
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. FDII and BEAT sound like characters from a bad sci-fi movie, but they're crucial for US companies operating globally. Think of FDII as the government saying, 'Hey, export your goods and services, and we'll give you a tax break!' It's the carrot. BEAT, on the other hand, is the stick, preventing companies from dodging taxes by shifting profits overseas. Mastering these is key to keeping your clients (and yourself) out of hot water with the IRS. Don't sleep on this stuff!

sections.title: FDII vs. GILTI: Not Interchangeable!
sections.content: Students often confuse FDII with GILTI (Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income) because both deal with international tax and intangible income. However, they operate in opposite directions. FDII incentivizes exports *from* the US, while GILTI taxes income earned *abroad* by controlled foreign corporations. A common mistake is assuming that a company benefiting from GILTI deductions automatically qualifies for FDII. Remember, FDII requires actual export activity and specific documentation. Focus on where the income originates and the nature of the transaction.

sections.title: Tech Troubles: BEATing Around the Bush
sections.content: Sarah, a tax manager at a software company, noticed a significant increase in related-party payments to their Irish subsidiary for software development. While the payments seemed legitimate, Sarah realized the company might be subject to BEAT if these payments exceeded the base erosion threshold (typically $500 million in gross receipts). She needs to analyze the payments carefully to determine if they are deductible and if BEAT applies, potentially requiring an additional tax liability. This highlights the importance of understanding related-party transactions and their impact on BEAT.

sections.title: Real-World FDII: Document, Document, Document!
sections.content: In practice, you'll likely encounter FDII when working with companies that export goods, provide cross-border services, or license intellectual property to foreign entities. A critical aspect is meticulous documentation. You need to prove that the income is indeed 'foreign-derived.' This means tracking the destination of goods, the location of service recipients, and the end-use of IP. Without proper documentation, the IRS can easily disallow the FDII deduction, leading to significant tax assessments. Build a robust documentation process from the start, and you'll save yourself (and your client) a lot of headaches down the road.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• FDII: 37.5% deduction for export income → 13.125% effective rate
• FDII mirrors GILTI: Income above 10% return on QBAI
• BEAT: Minimum tax on large corps with base erosion payments
• BEAT adds back deductible payments to foreign affiliates
• BEAT rate: 10% (rising to 12.5%)
• FDII encourages US exports; BEAT discourages profit shifting
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A U.S. corporation has Foreign-Derived Intangible Income (FDII) of $1,000,000. What is the amount of the FDII deduction?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The FDII deduction is calculated as 37.5% of the FDII. Therefore, $1,000,000 * 37.5% = $375,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall the percentage used for the FDII deduction.

reveal.question: What is the effective tax rate on Foreign-Derived Intangible Income (FDII) after considering the deduction?
reveal.answer: The effective tax rate on FDII is 13.125%. This is calculated by multiplying the standard corporate tax rate of 21% by (1 - 0.375), where 0.375 is the FDII deduction percentage.  Therefore, 21% * (1 - 0.375) = 13.125%.

sections.title: FDII vs. GILTI: Carrot and Stick

rows.label: Purpose
values: Incentivizes U.S. corporations to generate income from foreign sales and services using U.S. based intellectual property.
Discourages U.S. corporations from shifting profits to low-tax foreign jurisdictions.

rows.label: Mechanism
values: Provides a deduction, reducing the effective tax rate on foreign-derived income.
Imposes a current tax on certain income earned by controlled foreign corporations (CFCs).

rows.label: Impact
values: Lowers the tax burden on export-related income.
Increases the tax burden on certain foreign income.

title: Transfer Pricing Concepts
description: Understand arm's length principles and transfer pricing rules for related party transactions

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Transfer pricing determines how multinational groups allocate income among related entities in different jurisdictions. Getting it wrong triggers IRS adjustments, double taxation, and penalties. Understanding arm's length principles is fundamental for international tax compliance.

sections.title: What Is Transfer Pricing?
sections.content: Transfer pricing refers to the prices charged between related parties for goods, services, and intangibles. Section 482 gives IRS authority to reallocate income if related party transactions don't reflect arm's length results—what unrelated parties would charge.

sections.title: The Arm's Length Standard
sections.content: Related parties must price transactions as if they were dealing with unrelated parties at arm's length. This standard, adopted globally via OECD Guidelines, prevents profit shifting by ensuring taxable income is reported where economic value is created.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Methods
rows: Compare profit margin to comparable companies

sections.title: Best Method Rule
sections.content: US regulations require the 'best method'—the method that provides the most reliable measure of arm's length result under the facts. Consider: Comparability, quality of data, reliability of assumptions. No hierarchy—choose based on circumstances.

sections.title: Example: Cost Plus Method
sections.content:
US Parent contracts with Mexican Sub to manufacture products.

Mexican Sub's costs: $1,000,000
Comparable manufacturers earn 8% cost plus margin

Arm's length price:
$1,000,000 × 1.08 = $1,080,000

US Parent should pay Mexican Sub $1,080,000
Mexican Sub reports $80,000 profit
US Parent's COGS = $1,080,000

If US Parent paid only $1,000,000 (no markup):
IRS could reallocate $80,000 to Mexican Sub
Result: US income increased, potential double taxation

sections.title: Comparability Factors
items: Functions performed: What activities does each party perform?
Assets used: Tangible, intangible, financial assets employed
Risks assumed: Market, credit, inventory, operational risks
Contractual terms: Payment terms, warranties, exclusivity
Economic circumstances: Market size, competition, stage of product cycle
Business strategies: Market penetration, capacity utilization

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
rows: Global overview of MNE, value chain, intangibles
Detailed TP analysis for local entity transactions
Allocation of income, taxes, employees by jurisdiction

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Penalties
items: 20% penalty on substantial valuation misstatement (200%+ overstatement)
40% penalty on gross valuation misstatement (400%+ overstatement)
Documentation exception: Penalties avoided with contemporaneous documentation + reasonable cause
Penalty can apply even if no tax underpayment after foreign tax credits

sections.title: Common Controlled Transactions
rows: Comparable pricing, resale margins, cost plus

sections.title: Advance Pricing Agreements (APAs)
sections.content: APAs are prospective agreements with IRS on transfer pricing methodology. Unilateral (US only), bilateral (US + treaty partner), or multilateral. Provide certainty and eliminate double taxation risk. Process takes 2-4 years; binding for agreed period (typically 5 years).

sections.title: Intangibles and DEMPE
items: DEMPE: Development, Enhancement, Maintenance, Protection, Exploitation
Legal ownership alone doesn't entitle to all intangible income
Income allocated based on DEMPE functions performed
Cost sharing: Participants share development costs and own resulting IP
Section 367(d): Outbound IP transfers trigger deemed royalty

sections.title: Memory Aid: Transfer Pricing Methods
sections.content:
'CR-C-C-P' for the 5 methods:
C = CUP (Comparable Uncontrolled Price)
R = Resale Price
C = Cost Plus
C = CPM (Comparable Profits)
P = Profit Split

'CUP of coffee CRP served with a Cost-Plus CPM Profit Split'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Transfer pricing adjustments can create DOUBLE TAXATION—IRS increases US income but foreign country already taxed it at the lower price. Competent Authority relief under tax treaties may provide relief but isn't guaranteed. Documentation is key to avoiding adjustments.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Transfer Pricing: It's All About the Arm's Length
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk turkey. Transfer pricing sounds fancy, but it's really about fairness. Think of it like this: your sibling "sells" you their old bike for $1. Totally not arm's length! The IRS (and other tax authorities) get grumpy when related companies artificially shift profits to low-tax jurisdictions. Section 482 is their weapon. So, if you see a transaction between subsidiaries that seems fishy, trust your gut. It probably is. Make sure you can justify the price with solid arm's length evidence. It will save your client (and you!) a massive headache.

sections.title: Confusing Cost-Plus with Resale Price Methods
sections.content: Students often confuse the cost-plus and resale price methods because both involve a markup. The key difference lies in what the markup is applied to. Cost-plus applies a markup to the *seller's* costs to arrive at a transfer price. Resale price method starts with the *buyer's* resale price to an unrelated party and then reduces it by a gross profit margin to determine the arm's length price for the related party transaction. Remember, cost-plus focuses on the seller's perspective, while resale price focuses on the buyer's resale margin. Choosing the wrong one can significantly distort taxable income.

sections.title: Acme Corp and the Shifting Software
sections.content: Acme Corp's US subsidiary develops cutting-edge accounting software. They license the software to their Irish subsidiary for a pittance, who then sub-licenses it to customers worldwide, booking massive profits in Ireland (a low-tax jurisdiction). The IRS audits Acme and argues that the licensing fee was far below arm's length. The learning point: Intangibles like software are prime targets for transfer pricing scrutiny. Make sure the royalty rate reflects the software's actual value and profit potential, supported by comparable transactions.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Documentation: Your Best Friend
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter transfer pricing when preparing consolidated tax returns for multinational corporations. You'll need to analyze intercompany transactions and determine if they comply with the arm's length standard. That's why mastering this now saves hours later. Strong documentation is key. A well-prepared transfer pricing study, including benchmarking analyses and economic justification for pricing, can be crucial in defending your client against a potential IRS challenge. Remember, contemporaneous documentation is always best!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Transfer pricing governs related party transaction pricing
• Arm's length standard: Price as if unrelated parties
• Five methods: CUP, Resale Price, Cost Plus, CPM, Profit Split
• Best method rule: Choose most reliable method for facts
• Documentation critical: Avoids penalties, supports position
• APAs provide prospective certainty
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Corp, a US-based company, sells goods to its subsidiary, Beta Ltd, located in a low-tax jurisdiction. To minimize its overall tax burden, Apex Corp. charges Beta Ltd. a significantly reduced price for the goods. Which of the following principles is MOST directly violated by this arrangement?
options: The Arm's Length Standard
The Best Method Rule
The Economic Substance Doctrine
The Business Purpose Doctrine
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Arm's Length Standard requires related parties to transact as if they were unrelated. By charging Beta Ltd. a reduced price to shift profits to a low-tax jurisdiction, Apex Corp. is not adhering to this standard. The Best Method Rule is relevant for determining an appropriate transfer price, but the initial violation is of the Arm's Length Standard. The Economic Substance and Business Purpose Doctrines are broader anti-abuse provisions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental principle guiding transfer pricing regulations.

reveal.question: What section of the Internal Revenue Code grants the IRS the authority to reallocate income between related parties in the context of transfer pricing?
reveal.answer: Section 482. This section is the foundation of the IRS's power to ensure that related party transactions reflect arm's length results.

rows.label: Global Adoption
values: Widely adopted and influential in shaping transfer pricing practices worldwide.
Must be followed by US taxpayers engaged in related-party transactions, and considered by other countries when dealing with US entities.

title: Business Succession Planning
description: Design tax-efficient business succession and transition strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Business succession planning addresses the inevitable transition of ownership and control. Without proper planning, transfers can trigger massive tax bills, family conflicts, and business failure. CPAs play a critical role in designing tax-efficient succession strategies.

sections.title: Succession Planning Overview
sections.content: Succession planning involves transferring ownership and control of a business to the next generation, employees, or outside buyers. Key considerations: Timing (lifetime vs death), recipients (family, employees, third parties), tax efficiency, and maintaining business continuity.

sections.title: Transfer Options
rows: Sale to family, ESOP, or third party

sections.title: Valuation Discounts
items: Lack of Control Discount: Minority interest can't control decisions
Lack of Marketability Discount: No ready market for private company stock
Combined discounts can reach 25-40%
Must be supported by qualified appraisal
IRS scrutinizes aggressive discounts
FLPs (Family Limited Partnerships) commonly used for discounts

sections.title: Example: Valuation Discount Transfer
sections.content:
Family business FMV: $10,000,000
Parent gifts 30% interest to child

Without discount:
Gift value: $10M × 30% = $3,000,000

With valuation discounts (30% combined):
Discounted value: $3M × (1 - 30%) = $2,100,000

Gift tax savings at 40% rate:
($3M - $2.1M) × 40% = $360,000

Transferred $3M of value while using only $2.1M of exemption!

sections.title: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
sections.content: GRAT freezes value for estate purposes. Grantor transfers business to trust, retains annuity for term. If business appreciates faster than IRS assumed rate (§7520 rate), excess passes to beneficiaries gift-tax-free. Zeroed-out GRATs minimize gift tax.

sections.title: GRAT Mechanics
items: Grantor transfers property to irrevocable trust
Receives fixed annuity for specified term (e.g., 2 years)
Remainder to beneficiaries (children/trusts for them)
Gift = Transfer value minus present value of annuity
'Zeroed-out' GRAT: Annuity set so gift = $0
Risk: Grantor dies during term—full value in estate

sections.title: Installment Sale to Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT)
sections.content: Grantor sells business to trust (seeded with gift) in exchange for installment note. Trust is 'defective' for income tax (grantor pays tax on trust income) but outside estate. Growth above interest rate passes estate-tax-free. Powerful for appreciating assets.

sections.title: Buy-Sell Agreements
rows: Remaining owners buy departing owner's interest
No basis step-up for remaining owners

sections.title: Life Insurance in Succession Planning
items: Fund buy-sell agreements at death
Provide liquidity for estate taxes
Key person insurance for business continuity
ILIT (Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust) keeps proceeds out of estate
Premium financing for large policies
Split-dollar arrangements with family members

sections.title: Employee Stock Ownership Plan (ESOP)
items: Tax-qualified retirement plan that invests in employer stock
Seller can defer gain with §1042 rollover (C corp only)
Company gets deduction for contributions
S corp ESOP: Ownership % is tax-exempt
Provides market for private company stock
Employee retention and motivation benefits

sections.title: Family Limited Partnerships (FLPs)
sections.content: FLPs allow gradual transfer of business interests with valuation discounts. Parents as GPs maintain control; children receive LP interests. Discounts for lack of control and marketability. IRS challenges require legitimate non-tax purposes (asset protection, family governance).

sections.title: Section 6166: Estate Tax Deferral
items: Defer estate tax on closely held business interests
Requirement: Business >35% of adjusted gross estate
Interest-only payments for 4 years
Then up to 10 annual installments of tax + interest
Special 2% interest rate on first $1.7M+ of tax
Must maintain business ownership

sections.title: Memory Aid: Succession Techniques
sections.content:
'GIFT the FLiP with a GRAT':
G = GRAT (grantor retained annuity trust)
I = IDGT (intentionally defective grantor trust)
F = Family limited partnership
T = Trust structures
FLiP = FLP (Family Limited Partnership)
GRAT = Freeze value, pass appreciation

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Valuation discounts are under constant IRS attack. 'Deathbed' FLPs formed shortly before death with no legitimate non-tax purpose have been disallowed. The FLP must be properly funded, operated, and have legitimate business reasons beyond tax savings.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Succession Planning: It's More Than Just Taxes!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Succession planning isn't *just* about minimizing estate taxes, although that's definitely important! It's about ensuring the family business doesn't implode when old man Johnson decides to finally retire to Boca. I've seen families torn apart because they didn't have a solid plan in place. We're talking sibling rivalries, lawsuits, the whole shebang. So, while we're crunching numbers, remember the human element. A well-crafted succession plan can save more than just tax dollars; it can save relationships.

sections.title: Forgetting Gift Tax Implications in Succession
sections.content: Students often overlook the gift tax implications when transferring business ownership during the owner's lifetime. They focus solely on estate tax avoidance and forget that transferring ownership interests, even at a discount, can trigger gift tax if the value exceeds the annual exclusion. For example, gifting 20% of a business valued at $5 million to a child, even with a valuation discount, could easily exceed the annual exclusion and require filing a gift tax return and potentially paying gift tax. Always remember to consider gift tax alongside estate tax in lifetime transfer strategies.

sections.title: The Smith Family Farm Succession Fiasco
sections.content: Old Man Smith wants to pass his farm to his three kids: two who actively work the land and one who's a lawyer in the city. He decides to simply divide the farm equally in his will. The problem? The city kid wants to sell his share, potentially forcing the working kids to take out a massive loan or sell off parts of the farm. A better plan would have considered buy-sell agreements or different asset allocations to reflect each child's involvement and goals.

sections.title: Succession Planning: A Constant Conversation
sections.content: In practice, business succession planning isn't a one-time event; it's an ongoing dialogue. You'll find yourself revisiting these plans every few years, especially with changes in tax laws or family dynamics. For instance, a client might initially want to leave everything to their children equally, but a divorce or change in business interest could necessitate a complete overhaul of the succession plan. Being proactive and engaging in regular reviews ensures the plan remains aligned with the client's evolving needs and objectives, minimizing potential tax liabilities and family conflicts.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Succession planning: Transfer ownership tax-efficiently over time
• Valuation discounts (LOC + LOM) can reduce transfer values 25-40%
• GRATs freeze value; excess appreciation passes tax-free
• IDGT: Sale to trust avoids gift/estate tax on growth
• Buy-sell agreements ensure orderly transition at death/disability
• §6166: Defer estate tax on closely held businesses up to 14 years

title: Equity Compensation Planning
description: Navigate stock options, restricted stock, and other equity compensation arrangements

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Equity compensation aligns employee and shareholder interests while providing tax-advantaged pay. Different vehicles (ISOs, NSOs, RSUs, restricted stock) have vastly different tax consequences. CPAs must understand timing and character of income to advise clients effectively.

sections.title: Non-Qualified Stock Options (NSOs)
items: Most common type of stock option
No tax at grant (unless readily ascertainable FMV)
Exercise: Ordinary income on spread (FMV - exercise price)
Subject to withholding and FICA
Employer gets deduction equal to employee's income
Future appreciation from exercise: Capital gain

sections.title: Example: NSO Exercise
sections.content:
Employee receives NSO: 1,000 shares, $10 exercise price
Exercises when FMV = $50
Sells later at $70

At Exercise:
• Spread: ($50 - $10) × 1,000 = $40,000
• Ordinary income: $40,000
• Tax (32% + 7.65% FICA): ~$15,860
• Basis in shares: $50 (FMV at exercise)

At Sale:
• Gain: ($70 - $50) × 1,000 = $20,000
• Character: LTCG if held >1 year from exercise
• Tax (15% LTCG): $3,000

sections.title: Incentive Stock Options (ISOs)
items: Preferential tax treatment for employees
No regular tax at grant or exercise
AMT adjustment: Spread at exercise is AMT income
Qualifying disposition: LTCG treatment on entire gain
Requirements: Hold 2+ years from grant, 1+ year from exercise
Employer gets NO deduction (trade-off for employee benefit)

sections.title: ISO Holding Periods
rows: 2+ years from grant AND 1+ year from exercise
Ordinary income on spread at exercise; rest is capital gain

sections.title: Restricted Stock
sections.content: Actual shares granted subject to vesting restrictions. Without 83(b) election: Taxed as ordinary income when restrictions lapse (at FMV). With 83(b) election: Pay tax on grant date value; future appreciation is capital gain. Risk: Forfeit and lose tax paid.

sections.title: Section 83(b) Election
items: Must file within 30 days of grant (NO EXTENSIONS)
Pay tax on current FMV (often low for startups)
Future appreciation taxed as capital gain
Risk: If forfeited, tax paid is not refundable
Best when: Low current value, high expected appreciation
Attach to return and send copy to employer

sections.title: Example: 83(b) Election
sections.content:
Founder receives 1,000,000 restricted shares at $0.01/share
4-year vesting; sells company at $10/share after 5 years

Without 83(b):
• At each vest: Ordinary income at FMV (could be $10M+)
• Tax rate: 37% + FICA = ~45%

With 83(b):
• At grant: 1,000,000 × $0.01 = $10,000 ordinary income
• Tax: ~$4,500
• At sale: ($10 - $0.01) × 1M = $9.99M LTCG
• Tax at 20%: ~$2M

Savings: Millions in taxes!

sections.title: Restricted Stock Units (RSUs)
items: Promise to deliver shares when vesting conditions met
No shares owned until vesting (no voting rights, dividends)
83(b) NOT available (no property transferred at grant)
At vesting: Ordinary income on FMV
Common at public companies (less risk than options)
Often 'sell to cover' for tax withholding

sections.title: Profits Interests (LLCs/Partnerships)
sections.content: Partnership equivalent of equity compensation. Receive interest in FUTURE profits/appreciation, not current capital. No tax at grant if properly structured. Income taxed as pass-through (may be capital gain, ordinary, or mixed). Liquidation value at grant must be $0.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Equity Compensation
sections.content:
'ISO = Income Saved (Optionally)'—No tax at exercise (but AMT!)
'NSO = Normal Stock Option'—Ordinary income at exercise
'RSU = Really Simple Unit'—Tax at vesting, no 83(b)
'83(b) = 30-day deadline, Big potential savings'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The 83(b) election has a STRICT 30-day deadline from grant date—there are NO extensions and NO relief for missing it! Also, ISOs have a $100,000 annual limit on options that can vest. Amounts over the limit are treated as NSOs. Watch both traps!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Stock Options: The Gold Rush (and the Tax Minefield)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Equity compensation sounds fancy, right? Stock options, restricted stock... it's like winning the lottery! But before you start picturing that yacht, remember Uncle Sam wants his cut. I've seen too many people get blindsided by the tax implications of incentive stock options (ISOs) triggering AMT. Suddenly that 'bonus' becomes a tax headache. So, pay attention, folks. Understanding the nuances now will save you (and your clients) a major headache later. Think of me as your tax Sherpa, guiding you through this mountain of complexity.

sections.title: ISO vs. NQSO: A Costly Mix-Up
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming all stock options are taxed the same. Students often lump Incentive Stock Options (ISOs) and Non-Qualified Stock Options (NQSOs) together. The key difference is when and how they're taxed. ISOs, if held long enough, get favorable capital gains treatment upon sale, but the bargain element (difference between market price and exercise price) is an AMT preference item. NQSOs, on the other hand, are taxed as ordinary income when exercised. Forgetting this distinction can lead to significant underpayment penalties and upset clients. Remember, ISOs: potential AMT; NQSOs: immediate income.

sections.title: Sarah's Restricted Stock Surprise
sections.content: Sarah received restricted stock units (RSUs) that vested this year. Her company withheld taxes based on the fair market value at vesting. However, Sarah forgot to consider the additional state income tax she owes. Now, she's facing a surprise tax bill in April. The lesson? Always factor in ALL applicable taxes (federal, state, and local) when planning for equity compensation, and encourage clients to adjust their withholdings accordingly. Don't just rely on the company's withholding calculations.

sections.title: Equity Comp: A Year-Round Planning Game
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter equity compensation issues primarily during tax season, but smart advisors address it year-round. During initial client onboarding, proactively ask about stock options, RSUs, and employee stock purchase plans. Then, use tax planning software to model different exercise scenarios and their potential tax consequences, especially regarding AMT. Proactive planning allows clients to make informed decisions about exercising options, selling stock, and managing their overall tax liability, leading to happier and more financially secure clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• NSOs: Ordinary income at exercise, employer deduction
• ISOs: LTCG treatment if qualified (2-year/1-year hold), but AMT issue
• Restricted stock: 83(b) election critical—30-day deadline
• RSUs: Tax at vesting; 83(b) not available
• Profits interests: Partnership equity compensation, no tax at grant
• Key dates: 30 days (83b), 2 years/1 year (ISO qualification)
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements regarding nonqualified stock options (NQSOs) granted to an employee is correct for federal income tax purposes?
options: The employee recognizes ordinary income when the option is granted if the option has a readily ascertainable fair market value.
The employer receives a tax deduction when the employee exercises the option, equal to the difference between the fair market value of the stock on the exercise date and the option's strike price.
The employee recognizes capital gain income when the option is exercised based on the difference between the fair market value of the stock and the strike price.
The employer never receives a tax deduction associated with the NQSO.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The employer gets a deduction equal to the ordinary income recognized by the employee. The employee recognizes ordinary income at exercise (not capital gain), and the employer gets a corresponding deduction. The employee recognizes ordinary income at grant only if the option has a readily ascertainable fair market value, which is rare for employee stock options.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about who recognizes income and when, and the corresponding effect on the other party.

reveal.question: List three requirements that must be met for an employee stock option to qualify as an Incentive Stock Option (ISO).
reveal.answer: 1. The option must be granted under a plan approved by the shareholders; 2. The option must be granted within 10 years of the plan's adoption or approval; 3. The option cannot be exercisable after 10 years from the date it is granted; 4. The option price cannot be less than the fair market value of the stock at the time the option is granted; 5. The option cannot be transferable other than at death; 6. The employee cannot own more than 10% of the company's stock (unless certain exceptions apply); 7. There is a $100,000 limit on the value of stock options that become first exercisable in any one year.

rows.label: Taxation at Grant (Employee)
values: Taxable if readily ascertainable FMV
Not taxable, generally

rows.label: Taxation at Exercise (Employee)
values: Ordinary Income
No tax, but may be subject to AMT

rows.label: Taxation at Sale (Employee)
values: Capital Gain (ST or LT)
Capital Gain (LT if holding period met)

rows.label: Employer Deduction
values: Yes, at employee's exercise, equal to employee's ordinary income
No deduction (unless disqualifying disposition)

title: Partnership Advanced Planning
description: Master complex partnership tax planning including special allocations and basis management

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Partnerships offer unparalleled flexibility through special allocations, basis from debt, and varied distribution rules. Mastering advanced partnership tax planning allows CPAs to structure transactions that minimize taxes while achieving clients' business objectives.

sections.title: Special Allocations (§704(b))
sections.content: Unlike S corps (pro-rata only), partnerships can specially allocate income, gain, loss, and deductions differently from ownership percentages. Allocations must have 'substantial economic effect'—they must affect dollar amounts partners receive, not just tax consequences.

sections.title: Substantial Economic Effect Requirements
items: Capital accounts maintained per §704(b) regulations
Liquidating distributions based on capital account balances
Partners with deficit capital accounts must restore deficit (DRO) OR
Qualified income offset (QIO) alternative
Allocation must be 'substantial'—not just tax-motivated

sections.title: Example: Special Allocation
sections.content:
AB Partnership: A and B each own 50%
Year 1: $100,000 income; $50,000 depreciation

Default (no special allocation):
• A: $50k income, $25k depreciation = $25k taxable
• B: $50k income, $25k depreciation = $25k taxable

Special allocation (A is in higher bracket):
• A: $50k income, $50k depreciation = $0 taxable
• B: $50k income, $0 depreciation = $50k taxable

Must have substantial economic effect (affects capital accounts and distributions). Works if A accepts reduced capital account.

sections.title: Debt Allocations
sections.content: Partners get basis from their share of partnership liabilities—a major advantage over S corps. Recourse debt: Allocated to partners who bear economic risk of loss. Nonrecourse debt: Allocated based on profit-sharing ratios (with adjustments for §704(c) and minimum gain).

sections.title: Recourse vs Nonrecourse Debt
rows: Partner with economic risk of loss
Real estate mortgage with no guarantees
Secured by real property in activity

sections.title: Section 704(c) Allocations
sections.content: When a partner contributes appreciated/depreciated property, §704(c) prevents shifting built-in gain/loss to other partners. The contributing partner must recognize the pre-contribution gain/loss. Methods: Traditional, Traditional with curative, Remedial.

sections.title: Hot Assets and §751
items: 'Hot assets': Unrealized receivables, inventory, depreciation recapture
Sale/exchange of partnership interest: Hot asset gain is ordinary income
Distribution of hot assets: May trigger ordinary income recognition
Purpose: Prevent converting ordinary income to capital gain
Carefully analyze before any partner disposition or distribution

sections.title: Section 754 Election
sections.content: §754 election allows partnership to adjust basis of assets when: (1) Partnership interest is sold/exchanged (§743(b)), or (2) Distribution to partner (§734(b)). Purpose: Align inside and outside basis. Once made, applies to all future transfers until revoked.

sections.title: §754 Example
sections.content:
ABC Partnership has $300k FMV assets; each partner's interest worth $100k.
A sells interest to D for $100k. A's basis was $60k (partnership has appreciated).

Without §754:
• D's outside basis: $100k (purchase price)
• D's inside basis: $60k (no adjustment)
• If partnership sells assets, D taxed on $40k 'phantom' gain

With §754:
• D's outside basis: $100k
• D gets $40k special basis adjustment
• D's share of gain on asset sale: $0 (basis equals FMV)

§754 prevents D from paying tax on appreciation that occurred before D bought in.

sections.title: Disguised Sale Rules (§707(a)(2))
items: Contribution followed by distribution may be recharacterized as sale
Two-year presumption: Distribution within 2 years presumed sale
Facts and circumstances test applies
Exceptions: Operating distributions, reasonable guarantees
Planning: Wait 2+ years for distributions if possible

sections.title: Guaranteed Payments
rows: Payment determined without regard to partnership income

sections.title: Memory Aid: Partnership Tax
sections.content:
'Partnerships are FLEXIBLE':
F = Flexible allocations (§704(b))
L = Liabilities add to basis (unlike S corps)
E = Economically substantial allocations required
X = eXcept for hot assets (§751 = ordinary)
I = Inside/outside basis may differ
B = Basis adjustment with §754
LE = Look at Economics, not just tax

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Special allocations that lack 'substantial economic effect' are REALLOCATED according to partners' interests in the partnership (PIP). The IRS looks at whether allocations affect actual economic outcomes, not just tax results. Tax-only allocations fail!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Partnerships: Not Your Grandpa's Tax Return
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Partnership tax can get hairy, fast. It's not like churning out 1040s. You're dealing with special allocations, disguised sales, and basis adjustments that can make your head spin. I've seen seasoned CPAs get tripped up by the 'substantial economic effect' test. Don't just gloss over it! Seriously, understand how allocations MUST affect the partners' bottom line, not just magically reduce taxes. Otherwise, the IRS will be knocking, and nobody wants that.

sections.title: Forgetting the 'Economic Effect' Part
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing only on the 'substantial' part of 'substantial economic effect' and ignoring the 'economic effect' requirement. Students often think that as long as the allocation changes someone's tax liability, it's good. Nope! The allocation *must* genuinely impact the partner's capital account and ultimate distributions. For example, allocating all depreciation to a partner who isn't bearing the economic risk of loss on the depreciated asset is a red flag. Always trace the allocation through to the partner's capital account and eventual liquidation.

sections.title: Sarah's Special Allocation Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a junior tax associate, allocated all of a partnership's research and development expenses to one partner, thinking it would maximize that partner's tax benefits. However, the partnership agreement didn't provide for a corresponding adjustment to the partner's capital account, nor did it affect the partner's share of liquidation proceeds. The IRS flagged this allocation during an audit, arguing it lacked economic effect. Sarah learned the hard way that tax benefits alone don't cut it; the allocation must have real economic consequences.

sections.title: Special Allocations in Real Estate Syndication
sections.content: You'll encounter special allocations frequently when working with real estate syndications or private equity funds. For example, in a real estate deal, one partner might contribute capital while another manages the property. The agreement might specially allocate depreciation to the capital-contributing partner and management fees to the managing partner. Understanding the 'substantial economic effect' test is crucial here. Improper allocations can lead to unexpected tax liabilities for the partners and potential legal issues for the firm. Get it right from the start!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Special allocations: Must have substantial economic effect
• Partners get basis from partnership debt (major advantage)
• Recourse debt: To partner bearing economic risk; Nonrecourse: Profit ratio
• §704(c): Prevents shifting built-in gain/loss to other partners
• §751 hot assets: Ordinary income on sale (receivables, inventory)
• §754: Basis adjustment for transfers/distributions

title: Installment Sale Planning
description: Optimize tax deferral through properly structured installment sales

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Installment sales allow sellers to defer gain recognition as payments are received, spreading tax over multiple years. This can keep taxpayers in lower brackets, manage NIIT exposure, and provide better cash flow. Understanding the rules is essential for transaction planning.

sections.title: What Is an Installment Sale?
sections.content: Under §453, if at least one payment is received after the year of sale, the seller can report gain as payments are received. Each payment consists of three components: return of basis, gain, and interest. No election required—installment method is the default.

sections.title: Installment Sale Formula
rows: Selling price - Adjusted basis - Selling expenses

sections.title: Example: Basic Installment Sale
sections.content:
Seller sells land for $100,000; basis $40,000
Terms: $20,000 down, four annual payments of $20,000 + interest

Gross Profit: $100,000 - $40,000 = $60,000
Contract Price: $100,000
Gross Profit Ratio: $60,000 ÷ $100,000 = 60%

Year 1: $20,000 payment × 60% = $12,000 gain
Years 2-5: Same—$12,000 gain each year

Total gain reported: $60,000 (same as lump sum, but spread over 5 years)
Benefit: Stay in lower bracket, manage income timing

sections.title: Properties That Cannot Use Installment Method
items: Inventory sales (dealer property)
Publicly traded securities
Depreciation recapture (§1245/§1250)—recognized in year of sale
Sales to related parties (special rules apply)
Electing out of installment method (report all gain in year of sale)

sections.title: Depreciation Recapture Rules
sections.content: Depreciation recapture (§1245, §1250, unrecaptured §1250 gain) must be recognized in the year of sale regardless of payment timing. Only the remaining gain (after recapture) qualifies for installment treatment.

sections.title: Example: Installment Sale with Recapture
sections.content:
Building sold for $500,000; adjusted basis $200,000
Accumulated depreciation: $100,000
10-year note; $50,000/year + interest

Total gain: $500,000 - $200,000 = $300,000
Recapture (unrecaptured §1250): $100,000—recognized Year 1
Remaining gain: $300,000 - $100,000 = $200,000

Gross profit ratio: $200,000 ÷ $500,000 = 40%

Year 1: $100,000 recapture + ($50,000 × 40%) = $120,000 gain
Years 2-10: $50,000 × 40% = $20,000 gain each

sections.title: Assumed Liabilities
rows: Excess treated as payment in year of sale

sections.title: Interest Requirements
items: Adequate stated interest required (AFR or higher)
If interest too low: OID rules impute interest
Unstated interest treated as interest income to seller
Interest income recognized as payments received
Buyer gets interest deduction (if allowed under §163)

sections.title: Electing Out of Installment Method
sections.content: Sellers can elect out, recognizing all gain in year of sale. Why elect out? To use capital loss carryforwards, if expecting higher future tax rates, or if gain qualifies for exclusion. Election made on timely filed return (including extensions); irrevocable without IRS consent.

sections.title: Disposition of Installment Obligation
items: Sale/exchange: Gain/loss = Amount received - Basis in note
Gift: Donor recognizes gain as if note sold for FMV
Cancellation: Treated as receipt of payment
Bequest at death: No gain recognition; heir takes installment obligation
Basis in note = Face - Unrecognized gain

sections.title: Pledge Rule
sections.content: If an installment obligation is pledged as security for a loan, the net loan proceeds are treated as a payment—triggering gain recognition. This prevents sellers from getting cash tax-free by borrowing against the note.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Installment Sales
sections.content:
'GRIP' the installment sale:
G = Gross profit ratio determines gain per payment
R = Recapture recognized immediately
I = Interest must be adequate (AFR minimum)
P = Pledge rule: Borrowing against note triggers gain

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Depreciation recapture is ALWAYS recognized in Year 1 even in an installment sale. Only the gain AFTER recapture qualifies for deferral. Don't forget to recognize the recapture amount when calculating Year 1 gain!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Installment Sales: The Tax Deferral Magic Trick
sections.content: Okay, so installment sales. Think of it as the tax code's way of saying, 'Hey, you don't have to pay all at once!' I've seen clients practically jump for joy when we structure a sale this way. Instead of a massive tax bill the year of the sale, they spread the pain (or should I say, gain!) over several years. Remember, it's the default method, which is a huge win. Just make sure you understand the basis recovery rules – that's where things can get a little hairy if you're not careful.

sections.title: Forgetting the Interest Component in Installment Sales
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing solely on the gain portion of each payment and neglecting the interest. Students often forget that each installment payment has an interest component that's taxable as ordinary income, separate from the capital gain. This oversight leads to an understated tax liability in the year the payments are received. To avoid this, meticulously calculate the interest portion using the stated interest rate (or imputed interest if none is stated) and report it correctly on Form 1040 Schedule B.

sections.title: Bob's Land Sale Blunder
sections.content: Bob sold land for $500,000 with a basis of $100,000. He received $100,000 in year one and will receive $100,000 annually for the next four years. Bob reported $80,000 of gain in year one ($100,000 payment less $20,000 basis). The correct calculation requires determining the gross profit percentage ($400,000/$500,000 = 80%). Thus, $80,000 of each $100,000 payment is gain, and $20,000 is return of basis. Bob underreported his gain because he incorrectly calculated his basis recovery.

sections.title: Installment Sales: Real-World Applications
sections.content: You'll frequently use installment sale planning when advising clients selling real estate or businesses. It's especially crucial for clients who don't need all the cash upfront. By spreading the gain, you can potentially keep them in a lower tax bracket and avoid a large tax burden in a single year. Furthermore, understanding the nuances of §453 is essential when reviewing purchase agreements to ensure the transaction qualifies for installment sale treatment and avoids inadvertent disqualification, which could trigger immediate recognition of the entire gain.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Installment method: Defer gain as payments received over time
• Gross profit ratio × payment = gain recognized per payment
• Depreciation recapture: Always recognized in year of sale
• Cannot use for: Inventory, public securities, related party (special rules)
• Pledge rule: Borrowing against note = deemed payment
• Election out: All gain in year of sale (use loss carryforwards)
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah sold land for $500,000 on December 30, Year 1. Her basis in the land was $200,000. She received a $50,000 down payment in Year 1 and will receive $100,000 (plus interest) in each of the following four years (Years 2-5). Assuming the installment method applies, what is the amount of gain Sarah should recognize in Year 1?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The gross profit is $500,000 (Sales Price) - $200,000 (Basis) = $300,000. The gross profit ratio is $300,000/$500,000 = 60%. Therefore, the gain recognized in Year 1 is $50,000 (Cash Received) * 60% = $30,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the gross profit ratio before determining the amount of gain to be recognized.

reveal.question: Is an election required to use the installment method for eligible sales?
reveal.answer: No, the installment method is the default method for reporting gains from eligible sales where at least one payment is received after the year of sale. An election *out* of the installment method is required if the taxpayer prefers to recognize the entire gain in the year of sale.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following sales is eligible to use the installment method?
options: Sale of publicly traded stock
Sale of inventory
Sale of land held as an investment
Sale of depreciable property where all depreciation recapture has been recognized in the year of sale.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sales of publicly traded stock and inventory are specifically excluded from using the installment method. While depreciation recapture is often recognized upfront, the remaining gain from the sale of depreciable property can still be reported using the installment method.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which types of property transactions are explicitly excluded from using the installment method.

title: Related Party Transactions
description: Navigate the complex tax rules governing transactions between related parties

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Related party rules prevent taxpayers from manufacturing tax benefits through transactions with family members or controlled entities. Violating these rules can result in disallowed losses, recharacterized gains, and accelerated income. CPAs must identify related party relationships before advising on transactions.

sections.title: Who Are Related Parties?
rows: Constructive ownership through family, entities, options

sections.title: Constructive Ownership (§318)
items: Family attribution: Spouse, children, grandchildren, parents
NOT siblings (unlike §267 direct relationship)
Entity-to-owner: Stock owned by partnership/corp attributed to owners
Owner-to-entity: Individual's stock attributed to controlled entity
Option attribution: Person holding option treated as owning stock
Double attribution: Some chains of attribution allowed

sections.title: Loss Disallowance (§267)
sections.content: Losses on sales/exchanges between related parties are permanently disallowed. The buyer takes a basis equal to their purchase price. However, when the buyer later sells to an unrelated party, gain can be reduced (not below zero) by the previously disallowed loss.

sections.title: Example: Related Party Loss
sections.content:
Father sells stock to Son:
Father's basis: $100,000
Sale price to Son: $60,000
Father's disallowed loss: $40,000

Son's basis: $60,000 (purchase price)

Later, Son sells to unrelated party for $150,000:
Son's realized gain: $150,000 - $60,000 = $90,000
Disallowed loss offset: $40,000
Son's recognized gain: $90,000 - $40,000 = $50,000

If Son sells for $85,000:
Realized gain: $85,000 - $60,000 = $25,000
Recognized gain: $25,000 - $25,000 = $0
(Remaining $15,000 disallowed loss is lost forever)

sections.title: Installment Sales to Related Parties (§453)
sections.content: If property is sold to a related party on installment, and the related party resells within 2 years, the remaining gain is accelerated. Purpose: Prevent using related party as conduit for immediate cash while deferring gain.

sections.title: Second Disposition Rules
items: Applies when related buyer resells within 2 years
Original seller recognizes gain to extent of resale proceeds
Exceptions: Death, involuntary conversion, same property, tax avoidance disproved
Marketable securities: First disposition treated as payment
Depreciable property: All gain treated as ordinary income

sections.title: Example: Related Party Installment Resale
sections.content:
Parent sells land to Child on installment:
Sales price: $500,000; Parent's basis: $200,000
5-year note: $100,000/year (GP ratio: 60%)

Year 1: Child pays Parent $100,000
Parent recognizes: $100,000 × 60% = $60,000

Year 2: Child resells to unrelated party for $600,000 cash
Second disposition triggers acceleration!

Parent must recognize:
Lesser of: Resale price ($600,000) or remaining payments ($400,000)
Remaining GP ratio gain: $400,000 × 60% = $240,000

Parent recognizes $240,000 gain in Year 2 (accelerated)

sections.title: Depreciable Property Sales (§1239)
sections.content: Gain on sale of depreciable property to related party is recharacterized as ordinary income (not capital gain). Prevents selling appreciated capital asset to related party who will depreciate it—converting one person's capital gain into another's ordinary deductions.

sections.title: Timing Rules (§267)
rows: Deduction deferred until included in payee's income
Watch December 31 accruals paid after 2.5 months

sections.title: Personal Use Assets (§1239)
sections.content: When a corporation sells personal-use assets (like a vacation home) to a controlling shareholder, gain is ordinary income, not capital gain. The shareholder will not depreciate personal-use property, but the conversion to ordinary income still applies.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Related Party Rules
sections.content:
'RELATED = No LOSSES Allowed':
Loss disallowance: §267 (no deduction, but buyer can offset gain)
Ordinary income: §1239 (depreciable property sales)
Second disposition: §453 (resale within 2 years accelerates gain)
Expense matching: Deduction when payee includes in income

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The disallowed loss in a related party sale is NOT added to the buyer's basis! Buyer's basis = purchase price. The disallowed loss can only offset gain when the buyer sells to an unrelated party. If the buyer sells at a loss, the original disallowed loss disappears forever.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Related Parties: Family Feuds (and Tax Implications)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Related party transactions? They're ripe for abuse. The IRS knows it, and that's why they scrutinize them like hawks. I've seen partners sell depreciated property to their S corps just to book a loss. Don't be that person! The loss disallowance rules are designed to prevent this. Think of it this way: if it sounds too good to be true when transferring assets within the family, the IRS probably thinks so too. Document, document, document! And when in doubt, ask a senior colleague.

sections.title: The Basis Boondoggle: Carryover Confusion
sections.content: Students often forget that while the *seller's* loss is disallowed, the *buyer* still takes a basis equal to their purchase price. They incorrectly assume the buyer inherits the seller's original basis, leading to incorrect gain/loss calculations when the buyer eventually sells to an unrelated party. Remember: the buyer's basis is what they paid! The disallowed loss *only* affects the amount of gain the buyer might recognize later. It doesn't change their initial basis in the property.

sections.title: Bob's Bad Bargain: A Disallowed Loss Disaster
sections.content: Bob sold his vacation cabin to his daughter, Alice, for $50,000. Bob's basis was $75,000, resulting in a $25,000 loss. Bob can't deduct this loss. Alice's basis is $50,000. Two years later, Alice sells the cabin to an unrelated party for $90,000. Her realized gain is $40,000 ($90,000 - $50,000), but she can reduce that gain by Bob's previously disallowed loss of $25,000, resulting in a taxable gain of $15,000. If Alice had only sold it for $60,000, her gain would have been $10,000 and she could only reduce it to $0.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Small Business Tax Planning
sections.content: You'll frequently encounter related party transaction issues when working with small business clients. Owners often transfer assets between themselves and their businesses (or among family members). For example, a sole proprietor might 'sell' a vehicle to their newly formed S-corp. Understanding the loss disallowance rules is crucial for accurately preparing their tax returns and advising them on the tax consequences of these transactions. Failing to properly account for these rules can lead to significant penalties and interest assessments, especially if the IRS flags the transaction as abusive.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Related parties: Family, >50% owned entities, trusts/beneficiaries
• §267 loss disallowance: Permanent; buyer's basis = purchase price
• Disallowed loss offsets buyer's gain on later sale (not below $0)
• Installment resale within 2 years: Gain accelerated
• §1239: Depreciable property gain = ordinary income
• Expense matching: Deduction when payee includes income
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John sells land with a basis of $100,000 to his wholly-owned corporation for $60,000. Later, the corporation sells the land to an unrelated party for $110,000. What is the corporation's recognized gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The initial loss of $40,000 ($100,000 - $60,000) is disallowed to John due to the related party rules. The corporation's basis in the land is its purchase price of $60,000. When the corporation sells the land for $110,000, its realized gain is $50,000 ($110,000 - $60,000). However, the previously disallowed loss of $40,000 can be used to reduce the corporation's gain, but not below zero. Therefore, the corporation's recognized gain is $10,000 ($50,000 - $40,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider the impact of the disallowed loss on the subsequent sale.

reveal.question: What happens to a loss recognized on the sale of property between related parties?
reveal.answer: The loss is permanently disallowed. The seller cannot deduct the loss on their tax return.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah sells stock with a basis of $20,000 to her brother, Tom, for $15,000. Tom later sells the stock to an unrelated party for $25,000. What is Tom's recognized gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah's loss of $5,000 ($20,000 - $15,000) is disallowed. Tom's basis in the stock is his purchase price of $15,000. When Tom sells the stock for $25,000, his realized gain is $10,000 ($25,000 - $15,000). He can reduce this gain by Sarah's previously disallowed loss of $5,000, resulting in a recognized gain of $5,000. However, if Tom had sold the stock for $18,000, his realized gain would be $3,000 ($18,000 - $15,000), and he could only offset the gain down to zero. Therefore, the gain would be $0 if it was $3,000 or less.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the buyer's basis and the limitation on using the disallowed loss.

title: Like-Kind Exchange Planning: Advanced
description: Master complex like-kind exchange structures including reverse and improvement exchanges

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Section 1031 like-kind exchanges are one of the most powerful tax deferral tools for real estate investors. Advanced structures—reverse exchanges, improvement exchanges, and drop-and-swap—allow flexibility in complex transactions. Mastering these structures can save clients millions in taxes.

sections.title: Basic §1031 Review
sections.content: Like-kind exchanges defer gain when exchanging real property held for business or investment. Since 2018, ONLY real property qualifies (personal property no longer eligible). Gain is recognized only to extent of boot received. Strict timelines: 45 days to identify, 180 days to close.

sections.title: §1031 Requirements
rows: Real property held for productive use or investment
Broad: Any real property for any real property (US only)
Must identify replacement property in writing

sections.title: Boot and Gain Recognition
sections.content: Boot is non-like-kind property received (cash, debt relief, personal property). Gain recognized = Lesser of: (1) Realized gain, or (2) Boot received. Any mortgage boot (debt relief) triggers gain unless offset by assuming new debt or adding cash.

sections.title: Example: Exchange with Boot
sections.content:
Taxpayer exchanges:
Relinquished property: FMV $800,000; Basis $300,000; Mortgage $200,000
Replacement property: FMV $700,000; Mortgage $150,000; Cash received $50,000

Realized gain: $800,000 - $300,000 = $500,000

Boot calculation:
• Cash received: $50,000
• Mortgage boot: $200,000 - $150,000 = $50,000
• Total boot: $100,000

Recognized gain: Lesser of $500,000 or $100,000 = $100,000

Basis in replacement:
$300,000 (old basis) - $50,000 (cash) - $50,000 (mortgage relief)
+ $100,000 (gain recognized) + $150,000 (new mortgage) = $450,000

sections.title: Reverse Exchanges
sections.content: Sometimes you find replacement property before selling relinquished property. In a reverse exchange, an Exchange Accommodation Titleholder (EAT) acquires and 'parks' property until the exchange can complete. Must complete within 180 days per Rev. Proc. 2000-37.

sections.title: Reverse Exchange Structure
items: EAT (Exchange Accommodation Titleholder) acquires new property
Property 'parked' under qualified exchange accommodation agreement (QEAA)
45 days to identify which property is relinquished
180 days to complete exchange (sell relinquished, transfer replacement)
EAT cannot hold property more than 180 days
More expensive due to parking costs and complexity

sections.title: Improvement Exchanges (Build-to-Suit)
sections.content: What if replacement property needs improvements to equal relinquished property value? In an improvement exchange, EAT acquires property and makes improvements during parking period. Improvements add to replacement value, reducing boot.

sections.title: Improvement Exchange Example
sections.content:
Relinquished property sold: $2,000,000
Replacement property (land + building): $1,500,000
Needed improvements: $500,000

Direct exchange: $500,000 cash boot = taxable gain

Improvement exchange:
1. QI transfers funds to EAT
2. EAT acquires replacement for $1,500,000
3. EAT uses remaining $500,000 for improvements
4. At 180 days, EAT transfers improved property (now worth $2M)
5. Full deferral—no boot!

Key: Improvements must be completed within 180 days

sections.title: Drop-and-Swap / Swap-and-Drop
rows: Partnership distributes property to partners; partners exchange individually
Partnership exchanges; then distributes replacement to partners

sections.title: Identification Rules
items: Three-property rule: Identify up to 3 properties (any value)
200% rule: Identify any number if total FMV ≤ 200% of relinquished
95% rule: If over 200%, must acquire 95% of identified value
Identification must be in writing, signed, delivered to QI
Can revoke before 45-day deadline

sections.title: Related Party Exchanges
sections.content: Exchanges with related parties require both parties to hold property for 2 years after exchange. If either party disposes within 2 years, gain is recognized. Exceptions: Death, involuntary conversion, non-tax-avoidance transactions.

sections.title: DSTs (Delaware Statutory Trusts)
items: Allows fractional ownership of larger properties
Investor receives beneficial interest in trust owning real estate
Qualifies as real property for §1031 per Rev. Rul. 2004-86
Passive investment—no management responsibilities
Popular exit strategy: 'Lazy 1031'
Strict DST rules: No new debt, no reinvestment of cash

sections.title: Memory Aid: §1031 Timing
sections.content:
'45-180 Rule':
45 days: ID (Identify) your replacement properties
180 days: DONE (Close on replacement)

Remember: 45 + 135 = 180
If you miss 45-day ID deadline, the exchange fails completely!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Since 2018 (TCJA), ONLY REAL PROPERTY qualifies for §1031! No more equipment, vehicles, or artwork exchanges. Also, real property must be within the US—foreign real property does not qualify for exchange with US property.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Like-Kind Exchanges: Ain't No Free Lunch
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Like-kind exchanges aren't some magical tax loophole. They're more like a carefully choreographed dance. You gotta know the steps: identify the replacement property within 45 days, close the deal within 180. Forget a step, and BAM! Uncle Sam's hand is out for his share. And don't even think about slipping in some personal property – since 2018, it's real estate only. So, pay attention to the details, or you'll be singing a different tune come April 15th.

sections.title: Forgetting the Boot Offset Rule
sections.content: Students frequently forget that mortgage boot (debt relief) can be offset by other boot given. They see debt relief and immediately think 'gain recognized!' But, if you give boot in the form of cash or other property *greater* than the debt relief, you might still defer the entire gain. Example: You're relieved of $50k debt, but you give $60k cash to the other party. No gain recognized! Always net boot given against boot received before calculating recognized gain.

sections.title: Bob's Reverse Exchange Blunder
sections.content: Bob, a real estate developer, wants to build condos on land he owns, but first, he needs to exchange that land for a larger parcel. He buys the larger parcel *before* selling his current land. Big mistake! A true reverse exchange requires a qualified exchange accommodation titleholder (EAT) to hold title to *either* the relinquished or replacement property. Bob's premature purchase disqualifies the exchange, triggering a taxable event. Ouch.

sections.title: Negotiating Exchange Agreements
sections.content: In practice, you'll be reviewing like-kind exchange agreements all the time, especially for clients in real estate. Understanding the nuances of boot, particularly mortgage boot, is crucial for negotiating favorable terms. Imagine representing a client selling a property with significant debt. Minimizing their recognized gain through careful structuring of the exchange and potentially contributing cash to offset debt relief is a key value-add. Master this, and you'll be a hero to your clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• §1031: Defer gain on real property exchanges (ONLY real property post-2017)
• Strict timelines: 45 days to identify, 180 days to close
• Boot (cash, debt relief) triggers gain recognition
• Reverse exchange: Park property with EAT when buying before selling
• Improvement exchange: EAT makes improvements during parking period
• DSTs: Fractional ownership qualifies as replacement property
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer A exchanges real property with an adjusted basis of $500,000 and a fair market value of $800,000 for other real property with a fair market value of $700,000 and receives $100,000 cash. What is the amount of gain recognized by Taxpayer A?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The realized gain is $300,000 ($800,000 FMV - $500,000 Basis). However, gain is recognized only to the extent of boot received, which is $100,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Gain Recognized = Lesser of (Realized Gain, Boot Received).

reveal.question: What is the significance of the 45-day rule in a like-kind exchange?
reveal.answer: The taxpayer must identify the replacement property or properties within 45 days of transferring the relinquished property. This deadline is strict and cannot be extended.

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer B exchanges real property with a fair market value of $900,000 and subject to a $200,000 mortgage. Taxpayer B's adjusted basis in the relinquished property is $400,000. Taxpayer B receives real property worth $700,000 in the exchange and the other party assumes the $200,000 mortgage. How much gain does Taxpayer B recognize?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The realized gain is $500,000 ($900,000 FMV - $400,000 Basis). The boot received is $200,000 (mortgage relief). Therefore, the recognized gain is the lesser of $500,000 and $200,000, which is $200,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Mortgage relief is treated as boot received.

title: Stock vs Asset Sales
description: Analyze the tax implications of structuring business sales as stock or asset transactions

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The decision to structure a business sale as a stock sale or asset sale has enormous tax implications for both buyer and seller. Sellers typically prefer stock sales (single level of tax); buyers prefer asset sales (stepped-up basis). Understanding both perspectives is critical for negotiating deals.

sections.title: Stock Sale Overview
sections.content: In a stock sale, shareholders sell their ownership interests directly to the buyer. The corporation continues with the same tax attributes (basis, NOLs, etc.). The buyer steps into the sellers' shoes as the new shareholder(s).

sections.title: Asset Sale Overview
sections.content: In an asset sale, the corporation sells its assets (not stock) to the buyer. The corporation recognizes gain/loss on asset sales. Proceeds are distributed to shareholders (liquidation), triggering a second tax. Buyer gets fair market value basis in assets.

sections.title: Seller's Perspective
items: Stock sale: Single capital gains tax (20% + 3.8% NIIT)
Asset sale: Corporate tax (~21%) + shareholder tax on distribution
Asset sale double tax can exceed 40% effective rate
Stock sale transfers all liabilities (including unknown)
Seller may accept lower price for stock sale (tax savings)

sections.title: Example: Stock vs Asset Sale (C Corp)
sections.content:
C Corp: Assets FMV $10M; Asset basis $4M; Stock basis $2M

Asset Sale:
• Corp gain: $10M - $4M = $6M
• Corp tax (21%): $1,260,000
• After-tax proceeds: $8,740,000
• Shareholder gain: $8.74M - $2M = $6,740,000
• Shareholder tax (23.8%): $1,604,120
• Net to shareholders: $7,135,880
• Total tax: $2,864,120 (28.6% effective rate)

Stock Sale:
• Shareholder gain: $10M - $2M = $8M
• Tax (23.8%): $1,904,000
• Net to shareholders: $8,096,000
• Tax savings: $960,120!

sections.title: Buyer's Perspective
items: Asset sale: Stepped-up basis = future depreciation/amortization deductions
Stock sale: Carryover basis—no additional deductions
Asset sale: Can allocate purchase price to maximize deductions
Asset sale: No inherited liabilities (generally)
Stock sale: Inherits all liabilities, including unknown claims
Buyer may pay premium for asset sale (tax benefits)

sections.title: S Corporation Considerations
sections.content: S corps avoid double taxation—corporate-level gain flows through to shareholders. However, if S corp was formerly a C corp, built-in gains tax may apply for 5 years after conversion. Stock vs asset difference smaller for S corps, but basis step-up still matters to buyers.

sections.title: Section 338(h)(10) Election
sections.content: This election treats a stock sale as an asset sale for tax purposes. The target is deemed to sell all assets at FMV, then liquidate. Buyer gets stepped-up basis. Available for: S corps and corporate subsidiaries. Both buyer and seller must agree to election.

sections.title: §338(h)(10) Example
sections.content:
S Corp stock sale: $10M purchase price
Asset basis: $4M; Stock basis: $2M

Without §338(h)(10):
• Shareholder gain: $10M - $2M = $8M
• Buyer's inside basis: $4M (carryover)

With §338(h)(10):
• Deemed asset sale: $10M - $4M = $6M (flows to shareholders)
• Shareholders taxed on $6M (not $8M!)
• Buyer's inside basis: $10M (stepped up)

Better for shareholders AND buyer gets step-up!

sections.title: Earnouts and Contingent Payments
items: Additional payments based on future performance
Stock sale: Installment method may apply
Asset sale: Open transaction vs closed transaction treatment
Character issues: Ordinary income vs capital gain
Non-compete covenants: Ordinary income to seller

sections.title: Memory Aid: Stock vs Asset
sections.content:
'Sellers want STOCK, Buyers want ASSETS':
STOCK = Single Tax, No step-up (bad for buyer)
ASSETS = Allocated basis, Stepped-up, Seller pays double Tax

§338(h)(10) = Best of both worlds for S corps/subsidiaries!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Don't assume double taxation always makes asset sales worse! If the corporation has NOLs or the assets have low built-in gain, the math can favor asset sales. Always calculate both scenarios. Also, §338(h)(10) is available only for S corps and subsidiary corps—not stand-alone C corps!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Been there, stressed that: Stock vs. Asset Sale Drama
