sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, stock vs. asset sales can feel like navigating a minefield. I've seen deals fall apart because someone didn't fully grasp the tax implications. The client's thinking 'easy peasy, sell the company!' but you're picturing double taxation and basis nightmares. So, pay attention! Understanding the nuances now will save you from some serious 'what if' scenarios later. You don't want to be the reason a deal craters. Trust me.

sections.title: Double Tax Tango: The Asset Sale Gotcha
sections.content: A common error is forgetting the double taxation in asset sales. Students often only consider the corporate-level gain/loss on the asset sale itself, neglecting the subsequent shareholder-level tax when the proceeds are distributed (usually via liquidation). Remember, the corporation pays tax on the asset sale, and then the shareholders pay tax on the distribution of the remaining proceeds. This is a key disadvantage compared to stock sales where only one tax event occurs at the shareholder level.

sections.title: Sarah's Acquisition Snafu: NOLs at Stake
sections.content: Sarah is advising a client selling their business. The client is leaning towards an asset sale to avoid liabilities, but the company has significant net operating losses (NOLs). Sarah needs to remember that in a stock sale, the NOLs carry over (subject to limitations), while in an asset sale, they generally disappear. Failing to consider this could cost the client a significant tax benefit, making the stock sale a surprisingly better option despite the liability concerns.

sections.title: Due Diligence Deep Dive: Asking the Right Questions
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this when performing due diligence on potential acquisitions. You'll need to analyze the target company's tax attributes, including basis in assets, NOL carryforwards, and potential liabilities. The structure of the transaction (stock vs. asset) will significantly impact the tax consequences for both the buyer and seller. Understanding this allows you to advise your client on the optimal structure and negotiate favorable terms. You'll be reviewing purchase agreements and tax returns constantly.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Stock sale: Single tax (shareholders); no basis step-up for buyer
• Asset sale: Double tax (C corp); stepped-up basis for buyer
• Sellers generally prefer stock sales; buyers prefer asset sales
• §338(h)(10): Treats stock sale as asset sale (S corps/subsidiaries)
• Purchase price allocation: §1060 residual method
• Always model both structures to find optimal solution
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Stock vs. Asset Sale - Key Differences

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding the tax implications of an asset sale versus a stock sale?
options: In an asset sale, the buyer typically inherits the seller's tax attributes, such as net operating losses (NOLs).
In a stock sale, the buyer acquires the assets at their fair market value basis.
An asset sale generally results in a single level of taxation at the shareholder level when the proceeds are distributed.
In a stock sale, the corporation retains its original tax attributes, and the buyer essentially steps into the shoes of the seller.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is (D). In a stock sale, the buyer acquires the shares of the company, and the company itself continues to exist with all its existing tax attributes (basis in assets, NOLs, etc.). The buyer takes on the position of the previous shareholder(s). (A) is incorrect because the buyer does NOT inherit tax attributes in an asset sale. (B) is incorrect because the buyer gets a FMV basis in an ASSET sale. (C) is incorrect because an asset sale has TWO levels of tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who is selling what - the shareholders or the corporation itself.

reveal.question: Describe the two potential levels of taxation in an asset sale.
reveal.answer:
1. The corporation recognizes gain or loss on the sale of its assets.
2. Shareholders recognize gain or loss when the proceeds from the asset sale are distributed to them (typically in liquidation).

sections.title: Stock Sale vs. Asset Sale - A Comparison

rows.label: Seller
values: Shareholders
Corporation

rows.label: What is Sold
values: Ownership Interests (Shares)
Assets of the Corporation

rows.label: Tax Attributes
values: Retained by Corporation
Generally Lost to the Seller

rows.label: Buyer's Basis in Assets
values: No change to existing basis within the corporation
Fair Market Value (FMV)

rows.label: Levels of Taxation
values: One (Shareholder Level)
Two (Corporate and Shareholder Levels)

title: Section 338 Elections
description: Apply Section 338 elections to optimize the tax treatment of corporate acquisitions

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Section 338 elections allow buyers to treat a stock purchase as an asset purchase for tax purposes, obtaining a stepped-up basis in target assets. Understanding when to use §338(g) vs §338(h)(10)—and when NOT to elect—is critical for M&A tax planning.

sections.title: Two Types of §338 Elections
rows: Treats stock sale as asset sale; often tax-efficient

sections.title: Section 338(g) Election
sections.content: A §338(g) election, made solely by the purchasing corporation, treats the target as selling all assets at FMV, then purchasing them as 'new target.' Rarely used because it triggers corporate-level tax WITHOUT eliminating shareholder-level tax—worst of both worlds.

sections.title: When §338(g) Makes Sense
items: Target has large NOL carryforwards (offset deemed sale gain)
Target has built-in losses (negative goodwill)
Target is foreign corporation (no US corporate tax anyway)
Buyer needs stepped-up basis regardless of seller's tax
Tax attributes more valuable than double tax cost

sections.title: Qualified Stock Purchase (QSP)
sections.content: To make any §338 election, buyer must make a 'qualified stock purchase'—acquire at least 80% of vote AND value within a 12-month period. Stock acquired from related parties, in tax-free exchanges, or previously owned doesn't count toward 80%.

sections.title: Section 338(h)(10) Election
sections.content: Available only for S corporations and corporate subsidiaries. Joint election by buyer and seller to treat stock sale as asset sale. Target is deemed to sell assets, then liquidate. Seller recognizes corporate-level gain (flows through for S corp); no shareholder-level stock gain.

sections.title: §338(h)(10) Benefits
items: Buyer: Stepped-up basis in target assets
Buyer: Amortizable goodwill (15 years)
Seller (S corp shareholders): May have LESS tax than stock sale
Single level of tax on deemed asset sale
Avoid inheriting historic tax liabilities of target

sections.title: Example: §338(h)(10) Math
sections.content:
S Corp: Stock purchase price $10M
Shareholder's stock basis: $2M
Corporation's asset basis: $6M

Without §338(h)(10):
• Shareholder gain: $10M - $2M = $8M
• Tax (23.8%): $1,904,000

With §338(h)(10):
• Deemed asset sale gain: $10M - $6M = $4M (flows through)
• Shareholder taxable gain: $4M
• Tax (23.8%): $952,000
• Basis step-up for buyer!

Seller saves $952,000 in tax AND buyer gets stepped-up basis!

sections.title: Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (ADSP)
sections.content: ADSP represents the price at which target is deemed to sell assets. ADSP = Purchase price + Liabilities + Other relevant items. This amount is allocated among assets using §1060 residual method (same as actual asset sale).

sections.title: Adjusted Grossed-Up Basis (AGUB)
sections.content: AGUB is the buyer's deemed purchase price for target's assets. AGUB = Amount paid for stock (grossed up for minority interests) + Liabilities assumed. Allocated to assets per §1060, with residual to goodwill.

sections.title: Making the Election
items: §338(g): Form 8023, filed by buyer alone within 8.5 months
§338(h)(10): Form 8023, signed by both parties
Must be made by 15th day of 9th month following acquisition
Irrevocable once made
Allocate ADSP/AGUB via §1060 and report on Form 8594

sections.title: Built-in Gains Tax Considerations
sections.content: If target S corp was formerly a C corp, the §338(h)(10) deemed sale may trigger built-in gains (BIG) tax if within 5 years of S election. BIG tax is at highest corporate rate on pre-conversion appreciation. Factor this into the election analysis.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Section 338 Elections
sections.content:
'§338(g) = Going it alone' (buyer only, usually bad)
'§338(h)(10) = Help from seller' (joint, often good for S corps)

'QSPS' = Qualified Stock Purchase required (80%+ in 12 months)

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: §338(h)(10) is NOT available for stand-alone C corporations—only S corporations and subsidiaries of C corporations. A §338(g) election on a stand-alone C corp creates TRIPLE taxation: corporate tax on deemed sale, shareholder tax on stock sale, then no seller benefit!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Section 338: The Tax Double Whammy!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Section 338(g) elections are like ordering the extra-large pizza when you're already full. You *can* do it, but you're probably going to regret it later. Seriously, it's rare. It triggers tax at the corporate level, AND the shareholders still have to pay tax on their gain. It's the tax equivalent of paying twice for the same meal. So, remember, think long and hard before even considering a regular 338(g) election. There are usually better options!

sections.title: 80% Ownership: Don't Count the Wrong Stock!
sections.content: A common mistake is including stock acquired in a tax-free reorganization or from a related party when calculating the 80% ownership requirement for a qualified stock purchase. Students often assume that *any* stock acquired counts towards the 80%. However, these types of acquisitions are specifically excluded. Remember, the 80% threshold must be met through taxable purchases within a 12-month acquisition period. Failing to account for this could lead to an invalid election and unexpected tax consequences.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: Related Party Stock
sections.content: Sarah is advising a client, BigCo, that acquired 75% of SmallCo's stock in a taxable purchase. BigCo already owned 10% of SmallCo's stock, acquired from SmallCo's CEO (Sarah's client's brother). Even though BigCo now owns 85% of SmallCo, they *cannot* make a Section 338 election because the stock acquired from the related party doesn't count towards the qualified stock purchase. Sarah needs to advise them on alternative strategies.

sections.title: Due Diligence: Spotting Section 338 Potential
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter Section 338 elections primarily during M&A due diligence. Your role will be to analyze the target company's assets and tax attributes to determine if a 338(h)(10) election (which is more common) would be beneficial. Look for situations where the target's asset basis is significantly lower than its fair market value. That's where a 338(h)(10) election might create significant tax savings for the buyer through increased depreciation deductions and reduced gains on future asset sales. Remember to also consider the impact on state taxes.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• §338(g): Buyer-only election; rarely beneficial (double tax)
• §338(h)(10): Joint election; often beneficial for S corps/subsidiaries
• QSP required: 80%+ of vote and value in 12 months
• §338(h)(10) can reduce seller's tax while giving buyer step-up
• ADSP/AGUB: Deemed sale/purchase prices allocated per §1060
• Watch built-in gains tax for former C corps electing S status
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Acquiring Corp makes a Section 338(g) election after purchasing 100% of Target Corp's stock. Which of the following best describes the tax consequences?
options: Target Corp recognizes gain or loss as if it sold all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction, and the purchasing corporation takes a cost basis in the Target Corp's assets. Target Corp's shareholders also recognize gain or loss on the sale of their stock.
Target Corp's shareholders recognize gain or loss on the sale of their stock, but Target Corp does not recognize gain or loss on its assets.

Target Corp recognizes gain or loss as if it sold all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction, and the purchasing corporation takes a cost basis in the Target Corp's assets. Target Corp's shareholders do not recognize gain or loss on the sale of their stock.
Neither Target Corp nor its shareholders recognize any gain or loss.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Section 338(g) election treats the target corporation as if it sold all of its assets at fair market value, triggering corporate-level tax. Crucially, it does NOT eliminate shareholder-level tax on the sale of stock. This 'double tax' is why the election is rarely used.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the primary reason the Section 338(g) election is generally unfavorable.

reveal.question: What are the two key requirements for a Qualified Stock Purchase (QSP) necessary to make a Section 338 election?
reveal.answer: 1. The purchasing corporation must acquire at least 80% of the target corporation's total voting power AND 80% of the total value of its stock. 2. This acquisition must occur within a 12-month acquisition period.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following acquisitions of Target Corp. stock would NOT count towards a Qualified Stock Purchase (QSP) for a Section 338 election?
options: Stock acquired from an unrelated individual in an arm's length transaction.
Stock acquired in a taxable purchase from a wholly owned subsidiary of the acquiring corporation.
Stock acquired from a related party as defined under Section 267(b) or 707(b).
Stock acquired through open market purchases.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Stock acquired from related parties (as defined under Section 267(b) or 707(b)) is specifically excluded from counting towards the 80% ownership requirement for a QSP. The other options represent valid stock acquisitions that would count towards the QSP.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the limitations on what counts towards the 80% ownership requirement.

title: Corporate Liquidations Planning
description: Understand the tax consequences of complete and partial corporate liquidations

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Corporate liquidations involve complex tax rules affecting both the corporation and its shareholders. Understanding the general rules under §331 and the parent-subsidiary exception under §332 is essential for advising clients on exit strategies and corporate restructurings.

sections.title: General Rule: §331 Liquidations
sections.content: When a corporation completely liquidates, it's treated as a sale of stock. Shareholders recognize gain or loss equal to the difference between the FMV of property received and their stock basis. The character is typically capital gain/loss (LTCG if stock held >1 year).

sections.title: Corporate Level Tax: §336
sections.content: The liquidating corporation recognizes gain/loss as if it sold all assets at FMV. This creates double taxation for C corps: corporate-level gain under §336, then shareholder-level gain under §331. Losses may be limited if property distributed to related parties.

sections.title: C Corp Liquidation Example
sections.content:
C Corp liquidates, distributes to sole shareholder:
Asset FMV: $500,000; Asset basis: $200,000
Shareholder's stock basis: $100,000

Corporate Level (§336):
• Gain: $500,000 - $200,000 = $300,000
• Tax (21%): $63,000
• Cash available: $500,000 - $63,000 = $437,000

Shareholder Level (§331):
• Amount received: $437,000
• Gain: $437,000 - $100,000 = $337,000
• Tax (23.8%): $80,206

Total tax: $143,206 (28.6% of $500,000 value)

sections.title: Parent-Subsidiary Liquidation: §332
sections.content: §332 provides nonrecognition for liquidations of 80%-owned subsidiaries. Parent receives subsidiary's assets and liabilities tax-free. Parent takes carryover basis and holding period. Subsidiary recognizes no gain/loss under §337.

sections.title: §332 Requirements
items: Parent owns 80% of vote AND 80% of value
Complete liquidation pursuant to a plan
Distribution within one taxable year, OR
Series of distributions within 3 years of plan adoption
Subsidiary must be solvent (assets > liabilities)

sections.title: §332 Example
sections.content:
Parent owns 100% of Subsidiary
Subsidiary assets: FMV $1,000,000; Basis $400,000
Parent's stock basis: $300,000

Under §332:
• Subsidiary gain: $0 (no gain recognized under §337)
• Parent gain: $0 (no gain recognized under §332)
• Parent's basis in assets: $400,000 (carryover)

Compare to taxable liquidation:
• Subsidiary tax: ($600k × 21%) = $126,000
• Parent tax: Based on value received

§332 preserves basis and defers all gain!

sections.title: Loss Limitations
items: §336(d): No loss on distributions to related parties (>50% owned)
Exception: Pro-rata distributions AND property not acquired in §351 in past 5 years
Built-in loss property: Loss limited to duplicated loss
Purpose: Prevent manufactured losses through related-party transactions

sections.title: S Corp Liquidations
sections.content: S corp liquidations avoid double taxation—corporate gain flows through to shareholders (single tax). However, watch for built-in gains tax if S corp was formerly C corp within 5 years. Shareholder also has §331 gain/loss on stock.

sections.title: Partial Liquidations
sections.content: A partial liquidation (§302(b)(4)) results in exchange treatment for non-corporate shareholders. Must be a genuine contraction of business (not just redemption from E&P). Corporate shareholders get dividend treatment. Requires distribution within taxable year of plan adoption or succeeding year.

sections.title: Planning Considerations
items: Convert C corp to S corp and wait 5 years before liquidating
Use §338(h)(10) for subsidiary sales (deemed liquidation)
Consider installment sale of assets before liquidation
Distribute appreciated property to reduce shareholder gain
Watch for PHC or accumulated earnings tax exposure
Use §332 for subsidiary consolidations

sections.title: Memory Aid: Liquidation Rules
sections.content:
'331-336-332-337':
§331: Shareholder = Sale of stock (gain/loss)
§336: Corp = Sale of assets (gain/loss)
§332: 80% Parent = No gain (carryover basis)
§337: 80% Sub = No gain (to parent)

'Regular rules: Double tax. 80% parent-sub: No tax.'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: In a §332 liquidation, minority shareholders (those who don't own 80%) are taxed under the regular §331 rules. They recognize gain/loss even though the parent doesn't. Always check ownership percentages before applying §332!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Liquidation: It's Like a Fire Sale, But With Taxes!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Corporate liquidations? They sound fancy, but it's often a company saying, 'We're done!' and selling everything off. Think of it as a giant garage sale, but Uncle Sam wants his cut. The double taxation for C corps is the kicker. The corporation gets taxed on selling its assets, then the shareholders get taxed when they receive the proceeds. It's like being taxed on your paycheck and then taxed again when you spend it. Ouch! So, plan carefully and minimize those tax bites!

sections.title: Forgetting the Corporate-Level Gain/Loss!
sections.content: Students often focus solely on the shareholder's gain or loss under Section 331 and completely forget the corporation's responsibility under Section 336! They'll calculate the shareholder's tax implications, pat themselves on the back, and boom – wrong answer. Remember, the liquidating corporation *also* recognizes gain or loss as if it sold all its assets at fair market value. This is crucial for understanding the full tax burden of a corporate liquidation. Don't just look at one side of the transaction!

sections.title: Sarah's Slippery Slope with Section 331
sections.content: Sarah, a tax associate, is assisting with the liquidation of Acme Corp. She correctly calculates the shareholder's capital gain under Section 331 but overlooks the fact that Acme Corp. held some land with a significantly appreciated value. The learning point? Always remember the corporate-level tax implications under Section 336 before finalizing the shareholder's tax return. Overlooking the corporate level can lead to significant penalties and interest for Acme.

sections.title: Real-World Liquidations: Due Diligence is Key
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter corporate liquidations when advising businesses that are restructuring, being acquired, or simply shutting down. Performing thorough due diligence on the corporation's assets is critical. You need to determine the fair market value of all assets being distributed to shareholders to properly calculate both the corporate-level gain or loss and the shareholder-level gain or loss. Neglecting this step can lead to incorrect tax filings and potential audits. Always verify, verify, verify!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• §331: Shareholders treat liquidation as stock sale (capital gain/loss)
• §336: Corp recognizes gain/loss on asset distribution
• §332: 80% parent-sub liquidation is tax-free
• §337: Subsidiary recognizes no gain to 80% parent
• Loss limitations apply to related-party distributions
• S corp liquidation: Single tax (pass-through), but watch BIG tax
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation is undergoing a complete liquidation. Shareholder John received property with a fair market value of $200,000. John's basis in his ABC stock is $50,000 and he held the shares for 3 years. What is the amount and character of John's gain or loss?
options: $150,000 Long-Term Capital Gain
$150,000 Ordinary Income
$200,000 Long-Term Capital Gain
$50,000 Long-Term Capital Gain
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The shareholder recognizes a gain or loss equal to the difference between the FMV of property received ($200,000) and their stock basis ($50,000). Thus, John's gain is $150,000. Since the stock was held for more than a year, it's a long-term capital gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the general rule for §331 liquidations and the holding period rules for capital assets.

reveal.question: In a complete corporate liquidation, how does the liquidating corporation treat the distribution of its assets?
reveal.answer: The liquidating corporation recognizes gain or loss as if it sold all its assets at fair market value (FMV).

sections.title: Corporate Level Loss Limitations in Liquidation

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation is liquidating. It distributes property with a FMV of $50,000 and a basis of $100,000 to its controlling shareholder, who is also a related party. What is the amount of loss XYZ Corporation can recognize on this distribution?
options: Depends on whether the distribution is pro rata
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Losses are generally disallowed on distributions to related parties in a complete liquidation if the distribution is not pro rata or the property was acquired by the corporation in a §351 transaction or as a contribution to capital during the 2-year period ending on the date of the distribution and the principal purpose for acquiring the property was to recognize loss by the corporation on the distribution. Since a controlling shareholder is a related party, the loss is likely disallowed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the related party rules and potential loss limitations in a corporate liquidation.

title: Gift Tax: Annual Exclusion & Lifetime
description: Master the gift tax annual exclusion and lifetime exemption for tax-efficient wealth transfers

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Gift tax planning is fundamental to wealth transfer strategies. The annual exclusion allows tax-free transfers each year, while the lifetime exemption provides a massive bucket for larger gifts. Understanding these tools helps clients transfer wealth efficiently across generations.

sections.title: Gift Tax Overview
sections.content: Gift tax applies when you transfer property for less than full consideration. The DONOR (not recipient) pays any tax due. Key exclusions: Annual exclusion ($18,000 in 2024), unlimited marital deduction, unlimited charitable deduction, qualified transfers for education/medical.

sections.title: Present Interest Requirement
sections.content: Annual exclusion only applies to gifts of present interest—the donee must have immediate right to use, possess, or enjoy the property. Future interests (remainder interests, reversions) don't qualify. Crummey powers can convert future interests to present interests for trusts.

sections.title: Example: Annual Exclusion Planning
sections.content:
Parents with 4 children and 8 grandchildren (12 donees)
Both parents gift $18,000 to each (gift-splitting)

Annual tax-free transfers:
$18,000 × 2 parents × 12 donees = $432,000/year

Over 10 years: $4,320,000 transferred gift-tax-free!

Bonus: If children are married (4 spouses = 4 more donees):
$18,000 × 2 × 16 = $576,000/year

sections.title: Lifetime Gift Tax Exemption
sections.content: The lifetime exemption ($13.61M in 2024) covers gifts exceeding the annual exclusion. It's unified with the estate tax exemption—lifetime gifts reduce the amount available at death. Use it or lose it: The exemption is scheduled to drop to ~$6M in 2026.

sections.title: Unlimited Exclusions
items: Marital deduction: Unlimited gifts to US citizen spouse
Charitable deduction: Unlimited gifts to qualified charities
Qualified tuition: Direct payment to educational institution
Qualified medical: Direct payment to medical provider
Note: Tuition/medical must be DIRECT payment, not reimbursement

sections.title: Example: Qualified Transfers
sections.content:
Grandparent wants to help grandchild:

Strategy A: Give $50,000 to grandchild
• Annual exclusion: $18,000
• Taxable gift: $32,000 (uses lifetime exemption)

Strategy B: Pay tuition directly + give $18,000
• Tuition paid direct to school: $32,000 (excluded)
• Cash gift: $18,000 (annual exclusion)
• Taxable gift: $0

Strategy B is better—no exemption used!

sections.title: Gift Tax Return (Form 709)
items: Required if: Gifts exceed annual exclusion to any donee
Required for: Gift-splitting election (even if under $18k)
Required for: Gifts of future interests (any amount)
Due date: April 15 (extends with income tax return)
No return needed for: Gifts within annual exclusion, qualified transfers, charitable gifts

sections.title: Basis Rules for Gifts
rows: Dual basis: Carryover for gain, FMV for loss
Only if FMV > donor's basis at gift

sections.title: Planning for 2026 Sunset
sections.content: The $13.61M exemption is scheduled to drop to approximately $6M in 2026 (indexed from 2017's $5M). Strategy: Use excess exemption NOW through gifts or trust funding. 'Anti-clawback' rules protect pre-2026 gifts from being taxed at death even with lower exemption.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Gift Tax Exclusions
sections.content:
'AMQQ + Present':
A = Annual exclusion ($18k/donee)
M = Marital (unlimited to US spouse)
Q = Qualified tuition (direct to school)
Q = Qualified medical (direct to provider)

Plus: PRESENT interest required for annual exclusion!

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Qualified tuition and medical payments must be made DIRECTLY to the institution/provider. Reimbursing someone for expenses they already paid is a taxable gift. Also, room and board don't qualify as 'qualified tuition'—only tuition itself.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Annual exclusion: $18,000 per donee (2024), present interest required
• Lifetime exemption: $13.61M unified with estate tax
• Unlimited exclusions: Marital, charitable, qualified tuition/medical
• Form 709 required for gifts over annual exclusion
• Donee gets carryover basis for appreciated property
• Plan for 2026 sunset: Use excess exemption now
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, which of the following gifts would qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion?
options: A gift of a remainder interest in a trust to a niece, where she will receive the assets upon the donor's death.
A gift of $20,000 cash to a friend.
A gift of $18,000 to a trust that includes a Crummey power allowing the beneficiary immediate withdrawal rights.
A gift of $10,000 to a college savings account (529 plan) for a grandchild; the donor elects to treat the gift as made ratably over five years.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is a gift of $18,000 to a trust that includes a Crummey power. The Crummey power gives the beneficiary immediate withdrawal rights, thus making it a present interest gift. The remainder interest is a future interest, so it does not qualify. The $20,000 cash gift exceeds the annual exclusion amount. While a 529 plan contribution qualifies, the election to treat it as made over five years means only 1/5 of the contribution ($2,000) would be considered for the annual exclusion in the current year.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the requirements for present vs. future interest and the 2024 annual exclusion amount.

reveal.question: Define 'present interest' in the context of gift tax.
reveal.answer: Present interest means the donee has the immediate right to use, possess, or enjoy the property. This is a crucial requirement for the annual gift tax exclusion.

rows.label: Definition
values: Donee has immediate right to use, possess, or enjoy the property.
Donee's right to use, possess, or enjoy the property is delayed to a future date.

rows.label: Annual Exclusion
values: Qualifies for the annual gift tax exclusion.
Does NOT qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion.

rows.label: Examples
values: Cash gifts, gifts to a trust with a Crummey power.
Remainder interests, reversionary interests.

title: Gift-Splitting Strategies
description: Maximize gift tax benefits through proper gift-splitting elections between spouses

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Gift-splitting allows married couples to treat a gift from one spouse as made 50% by each spouse. This doubles the annual exclusion and allows access to both spouses' lifetime exemptions without retitling assets. It's a powerful planning tool for wealth transfer.

sections.title: Gift-Splitting Basics
sections.content: Under §2513, married couples can elect to split gifts. One spouse makes the gift; both spouses consent to treat it as if each gave half. This doubles the annual exclusion ($36k vs $18k per donee) and can use both lifetime exemptions.

sections.title: Gift-Splitting Requirements
items: Must be married at time of gift
Both spouses must be US citizens or residents
Both spouses must consent to split ALL gifts for the year
Cannot split gifts of community property (already owned 50/50)
Must file Form 709 (even if no tax due)
Election is made on timely filed return

sections.title: Example: Gift-Splitting Benefit
sections.content:
Husband wants to give $50,000 to son (wife agrees to split):

Without Gift-Splitting:
• Annual exclusion: $18,000
• Taxable gift: $50,000 - $18,000 = $32,000
• Uses $32,000 of husband's lifetime exemption

With Gift-Splitting:
• Each spouse treated as giving $25,000
• Husband: $25,000 - $18,000 = $7,000 taxable
• Wife: $25,000 - $18,000 = $7,000 taxable
• Total exemption used: $14,000 (split between both)

Savings: $18,000 less lifetime exemption used!

sections.title: All-or-Nothing Rule
sections.content: Gift-splitting is ALL or NOTHING for the calendar year. If you elect to split one gift, you must split ALL gifts made by either spouse during that year. This can backfire if one spouse made gifts to someone you don't want the other spouse to 'give' to (like an ex-spouse's child).

sections.title: Consent Requirements
rows: Both sign Form 709, or separate returns with consent
Can revoke before April 15 (or extended due date)
Executor can consent on behalf of deceased
Can split gifts made while married

sections.title: When Gift-Splitting Is Valuable
items: One spouse has significant separate property
Large gift exceeds one spouse's annual exclusion
Want to use both spouses' lifetime exemptions
Funding irrevocable trust with one spouse's assets
Equalizing use of lifetime exemptions between spouses

sections.title: When Gift-Splitting Is NOT Needed
items: Community property states (already owned 50/50)
Gifts under $18,000 per donee per spouse
Both spouses have sufficient assets to gift separately
Gifts to spouse (marital deduction applies)
Concern about all-or-nothing rule consequences

sections.title: Example: Large Gift with Gift-Splitting
sections.content:
Wife gifts $1,000,000 to irrevocable trust for children.
Husband consents to gift-splitting.

Each spouse treated as giving $500,000:
• Wife's taxable gift: $500,000 - $18,000 = $482,000
• Husband's taxable gift: $500,000 - $18,000 = $482,000

Each uses $482,000 of their lifetime exemption.
Neither exceeds their individual exemption.

Without splitting:
Wife's taxable gift: $1,000,000 - $18,000 = $982,000
All from wife's exemption alone.

sections.title: Gift-Splitting and Trusts
sections.content: Gift-splitting is commonly used when funding irrevocable life insurance trusts (ILITs), GRATs, or other planning vehicles. One spouse provides the funds; both consent to split. Crummey powers still required for annual exclusion on trust gifts.

sections.title: Form 709 Requirements
items: Both spouses must file if gift-splitting elected
Can file joint Form 709 (one return, both sign)
Or each files separate Form 709 with consent box
Report all gifts by both spouses for the year
Due April 15 (extends with income tax extension)

sections.title: Memory Aid: Gift-Splitting
sections.content:
'SPLIT = Double the Benefit':
S = Spouses must both consent
P = Property from one, benefit to both
L = Lifetime exemption of both available
I = It's all-or-nothing for the year
T = Timely filed Form 709 required

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Gift-splitting creates a DEEMED gift from the non-donor spouse. If the non-donor spouse has creditor issues, this deemed gift could be attacked as a fraudulent transfer. Also, the consenting spouse is secondarily liable for gift tax on the split portion.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Gift-Splitting: The 'Two for One' Deal
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk gift-splitting. Think of it as a 'buy one, get one free' deal for gift tax! Seriously, it's one of the easiest ways for married couples to double their annual exclusion and lifetime exemption. Don't let clients miss out! I've seen too many folks leave tax savings on the table simply because they didn't understand the power of §2513. Get this right, and you'll be a hero. Mess it up, and well… let’s just say your client might not be sending you a holiday card.

sections.title: Gift-Splitting: Not Automatic!
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming gift-splitting is automatic for married couples. It's NOT! Both spouses MUST consent to split ALL gifts made during the year, even small ones. You can't pick and choose. Students often think if one spouse makes a large gift, they can automatically split it. Incorrect! A timely gift tax return (Form 709) needs to be filed, indicating the election to split gifts, and both spouses must sign consenting to the election. Failing to do so means only one spouse's exclusion and exemption are used.

sections.title: The Smiths' Generous Grandchild Gift
sections.content: John Smith gifted his grandchild $36,000 for college in 2024. He and his wife, Mary, want to use gift-splitting. If they properly elect gift-splitting, John and Mary each report a $18,000 gift. This is fully covered by their individual $18,000 annual exclusions, resulting in no taxable gift and preserving their lifetime exemptions. Without gift-splitting, John would have to use $18,000 of his lifetime exemption because the gift exceeds his annual exclusion.

sections.title: Gift-Splitting: Estate Planning's Secret Weapon
sections.content: In practice, you'll use gift-splitting as a key component of larger estate planning strategies. When working with high-net-worth married couples, proactively suggesting gift-splitting can significantly reduce potential estate tax liabilities down the road. Think about it: consistent use of annual exclusions, doubled via gift-splitting, gradually shrinks the taxable estate over time. It also helps equalize the estates of both spouses, which can be important for minimizing taxes upon the death of the second spouse. It's a long-term game, but the payoffs are huge.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Gift-splitting: Treat one spouse's gift as 50% from each
• Doubles annual exclusion: $36k per donee vs $18k
• All-or-nothing: Must split ALL gifts for the year
• Both spouses must consent and file Form 709
• Not needed for community property (already 50/50)
• Powerful for large gifts and trust funding
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold made a gift of $30,000 to his niece, Sarah. Harold's wife, Wendy, consents to split the gift. Assuming they have made no prior taxable gifts, what is the amount of Harold's taxable gift, if any, after considering the annual exclusion and gift-splitting?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With gift-splitting, the gift is treated as if half ($15,000) was made by Harold and half by Wendy. Since the annual exclusion is $18,000 per donee (in 2024), each spouse's $15,000 portion is fully covered by the annual exclusion. Therefore, Harold's taxable gift is $0.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to divide the total gift amount by two when gift-splitting.

reveal.question: What form is used to elect gift-splitting between spouses?
reveal.answer: Form 709, United States Gift (and Generation-Skipping Transfer) Tax Return. Both spouses must signify their consent on this form.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for a married couple to elect gift-splitting?
options: Both spouses must be citizens or residents of the United States at the time of the gift.
Both spouses must signify their consent to split all gifts made during the calendar year.
The spouses must be married to each other at the time of the gift.
Only the spouse making the gift needs to file a gift tax return (Form 709).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Both spouses are generally required to file Form 709 to signify their consent to gift-splitting, even if only one spouse made the gift. However, under certain circumstances, only the donor spouse needs to file. The other options are all requirements for gift-splitting.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of gift-splitting – both spouses are taking responsibility.

title: Valuation Discounts: FLPs & LLCs
description: Apply valuation discounts using family limited partnerships and LLCs for estate planning

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Valuation discounts can significantly reduce gift and estate taxes by transferring business or investment interests at values below proportionate asset values. Family Limited Partnerships (FLPs) and LLCs are the primary vehicles. However, IRS scrutiny is intense—proper structure is essential.

sections.title: Types of Valuation Discounts
rows: Can't force distributions, liquidation, or management decisions
No ready market; can't easily sell

sections.title: How Discounts Work
sections.content:
Parent owns real estate worth $10,000,000
Transfers to FLP, receives 99% LP interest + 1% GP interest
Gifts 30% LP interest to children

Without FLP:
Gift value: $10M × 30% = $3,000,000

With FLP (35% combined discount):
LP interest value: $9.9M × 30% = $2,970,000
Discounted value: $2,970,000 × (1 - 35%) = $1,930,500

Transfer tax savings at 40%:
($3M - $1.93M) × 40% = $428,000!

sections.title: Family Limited Partnership Structure
items: General Partner (GP): 1-2%, controls management and distributions
Limited Partner (LP): 98-99%, passive economic interest
Parents typically retain GP interest (control)
LP interests transferred to children/trusts over time
Partnership agreement restricts transfers and distributions

sections.title: LLC Alternative
sections.content: LLCs offer similar benefits with different structure. Manager-managed LLCs work like FLPs (manager = GP, members = LPs). Member-managed LLCs have all owners involved. Operating agreement provisions create discount-eligible restrictions.

sections.title: Legitimate Non-Tax Purposes
items: Asset protection from creditors
Centralized management of family investments
Training next generation in business/investment management
Preventing asset fragmentation
Maintaining family control of business
Facilitating family wealth governance

sections.title: IRS Attack Grounds
rows: FLP formed and transferred at death
Entity not respected for tax purposes

sections.title: Section 2036 Danger
sections.content: If the transferor retains the right to income or enjoyment, or control over who receives income, §2036 includes the transferred property in the estate at FULL VALUE. Deathbed FLPs, commingling personal expenses, and informal distributions are red flags that trigger §2036.

sections.title: Best Practices for FLP/LLC Success
items: Establish legitimate non-tax purposes (document!)
Properly fund with business/investment assets (not personal use)
Follow formalities: Meetings, capital accounts, K-1s
Don't commingle personal and partnership funds
Make proportionate distributions (or none)
Retain sufficient assets outside FLP for living expenses
Don't form on deathbed—allow time to pass
Obtain qualified appraisal

sections.title: Appraisal Requirements
sections.content: Discounts must be supported by qualified appraisals from accredited business valuators. The appraiser considers: Underlying asset values, partnership agreement restrictions, comparable transactions, and market data. Appraisals should be contemporaneous with transfers.

sections.title: Example: Properly Structured FLP
sections.content:
Good Facts:
• FLP formed 5 years before any transfers
• Holds rental properties and marketable securities
• Annual meetings, proper records, K-1s issued
• Children actively learn investment management
• No personal use assets in FLP
• Parents retain adequate assets outside FLP
• Distributions are proportionate and documented

Result: Discounts likely sustained

Bad Facts:
• FLP formed months before death
• Holds personal residence, art, jewelry
• No formalities followed
• Parents pay personal expenses from FLP

Result: §2036 inclusion—full value in estate

sections.title: Memory Aid: FLP Discounts
sections.content:
'Control + Market = Discount':
Lack of CONTROL (can't force decisions) = 15-25% off
Lack of MARKET (can't easily sell) = 15-35% off

'But watch 2036!'
Retained control/enjoyment = NO discount, FULL inclusion

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Discounts are applied MULTIPLICATIVELY, not additively. A 20% control discount and 25% marketability discount is NOT 45%. It's: (1 - 20%) × (1 - 25%) = 80% × 75% = 60%, so 40% total discount. The order doesn't matter mathematically.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: FLPs & LLCs: A Tax Pro's Therapy Session
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. FLPs and LLCs sound intimidating, right? Like some Wall Street wizardry. But really, they're just fancy wrappers around assets to minimize estate taxes. Think of it as legally gifting your kids a slightly dented version of your wealth. The 'dent' is the valuation discount! Don't get greedy though; the IRS is watching. Overdoing it is like claiming your cat as a dependent - they'll notice. It's about reasonable restrictions and planning, not outright avoidance.

sections.title: Discount Confusion: Control vs. Lack of Marketability
sections.content: Students often lump control discounts and lack of marketability discounts together, thinking they're interchangeable. Big mistake! Control discounts apply when a minority interest is transferred, reflecting the lack of ability to dictate company decisions. Lack of marketability stems from the difficulty in selling a closely held interest quickly and easily. They overlap, sure, but they are DISTINCT. Don't just blindly apply a 35% discount; justify each component separately!

sections.title: The Case of Grandpa Joe's Farm
sections.content: Grandpa Joe wants to pass his farm to his grandkids but is worried about estate taxes. His CPA suggests forming an FLP, transferring the farm assets into it, and gifting limited partnership interests over time. The operating agreement includes restrictions on transferring interests outside the family. By doing this, the value of each gifted interest can be discounted due to lack of control and marketability, significantly reducing the taxable value of Joe's estate upon his passing.

sections.title: FLPs/LLCs: Beyond the Textbook
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter FLPs and LLCs most often when working with high-net-worth individuals planning for retirement or business succession. You'll need to analyze operating agreements for restrictions, engage valuation experts to determine appropriate discount rates, and meticulously document the rationale. A poorly structured FLP or LLC can be easily challenged by the IRS, leading to costly litigation and penalties. Thorough due diligence is key to protecting your client and yourself.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Lack of control + lack of marketability can yield 25-45% discounts
• FLPs/LLCs are primary vehicles for discount planning
• Must have legitimate non-tax purposes (asset protection, management)
• §2036 is main IRS attack—retained control kills discounts
• Follow formalities: Meetings, records, proportionate distributions
• Qualified appraisal required to support discounts

title: Estate Tax: Valuation & Deductions
description: Master estate tax valuation rules and available deductions to minimize estate tax liability

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Estate tax is calculated on the fair market value of assets at death, reduced by allowable deductions. Understanding valuation methods and maximizing deductions can save significant taxes. The difference between date of death and alternate valuation can be millions of dollars.

sections.title: Gross Estate Components
items: Assets owned outright at death
Jointly held property (include portion based on contribution)
Life insurance (if incidents of ownership retained)
Retirement accounts (IRAs, 401(k)s)
Revocable trust assets
Powers of appointment (general powers)
§2036/2037/2038 transfers with retained interests

sections.title: Valuation Date Options
rows: Default; use FMV on date of death
6 months later; only if reduces estate AND tax

sections.title: Alternate Valuation Date (§2032)
items: Elect to value assets 6 months after death
Must reduce BOTH gross estate AND estate tax
If asset sold/distributed before 6 months, use that date
Election is irrevocable
Useful when market declined after death
Affects basis to heirs (lower value = lower basis)

sections.title: Example: Alternate Valuation
sections.content:
Decedent dies January 15, 2024
Stock portfolio: DOD value $5,000,000; 6-month value $4,200,000
Other assets: $10,000,000 (unchanged)

Date of Death Valuation:
Gross estate: $15,000,000
Taxable estate (after deductions): $14,500,000
Estate tax: ~$360,000

Alternate Valuation Date:
Gross estate: $14,200,000
Taxable estate: $13,700,000
Estate tax: ~$40,000

Savings: $320,000!
But: Heirs' basis in stock is $4.2M (not $5M)

sections.title: Allowable Deductions
rows: Unlimited for qualifying transfers to spouse

sections.title: Marital Deduction Requirements
items: Property must pass to surviving spouse
Spouse must be US citizen (or QDOT used)
Must be includible in decedent's gross estate
Terminable interest rule: Generally can't give spouse life estate
Exception: QTIP (Qualified Terminable Interest Property)
Unlimited amount—can reduce taxable estate to zero

sections.title: QTIP Election
sections.content: QTIP allows marital deduction for property giving spouse only income for life (terminable interest). Executor elects QTIP treatment on Form 706. Remaining property passes to others (often children from prior marriage). QTIP property is included in surviving spouse's estate.

sections.title: Special Use Valuation (§2032A)
items: Value farm/business real property at current use, not highest/best use
Can reduce value by up to $1,390,000 (2024)
Requirements: 50% of estate is farm/business; 25% is real property
Qualified heir must continue use for 10 years
Recapture tax if sold or use changes within 10 years

sections.title: Administration Expense Election
sections.content: Administration expenses can be deducted on EITHER the estate tax return (Form 706) OR the estate's income tax return (Form 1041)—but NOT both. Choose based on which provides greater tax benefit. Generally: Deduct on 706 if estate is taxable; on 1041 if estate is below exemption.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Estate Deductions
sections.content:
'FADE the estate tax':
F = Funeral expenses
A = Administration costs
D = Debts of decedent
E = Estate charitable + marital deductions

'Marital deduction = UNLIMITED (just delays tax to spouse's death)'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: Alternate valuation date affects BASIS to heirs! If you elect AVD to save estate tax, heirs receive lower stepped-up basis. This may not be optimal if heirs plan to sell assets soon—the income tax on gain could exceed estate tax savings.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Estate Tax: The Grim Reaper's Favorite Loophole?
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Estate tax is morbid, but someone's gotta deal with it. I've seen families leave millions on the table simply because they didn't understand valuation discounts. Think of it like this: the IRS wants full retail price, but you're negotiating with a desperate seller. Lack of marketability, minority interest – these are your haggling points! Don't just blindly accept the initial valuation; dig deep, find those discounts, and fight for every dollar. Your clients (and their heirs) will thank you for it.

sections.title: Confusing Date of Death Value with Alternate Valuation
sections.content: One of the biggest estate tax blunders? Using the date of death value when the alternate valuation date would result in a lower estate tax liability. Students often forget that the alternate valuation date (six months after death) is an *option*, not a requirement. If the estate's assets have decreased in value, electing the alternate valuation date can significantly reduce the taxable estate. Remember, you only use the alternate valuation if it *lowers* both the gross estate and the estate tax liability. Don't skip this critical step!

sections.title: The Case of Aunt Mildred's Art Collection
sections.content: John is preparing the estate tax return for his Aunt Mildred, who passed away leaving a substantial art collection. John initially values the art based on a quick online appraisal. However, a closer look reveals that many pieces are damaged or lack proper authentication. He hires a qualified art appraiser who provides a significantly lower valuation, reflecting the collection's true market value. The lesson? Don't rely on superficial valuations; always seek expert opinions, especially for unique or hard-to-value assets.

sections.title: Real-World Estate Tax Deductions: Beyond the Basics
sections.content: In practice, knowing the nuances of estate tax deductions is crucial. Sure, everyone knows about the marital and charitable deductions. But what about deducting unpaid property taxes, mortgages, or even funeral expenses? These seemingly small deductions can add up and significantly reduce the taxable estate. When reviewing an estate, meticulously analyze all potential deductions. Document everything thoroughly, because the IRS *will* scrutinize them. This attention to detail can save your clients thousands of dollars and demonstrate your expertise.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Gross estate: All assets at FMV including life insurance, retirement, trusts
• Deductions: Debts, expenses, marital (unlimited), charitable (unlimited)
• Alternate valuation: 6 months later if reduces estate AND tax
• §2032A: Value farm/business at current use (up to $1.39M reduction)
• QTIP: Marital deduction for terminable interest with election
• Admin expenses: Deduct on 706 or 1041, not both
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Decedent's gross estate includes real property with a fair market value of $800,000 on the date of death. An alternate valuation date was elected, and the property's value six months later was $700,000. However, the property was sold four months after the date of death for $750,000. What amount should be included in the gross estate for this property?
options: The average of $700,000 and $750,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When the alternate valuation date is elected, the property is valued at the fair market value on the date of disposition (sale, exchange, etc.) if that date is before the alternate valuation date. Here, the sale occurred at $750,000 four months after death, which is before the six-month alternate valuation date. Therefore, $750,000 is used.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the timing of the sale relative to the alternate valuation date.

reveal.question: List three common deductions from the gross estate.
reveal.answer:
1. Funeral Expenses
2. Administrative Expenses (e.g., executor fees, attorney fees)
3. Debts of the Decedent
4. Mortgages and Liens
5. Losses During Administration
6. Charitable Bequests
7. Marital Deduction

sections.title: Date of Death vs. Alternate Valuation Date

rows.label: General Rule
values: Assets are valued at their fair market value on the date of the decedent's death.
Assets are valued at their fair market value six months after the date of death, unless the asset is disposed of before that date, in which case the disposition date value is used.

rows.label: Conditions for Election
values: Always available, but not always advantageous.
The election can only be made if it decreases both the value of the gross estate AND the estate tax liability.

rows.label: Impact on Assets
values: Reflects market conditions at the time of death.
Reflects market conditions up to six months after death. Can capture post-death appreciation or depreciation.

rows.label: Impact on Income Tax Basis
values: The beneficiary's income tax basis is generally the fair market value on the date of death.
The beneficiary's income tax basis is generally the fair market value on the alternate valuation date.

title: Estate Tax: Portability Election
description: Leverage portability to transfer unused estate tax exemption between spouses

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: Portability allows the surviving spouse to use the deceased spouse's unused estate tax exemption (DSUE). This revolutionary 2010 provision simplifies estate planning for married couples, potentially doubling the combined exemption to $27.22M in 2024. But the election must be timely made!

sections.title: How Portability Works
sections.content: When the first spouse dies, any unused portion of their estate tax exemption ($13.61M in 2024) can transfer to the surviving spouse. The surviving spouse can then use both exemptions—their own plus the DSUE amount—for lifetime gifts or at death.

sections.title: Portability Requirements
items: Both spouses must be US citizens or residents
Election made on timely filed Form 706 (estate tax return)
Return required EVEN IF no tax is due
Must file within 9 months (15 months with extension)
Late election relief available in limited circumstances
Only works between spouses (not partners, not children)

sections.title: Example: Portability Benefit
sections.content:
2024: Husband dies with $5M estate, all to wife
Husband's exemption: $13.61M
Used: $0 (marital deduction covers $5M)
DSUE (unused): $13.61M

With portability election:
Wife's available exemption: $13.61M (own) + $13.61M (DSUE) = $27.22M

Wife dies in 2025 with $25M estate:
Taxable estate: $25M
Exemption available: $27.22M+
Estate tax: $0

Without portability:
Wife's exemption: $13.61M only
Taxable amount: $25M - $13.61M = $11.39M
Estate tax: ~$4.56M!

sections.title: DSUE Calculation
rows: Add any DSUE from predeceased spouse (if applicable)

sections.title: Last Deceased Spouse Rule
sections.content: DSUE only comes from the LAST deceased spouse. If surviving spouse remarries and the new spouse dies first, the new spouse's DSUE replaces the original DSUE. Strategy: Use the first DSUE for lifetime gifts before remarrying to 'lock in' that exemption.

sections.title: Form 706 Filing Requirement
items: Must file even if estate is below threshold
Due 9 months after death (6-month extension available)
Rev. Proc. 2022-32: Late election relief (within 5 years)
Complete return required—not just portability pages
Report all assets even if no tax due

sections.title: Example: Late Portability Election
sections.content:
Husband died in 2020; no Form 706 filed (estate was $2M).
Wife discovers portability in 2024 (within 5 years).

Rev. Proc. 2022-32 Relief:
• File late Form 706
• Statement: 'Filed pursuant to Rev. Proc. 2022-32'
• Elect portability
• DSUE preserved: $11.58M (2020 exemption) - $2M = $9.58M

Wife now has: Own exemption + $9.58M DSUE

Note: 5-year window; after that, relief is discretionary with IRS.

sections.title: Why Still Use Trusts?
items: GST exemption is NOT portable (trusts preserve it)
Trust appreciation escapes both estates
Asset protection for surviving spouse
State estate tax planning (states don't allow portability)
Control over ultimate beneficiaries
Protection from surviving spouse's remarriage/creditors

sections.title: Planning Considerations
sections.content: Best practice: File Form 706 for portability AND use credit shelter trusts for GST planning and growth. Don't assume portability eliminates need for trusts. Consider: Large exemption may sunset in 2026; state estate taxes; non-citizen spouse (QDOT required, portability still available).

sections.title: Memory Aid: Portability
sections.content:
'PORT the exemption to your spouse':
P = Preserve unused exemption
O = Only from LAST deceased spouse
R = Return (Form 706) REQUIRED
T = Timely file (9 months + 6 extension, or 5-year relief)

'GST is NOT portable—only estate exemption!'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: The GST (Generation-Skipping Transfer) exemption is NOT PORTABLE! Only the estate/gift tax exemption transfers to the surviving spouse. If the first spouse doesn't use their GST exemption, it's lost. This is why trusts are still important for multi-generational planning.
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Portability: It's Free Money (Almost!)
sections.content: Let's be honest, estate tax can feel like another language. But portability? That's like finding a forgotten gift card in your wallet. Seriously, it's free exemption! If spouse one doesn't use all $13.61M (in 2024), why let it go to waste? Elect portability and let spouse two use it. I've seen families leave millions on the table because they skip this step. Don't be that family! It's a simple election on Form 706, and it can save a fortune.

sections.title: Forgetting the Form 706 Election
sections.content: A huge mistake is assuming portability happens automatically. It DOESN'T! You must actively elect it by filing Form 706 (United States Estate (and Generation-Skipping Transfer) Tax Return) for the deceased spouse's estate, even if no estate tax is due. Students often think, 'No tax, no filing!' Wrong! Missing this election means the surviving spouse loses that DSUE amount forever. File the 706, even if it's just to elect portability. The filing deadline is generally nine months after the date of death (with a possible extension).

sections.title: The Case of the Overlooked Exemption
sections.content: John died in 2024, leaving his entire estate of $5 million to his wife, Mary. His executor, thinking the estate was well below the exemption amount, didn't file Form 706. Years later, Mary's estate is worth $20 million. Because John's estate didn't elect portability, Mary can only use her own exemption amount, resulting in a significant estate tax liability. Had the executor filed Form 706 for John, Mary could have used John's unused exemption, potentially saving millions in taxes.

sections.title: Portability in Your Daily Grind
sections.content: As a CPA, you'll encounter portability when advising clients on estate planning. It's crucial when one spouse has significantly less wealth than the other. For instance, when preparing a married couple's tax plan, always ask about their asset distribution and consider the potential benefits of portability. Document your discussions and recommendations clearly. Even if they initially decline, circumstances change. Having that record demonstrates due diligence and protects you from future liability. Remember to emphasize the importance of proper documentation and timely filing of Form 706 to your clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Portability: Surviving spouse inherits unused estate exemption (DSUE)
• Must file Form 706 even if no tax due
• Only from LAST deceased spouse (remarriage risk)
• 5-year late election relief available (Rev. Proc. 2022-32)
• GST exemption is NOT portable—use trusts
• Trusts still valuable for appreciation, protection, state taxes
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold died in 2024 with a gross estate of $10 million. He made no taxable gifts during his lifetime. His executor properly elected portability of Harold's deceased spousal unused exclusion (DSUE) amount. Wilma, Harold's surviving spouse, dies in 2026, having made $2 million in taxable gifts. Assuming Wilma's gross estate is $14 million and the applicable exclusion amount for 2026 is $13 million, what is the amount of Wilma's taxable estate after considering the DSUE amount?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The DSUE amount Harold's estate can transfer to Wilma is $13.61M (2024 exemption) - $0 (amount used by estate) = $13.61M. Wilma's estate is $14M + $2M gifts = $16M. She can offset this with her own $13M exemption + Harold's $13.61M exemption for a total of $26.61M. Since her estate is less than this amount, her taxable estate is $0.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the DSUE amount and consider Wilma's own applicable exclusion amount.

reveal.question: What does DSUE stand for, and what does it represent in the context of estate tax?
reveal.answer: DSUE stands for Deceased Spousal Unused Exclusion. It represents the unused portion of the first spouse's estate tax exemption that can be transferred to the surviving spouse through the portability election.

knowledgeCheck.question: Within what timeframe must the portability election be made by the executor of the deceased spouse's estate?
options: Within 6 months of the date of death.
Within 9 months of the date of death.
Within 1 year of the date of death.
Within 2 years of the date of death.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The portability election is generally made on a timely filed estate tax return (Form 706), which is due nine months after the date of death, with a six-month extension available. Therefore, it must be made within 1 year from the date of death.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the due date of the estate tax return, including possible extensions.

title: Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax
description: Navigate the complex GST tax rules for transfers to grandchildren and beyond

sections.title: Why This Matters for CPAs
sections.content: The Generation-Skipping Transfer (GST) tax prevents wealthy families from avoiding estate tax by skipping generations. Without it, grandparents could transfer wealth directly to grandchildren, avoiding estate tax at the parent's level. At a flat 40% rate on top of other transfer taxes, GST planning is essential.

sections.title: What Is the GST Tax?
sections.content: GST tax applies to transfers to 'skip persons'—people two or more generations below the transferor. The tax ensures each generation pays transfer tax. It applies IN ADDITION to gift/estate tax, creating potentially devastating combined rates without proper planning.

sections.title: Types of GST Transfers
rows: Trust interest ends, skip persons remain
Child's life estate ends, grandkids get remainder
Distribution from trust to skip person

sections.title: GST Tax Rate and Exemption
items: Flat 40% rate (no graduated brackets)
GST exemption: $13.61M (2024), same as estate exemption
GST exemption is NOT PORTABLE between spouses
Once allocated, exemption permanently shelters transfers
Exemption indexed for inflation

sections.title: Example: GST Tax Impact
sections.content:
Grandparent gifts $5M to grandchild (no GST exemption allocated):

Gift tax:
• Taxable gift: $5M - $18k (exclusion) = $4,982,000
• Uses lifetime exemption (no gift tax paid)

GST tax (direct skip):
• GST rate: 40%
• GST tax: $5M × 40% = $2,000,000
• Tax-inclusive calculation may apply

Total transfer cost: $2M+ in GST tax!

With GST exemption allocated: $0 GST tax

sections.title: GST Exemption Allocation
items: Affirmative allocation: Taxpayer elects on Form 709
Automatic allocation: Applies to direct skips and GST trusts
Deemed allocation: Can elect OUT of automatic allocation
Inclusion ratio: Determines how much of transfer is GST-exempt
Once allocated, shelters all future appreciation

sections.title: Inclusion Ratio
sections.content: The inclusion ratio determines the GST tax rate. Inclusion ratio = 1 - (GST exemption allocated ÷ Value of transfer). If fully exempt (inclusion ratio = 0), no GST tax. If no exemption (inclusion ratio = 1), full 40% rate. Partial allocations create mixed ratios.

sections.title: Dynasty Trusts
items: Irrevocable trust designed to last multiple generations
Allocate GST exemption at funding = 0% inclusion ratio
All growth sheltered from estate AND GST tax
State laws vary: Some allow perpetual trusts
Can distribute to multiple generations GST-free

sections.title: Example: Dynasty Trust
sections.content:
Grandparent funds dynasty trust with $10M, allocates GST exemption.

At funding: Inclusion ratio = 0 (fully exempt)

Year 30: Trust grows to $50M
• Distributions to grandchildren: No GST
• Distributions to great-grandchildren: No GST
• Trust assets never subject to estate tax in any generation

Compare: No trust
• $10M in grandparent's estate: Estate tax
• Remainder in child's estate: Estate tax again
• Remainder in grandchild's estate: Estate tax again

Dynasty trust can save tens of millions over generations!

sections.title: Predeceased Parent Exception
sections.content: If the grandchild's parent (transferor's child) is deceased at the time of transfer, the grandchild 'moves up' a generation and is no longer a skip person. This prevents GST tax when the natural order of death has been disrupted.

sections.title: Planning Strategies
items: Allocate GST exemption to appreciating assets (leverage)
Use dynasty trusts in states with no rule against perpetuities
Don't waste GST exemption on non-skip transfers
Consider life insurance trusts (ILIT) with GST exemption
Annual exclusion gifts to grandchildren save GST exemption
Watch automatic allocation rules—may need to opt out

sections.title: Memory Aid: GST Tax
sections.content:
'SKIP = GST':
S = Skip persons (2+ generations below)
K = Kills wealth if no planning (40% tax)
I = Inclusion ratio determines tax
P = Predeceased parent exception

'GST exemption is NOT PORTABLE—use it or lose it!'

sections.title: Exam Trap
sections.content: GST tax applies IN ADDITION to gift or estate tax—not instead of. A direct skip gift to a grandchild can trigger both gift tax (using lifetime exemption) AND GST tax (40% if no GST exemption allocated). Combined rate can exceed 70% without proper planning!
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: GST Tax: The Ultimate Double Whammy!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. GST tax sounds like some obscure corner of the tax code, but trust me, it can pack a serious punch. Think of it as the taxman's way of saying, "Oh, you thought you were just paying gift or estate tax? Think again!" It's basically a second layer of tax imposed on transfers to grandkids (or further down the line). I've seen families get blindsided by this, especially when dealing with substantial wealth. So, pay attention now, and save your clients (and yourself) from a major tax headache later. It’s not just about avoiding penalties, it’s about protecting legacies.

sections.title: Confusing GST Exemption with Gift/Estate Exemption
sections.content: Students often mistakenly believe that the gift and estate tax exemption ($13.61 million in 2023) automatically shields them from GST tax. While it *can* be used to offset GST tax, it's a separate calculation and allocation. Just because you're under the gift/estate exemption doesn't mean you're automatically in the clear for GST. Always remember to specifically allocate your GST exemption to the transfer. Not doing so can result in a hefty, unexpected tax bill on future generations. Think of it as two separate buckets - you need to fill both to avoid getting soaked!

sections.title: The Case of Grandpa George's Trust
sections.content: Grandpa George set up a trust for his great-grandchildren, funding it with $2 million. He assumed his estate tax planning covered everything. However, he didn't specifically allocate his GST exemption to the trust. Now, years later, when distributions are made to the great-grandchildren, the trust owes significant GST tax because the transfer was a direct skip and the GST exemption wasn't used. The lesson? Always, always, always consider and allocate the GST exemption when planning multi-generational transfers.

sections.title: GST Tax in Estate Planning Engagements
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter GST tax most often during estate planning engagements, particularly when clients have significant assets and multiple generations of heirs. When drafting wills and trusts, you must proactively address GST tax implications. This includes discussing the GST exemption, strategies for allocating it efficiently, and the potential use of trusts designed to avoid GST tax. Failing to address GST tax can be a major oversight and expose your client to significant tax liabilities. Remember, it's your job to protect their wealth for generations to come, not just the next one.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• GST tax: 40% on transfers to skip persons (2+ generations below)
• Exemption: $13.61M (2024)—NOT portable between spouses
• Three types: Direct skip, taxable termination, taxable distribution
• Inclusion ratio: Determines portion subject to GST tax
• Dynasty trusts: Allocate exemption once, shelter generations
• Predeceased parent: Grandchild moves up if parent deceased
<!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following transfers is MOST likely subject to the Generation-Skipping Transfer (GST) tax, assuming no exemptions apply?
options: A gift of $10,000 to the transferor's child.
A bequest of $1,000,000 to the transferor's grandchild.
A transfer of assets to a trust where the beneficiaries are the transferor's spouse and children.
A gift of $15,000 to a friend.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The GST tax applies to transfers to 'skip persons,' defined as individuals two or more generations younger than the transferor. A grandchild is two generations younger, making this transfer most likely subject to the GST tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who is considered a 'skip person' under GST tax rules.

reveal.question: Define a 'skip person' in the context of the Generation-Skipping Transfer (GST) tax.
reveal.answer: A 'skip person' is an individual who is two or more generations younger than the transferor. It can also be a trust if all interests in the trust are held by skip persons or if no person holds an interest in the trust and future distributions or terminations from the trust could be made only to skip persons.

knowledgeCheck.question: What is the primary purpose of the Generation-Skipping Transfer (GST) tax?
options: To encourage charitable giving.
To ensure that wealth is taxed at each generation level.
To simplify estate planning for wealthy families.
To provide funding for social security.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The GST tax is designed to prevent wealthy families from avoiding estate taxes by transferring assets directly to grandchildren or more remote descendants, thereby skipping a generation of taxation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about why the government created this tax.

title: Check-the-Box Entity Classification
description: Master elective entity classification rules for tax purposes

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Entity classification can dramatically impact tax consequences! The check-the-box rules give taxpayers flexibility to choose how their entity is taxed. Understanding defaults and elections is essential for TCP and tax planning!

sections.title: What is Check-the-Box?
sections.content:
**Elective entity classification (Form 8832):**

• Allows eligible entities to choose tax classification
• "Check a box" to select treatment
• Simplifies what used to be complex classification tests

**Applies to:** LLCs, partnerships, foreign entities
**Does NOT apply to:** Corporations (per se corporations)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Defaults
sections.content:
**"DIPS"** for Domestic LLC Defaults:

**D**isregarded = Single member
**I**f multiple members
**P**artnership = Default
**S** corp requires election!

**Domestic LLCs default to pass-through!**

sections.title: Election Options
sections.content:
**Domestic eligible entities can elect:**

• Corporation (C corp treatment)
• Partnership (if 2+ members)
• Disregarded (if single member)

**Foreign eligible entities can elect:**
• Corporation
• Partnership or disregarded

**Once classified as corporation, can also elect S corp (Form 2553)**

sections.title: Per Se Corporations
sections.content:
**Automatically treated as corporations—no election available:**

• Business entity organized as corporation
• Insurance companies
• State-chartered banks
• Government-owned entities
• Certain publicly traded partnerships
• Specific foreign entities listed in regulations

**Cannot "check the box" to avoid corporate treatment**

sections.title: Making the Election
sections.content:
**Form 8832 requirements:**

• File within 75 days BEFORE or 12 months AFTER effective date
• All owners must consent (or single owner)
• Specify effective date
• Cannot change election for 60 months (generally)

**Late elections possible with reasonable cause relief**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Conversion Consequences
sections.content:
**Converting FROM corporation is TAXABLE!**

**Corporation → Partnership or Disregarded:**
• Deemed liquidation occurs
• Corporation recognizes gain on all assets
• Shareholders recognize gain on deemed distribution
• DOUBLE TAX potential!

**Avoid electing into corporation status without careful planning**

sections.title: S Corporation Election
sections.content:
**S corp requires TWO elections:**

1. First be classified as corporation (default or 8832)
2. Then elect S corp status (Form 2553)

**S corp election deadlines:**
• Within 75 days of beginning of tax year
• Or any time during preceding year
• Late election relief available

sections.title: Planning Considerations
content: **Self-employment tax** - LLC/partnership = SE tax; S corp = reasonable salary
**Liability protection** - Same regardless of tax classification
**Exit strategy** - C corps face double tax; pass-throughs don't
**State taxes** - Some states don't recognize S corps
**Investor types** - S corps have shareholder restrictions
items: Single-member LLC defaults to Disregarded Entity (Sole Prop/Branch)
Multi-member LLC defaults to Partnership
Both can elect C Corp status via Form 8832
Once C Corp, can elect S Corp status via Form 2553 (if eligible)

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Form 8832: Your Secret Weapon for Tax Planning
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Check-the-box regulations? Sounds boring, right? But trust me, this is one of the coolest tools in the tax planning toolbox. I've seen so many clients save serious money by strategically choosing their entity classification. It's like having a tax loophole, but it's totally legal! Don't underestimate the power of Form 8832. Master this now, and you'll be a tax planning rockstar. Seriously, it's way more exciting than depreciation, I promise. Just remember the per se corporation exception. Mess that up, and you're toast.

sections.title: LLC Confusion: Partnership vs. Disregarded Entity
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking that all LLCs are automatically taxed as partnerships. This is only true if the LLC has two or more members AND doesn't elect otherwise. A single-member LLC is automatically a disregarded entity, unless it actively elects to be taxed as a corporation (C corp). Failing to understand this distinction leads to incorrectly filing Form 1065 (Partnership Return) for a single-member LLC that should be reporting on Schedule C of the owner's Form 1040. Always check the number of members and whether Form 8832 has been filed!

sections.title: Foreign Entity Fiasco: Juan's Unexpected Tax Bill
sections.content: Juan, a US resident, invests in a foreign entity that's treated as a 'per se' corporation in its home country. He assumes it's automatically taxed as a corporation in the US too. Big mistake! Even though it's a corporation in its home country, if it meets the definition of an eligible entity under US tax law, Juan can elect to treat it as a partnership or disregarded entity using Form 8832. Otherwise, the default classification rules apply, which might not be advantageous. This highlights the importance of considering US tax rules even when dealing with foreign entities.

sections.title: LLC Scenarios: Where Check-the-Box Saves the Day
sections.content: In practice, you'll use check-the-box regulations most often when dealing with new LLCs. For example, a husband and wife starting a real estate investment business might form an LLC. You'll advise them on whether to be taxed as a partnership or, if they want liability protection but simplified taxes, to elect to treat their LLC as a corporation. Also, consider foreign subsidiaries of US companies. Electing partnership treatment or disregarded entity status can simplify the repatriation of profits and avoid double taxation. Mastering Form 8832 lets you tailor the tax treatment to the client's specific needs.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Check-the-box allows elective tax classification
Single-member LLC defaults to disregarded; multi-member to partnership
