sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Nonqualified deferred compensation (NQDC) can be a minefield. Think of it like this: the IRS is *really* skeptical of letting people defer income indefinitely. They want their tax dollars! So, if you screw up the written plan, the election timing, or the distribution events, BAM! 20% penalty plus interest, and the entire deferred amount is taxable *now*. Trust me, that's a conversation no one wants to have with their client. Get those distribution events ironed out!

sections.title: NQDC: Forgetting the Strict Election Timing
sections.content: A common mistake is blowing the election timing for NQDC. Students often think, 'Oh, I can just elect to defer some bonus whenever I feel like it.' Nope! Generally, the election has to be made *before* the year the compensation is earned, or within 30 days of becoming eligible for the plan. Forgetting this crucial rule means the deferral is treated as taxable income *immediately*. Don't let your clients get tripped up by this timing trap. Document everything!

sections.title: Sarah's NQDC Nightmare: A Costly Oversight
sections.content: Sarah, a CFO, implemented an NQDC plan for her executives but forgot to include a 'change in control' distribution event in the written plan. When the company was acquired, executives wanted their deferred compensation, but because it wasn't a specified distribution event, the entire deferred amount became immediately taxable, plus the 20% penalty. Sarah's oversight cost the company and its executives a fortune in unexpected taxes and penalties. The lesson? Read. The. Plan.

sections.title: Tax-Free Employee Benefits: A Daily Win
sections.content: In practice, you'll use the tax-free benefits rules constantly. Think about structuring compensation packages. Knowing that employer-paid health insurance premiums, group-term life insurance (up to $50k), dependent care (up to $5k), and educational assistance (up to $5,250) are tax-free to the employee allows you to create more attractive and tax-efficient compensation. For example, instead of a $6,000 raise, offer $5,000 in dependent care assistance and $1,000 cash. It's all about maximizing value while minimizing the tax burden for everyone.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Salary/wages: Ordinary income when received
ISO: No tax at exercise, LTCG treatment if held properly
NQSO: Ordinary income at exercise, employer deduction
Section 409A: Strict rules for deferred comp, 20% penalty
Many fringes tax-free to employee, deductible by employer
83(b) election: 30 days to elect, upfront tax for future LTCG
Reasonableness required; $1M limit for public companies

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. implemented a nonqualified deferred compensation plan on November 15, Year 1. An employee became eligible to participate on October 1, Year 1. What is the latest date the employee can make an initial deferral election?
options: October 31, Year 1
November 15, Year 1
October 1, Year 2
November 14, Year 1
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The election must be made within 30 days of becoming eligible. The employee became eligible on October 1, Year 1, so the latest date for election is October 31, Year 1.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 30-day rule for initial deferral elections.

reveal.question: What is the maximum tax-free amount an employer can provide for dependent care assistance?

knowledgeCheck.question: An employee receives $60,000 of group-term life insurance coverage from their employer. How much of the employer-paid premium is taxable income to the employee?
options: The cost of $10,000 of coverage
The cost of $60,000 of coverage
The cost of $50,000 of coverage
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The first $50,000 of group-term life insurance coverage is tax-free. The employee must include in income the cost of the coverage exceeding $50,000, which is $10,000 in this case.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the $50,000 limit for tax-free group-term life insurance.

title: Partnership Taxation Fundamentals
description: Master pass-through taxation and basis rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships are a major pass-through entity! Understanding flow-through taxation, basis rules, and special allocations is essential for TCP. Complex but highly testable!

sections.title: Pass-Through Taxation
sections.content:
**No entity-level tax:**

• Partnership files Form 1065 (informational)
• Items flow to partners on Schedule K-1
• Character of items preserved
• Partners report on personal returns

**Partners taxed whether or not distributed!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Basis Order
sections.content:
**"I SLID" for basis adjustments:**

**I**ncrease for Income
**S**ubtract Losses
**L**ess Distributions
**I**ncrease for share of liabilities
**D**on't go below zero!

**Order matters at year-end!**

sections.title: Loss Limitation Rules
sections.content:
**Four hurdles for loss deduction:**

1. **Basis limitation:** Cannot exceed outside basis
2. **At-risk limitation:** Amount economically at risk
3. **Passive activity rules:** PAL limitations apply
4. **Excess business loss:** $500K/$250K limit

**Apply in ORDER!**

sections.title: Guaranteed Payments
sections.content:
**Payments for services/capital use:**

**To partner:**
• Ordinary income (like salary)
• Reported on K-1
• Subject to SE tax

**To partnership:**
• Deductible expense
• Before computing partnership income

**Not affected by partnership profit/loss**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Property Contributions
sections.content:
**Contributed property:**

**Generally no gain/loss recognized:**
• Partner takes carryover basis
• Partnership takes carryover basis
• Holding period tacks

**Exceptions (gain recognized):**
• Investment company
• Partnership assumes excess liabilities
• Services for interest
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Partnership Tax: It's All About the K-1
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk partnerships. Trust me, understanding partnership taxation is like learning a new language, but instead of 'Bonjour,' it's 'Schedule K-1.' You'll be drowning in these things! The key takeaway is that the partnership itself doesn't pay taxes. It's all passed through to the partners, whether they actually *see* the cash or not. So, get cozy with those K-1s; they're your new best friends (or maybe frenemies, depending on the complexity!). Remember character preservation - ordinary income stays ordinary, capital gains stay capital gains. Don't mix 'em up!

sections.title: Forgetting the Loss Deduction Order
sections.content: A very common mistake is ignoring the order of loss deduction limitations. Students often jump straight to the passive activity rules without first checking basis and at-risk amounts. Imagine a partner with a $10,000 loss, but only a $5,000 basis. Even if they're 'at-risk' for the full $10,000 and the activity is active, they can only deduct $5,000 due to the basis limitation. The rest is suspended. Remember: Basis, At-Risk, Passive Activity Rules, Excess Business Loss. It's a waterfall, not a free-for-all!

sections.title: Bob's Bogus Basis Blues
sections.content: Bob invested $50,000 in cash into the ABC Partnership. During the year, the partnership incurred a $70,000 loss, and Bob's share was $25,000. Bob thinks he can deduct the full $25,000 on his personal return. He's wrong! Bob can only deduct $25,000 to the extent of his basis: $50,000. The remaining $25,000 loss is suspended and carried forward until he has sufficient basis. This highlights the importance of tracking outside basis carefully.

sections.title: Real-World Partnership Tax Planning
sections.content: In practice, understanding partnership taxation is essential for tax planning. For example, imagine you're advising a client forming a real estate partnership. You need to project potential losses and ensure the partners have sufficient basis and are 'at-risk' to deduct those losses in the early years. You'll also advise on strategies to increase basis, like contributing property or guaranteeing partnership debt. Proactive planning prevents unpleasant surprises when tax time rolls around. Ignoring these rules can lead to significant tax liabilities and penalties for your clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: No entity tax—items flow through to partners
Character of income/loss preserved
Partner basis: Contributions + Income − Distributions − Losses + Liabilities
Four loss limits: Basis, At-risk, PAL, Excess business
Guaranteed payments: Ordinary income to partner
Property contributions: Generally no gain/loss
Schedule K-1 reports each partner's share

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following represents the correct order in which partnership loss limitations are applied?
options: Basis limitation, At-risk limitation, Passive activity rules, Excess business loss limitation.
At-risk limitation, Basis limitation, Passive activity rules, Excess business loss limitation.
Passive activity rules, Basis limitation, At-risk limitation, Excess business loss limitation.
Excess business loss limitation, Passive activity rules, At-risk limitation, Basis limitation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The loss limitations are applied in the following order: 1. Basis limitation. 2. At-risk limitation. 3. Passive activity rules. 4. Excess business loss limitation. Applying them in this specific sequence determines the deductible loss for a partner.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which limitation is the most fundamental, considering the partner's investment.

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of Form 1065?
reveal.answer: Form 1065 is an informational return filed by the partnership. It reports the partnership's income, deductions, and other items, but the partnership itself does not pay income tax. The information on Form 1065 is used to prepare Schedule K-1s for each partner, which the partners then use to report their share of the partnership's income or loss on their individual income tax returns.

knowledgeCheck.question: Partner A has a 30% distributive share of partnership income. The partnership earned $100,000 in ordinary income and distributed $20,000 to Partner A during the year. How much income will Partner A report on their individual income tax return?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Partner A will report $30,000 on their individual income tax return. Partnerships are pass-through entities, meaning the partners are taxed on their distributive share of the partnership's income, regardless of whether the income is actually distributed to them. In this case, Partner A's distributive share is 30% of $100,000, which equals $30,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that partnerships are pass-through entities. Distributions do not determine taxable income.

title: S Corporation Taxation
description: Understand S corp requirements and taxation rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corps combine corporate liability protection with pass-through taxation! Understanding eligibility, basis rules, and the differences from partnerships is critical for TCP!

sections.title: S Corporation Requirements
sections.content:
**To elect S status:**

• Domestic corporation
• 100 or fewer shareholders
• Only eligible shareholders (individuals, estates, certain trusts)
• One class of stock
• No ineligible corporations as shareholders

**All shareholders must consent to election!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: S Corp Basis
sections.content:
**S Corp = Stock + Debt (personal loans only)**

**Key difference from partnership:**
• Entity debt does NOT increase basis
• Only loans FROM shareholder TO S corp

**"S = Shareholders own debt only"**

sections.title: Shareholder Basis Rules
sections.content:
**Ordering rules (year-end):**

1. Increase for income items
2. Decrease for distributions (not below zero)
3. Decrease for non-deductible expenses
4. Decrease for losses (not below zero)

**Losses in excess of stock basis:**
• Reduce debt basis (creates gain later)
• Carry forward excess losses

sections.title: Built-In Gains Tax
sections.content:
**When C corp converts to S:**

• 5-year recognition period
• Tax on built-in gains at corporate rate
• Limited to net built-in gain at conversion

**Purpose:** Prevent C corps from avoiding double taxation by converting to S

**Also: LIFO recapture tax**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Reasonable Compensation
sections.content:
**Shareholder-employees MUST receive:**

• Reasonable salary for services
• Subject to FICA/payroll taxes

**IRS scrutiny if:**
• All income as distributions
• No or minimal salary
• Services rendered

**Consequences:** Reclassification + penalties
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: S Corp Elections: Don't Miss the Deadline!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. S corp elections seem simple, right? But I've seen so many businesses screw this up. It's usually because they file late! Remember, Form 2553 must be filed by March 15th if you want S status for the current year (for calendar year entities). Miss that deadline and you're stuck as a C corp for another year. Think of it like this: procrastinate, and the IRS will procrastinate on giving you those sweet pass-through benefits. Set a reminder, people!

sections.title: Basis Calculations: Mixing Stock and Debt!
sections.content: Students often mix up stock basis and debt basis when dealing with S corp losses. Remember, losses first reduce stock basis to zero. Only *after* stock basis is exhausted do losses reduce debt basis. Then, when that debt is repaid, there can be taxable income! The key difference is that stock basis is the initial investment plus income items and minus distributions/losses, while debt basis is direct loans made from the shareholder to the S corp. Don't treat them the same!

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Loss Limitation
sections.content: Sarah owns 60% of an S corp and her initial stock basis was $20,000. The S corp had a $50,000 loss this year. Sarah can only deduct $20,000 of the loss on her personal return. The remaining $10,000 (60% of the $50,000 loss = $30,000, limited to her $20,000 basis) is suspended and carried forward until she has sufficient basis again. This highlights the importance of tracking shareholder basis annually.

sections.title: Basis Tracking: A Year-Round Task
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter S corp basis calculations constantly, especially when preparing K-1s. That's why mastering the ordering rules is crucial. You'll need to track income, deductions, and distributions throughout the year to accurately determine each shareholder's basis. Many firms use specialized software to manage these calculations, but understanding the underlying principles is essential for spotting errors and providing sound tax advice. Neglecting this can lead to incorrect loss deductions and potential penalties.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: S corp: 100 shareholders, one class stock, eligible owners
Pass-through taxation, no entity tax (usually)
Basis includes stock + shareholder loans only
Entity debt does NOT increase shareholder basis
Built-in gains tax on C to S conversion
Reasonable compensation required
Distributions tax-free to extent of basis

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following would disqualify a corporation from electing S corporation status?
options: Having 95 individual shareholders who are all US citizens.
Having one class of stock with differing voting rights.
Having an irrevocable trust as a shareholder.
Having a partnership as a shareholder.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A partnership is an ineligible shareholder for S corporation status. S corporations can only have individuals, estates, and certain trusts as shareholders. Differing voting rights within a single class of stock are permitted. 95 shareholders is under the 100 shareholder limit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the specific requirements for eligible shareholders.

reveal.question: What is the proper order for adjustments to shareholder basis at year-end?
reveal.answer:
1. Increase for income items.
2. Decrease for distributions (but not below zero).
3. Decrease for non-deductible expenses.
4. Decrease for losses (but not below zero).

knowledgeCheck.question: An S corporation shareholder has a stock basis of $10,000 and a debt basis of $5,000 at the beginning of the year. The S corporation incurs a loss of $20,000. What amount of loss can the shareholder deduct in the current year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The shareholder can deduct losses up to their stock and debt basis. In this case, the shareholder can deduct $10,000 (stock basis) + $5,000 (debt basis) = $15,000. The remaining $5,000 loss is carried forward.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that losses first reduce stock basis, then debt basis.

title: C Corporation Taxation Overview
description: Understand corporate tax calculation and distributions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: C corps face double taxation—understanding how to minimize it is key! Corporate tax rates, E&P calculations, and dividend treatment are essential TCP knowledge!

sections.title: Corporate Tax Rate
sections.content:
**Post-TCJA:**

• Flat 21% corporate rate
• No graduated brackets
• No AMT for most corporations

**Personal service corporations:**
• Also 21% (previously 35% flat rate)

**State taxes:** Additional (vary by state)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: E&P Purpose
sections.content:
**E&P = "Economic Ability to Pay Dividends"**

**Distribution treatment:**
1. Dividend (to extent of E&P)
2. Return of capital (to extent of basis)
3. Capital gain (excess)

**E&P determines dividend portion!**

sections.title: Distribution Ordering
sections.content:
**When corporation distributes cash:**

**1. Current E&P first** (pro rata by date)
**2. Accumulated E&P** (in order made)
**3. Return of capital** (reduce stock basis)
**4. Capital gain** (after basis is zero)

**Each layer has different tax treatment**

sections.title: Dividends Received Deduction
sections.content:
**Corporate shareholders get DRD:**

**Ownership %** → **DRD %**
• < 20%: 50% DRD
• 20-80%: 65% DRD
• > 80%: 100% DRD (affiliated group)

**Purpose:** Reduce triple taxation

**Taxable income limitation may apply**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Accumulated Earnings Tax
sections.content:
**20% penalty on excess accumulation:**

**When:**
• Accumulation beyond reasonable business needs
• Purpose: Avoid shareholder-level tax

**Credit allowed:**
• Greater of $250K or business needs
• $150K for PSCs

**Document business purpose for accumulation!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: 21% Flat Rate: It's Not as Simple as It Seems!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. The 21% corporate tax rate sounds simple, right? A welcome break from the old graduated brackets. But don't get complacent! Remember state taxes pile on top of that, and figuring out E&P is where things get hairy. I've seen partnerships try to sneak in as C-corps hoping for the lower rate, but they get crushed when the IRS reclassifies them. So, master these rules; it's the bread and butter of corporate tax work.

sections.title: E&P Confusion: Current vs. Accumulated
sections.content: Students often get tripped up distinguishing between current and accumulated E&P. They might just add them together without considering the distribution rules. Remember, distributions are deemed to come from current E&P first (pro rata by date). Only after current E&P is exhausted do distributions tap into accumulated E&P (in chronological order). Failing to account for this sequencing can lead to incorrect calculations of taxable dividends and return of capital, resulting in penalties.

sections.title: Sarah's Dividend Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, CFO of Acme Corp, needs to advise shareholders on the tax implications of a recent $50,000 distribution. Acme has $20,000 in current E&P and $40,000 in accumulated E&P. Sarah needs to explain that the $50,000 distribution is treated first as a $20,000 dividend (from current E&P), then a $30,000 dividend (from accumulated E&P). Understanding the E&P layering is crucial for providing accurate tax advice to Acme's shareholders.

sections.title: Real-World Dividend Analysis
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter these C-corp distribution rules when preparing tax returns for privately held companies. You'll analyze their financial statements to determine current and accumulated E&P. Clients often need help understanding the tax implications of dividends, especially when significant accumulated E&P exists. Being able to clearly explain the dividend rules and their impact on shareholders' tax liabilities is a valuable skill that clients will appreciate and pay for. Mastering this now will save you countless hours of research and potential errors later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Corporate rate: Flat 21%
E&P measures ability to pay dividends
Distributions: Dividend → Return of capital → Capital gain
Current E&P allocated pro rata by date
DRD reduces corporate dividend taxation
Accumulated earnings tax: 20% on excess
Document business needs for accumulations

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp., a regular C corporation, has taxable income of $500,000. What is its federal income tax liability?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since TCJA, the corporate tax rate is a flat 21%. Therefore, $500,000 * 0.21 = $105,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the flat corporate tax rate.

reveal.question: In what order are distributions from a corporation's E&P taxed to a shareholder?
reveal.answer:
1. Current E&P (pro rata).
2. Accumulated E&P (in order made).
3. Return of Capital (reduce stock basis).
4. Capital Gain (after basis is zero).

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. has $50,000 of current E&P and $25,000 of accumulated E&P. A shareholder receives a distribution of $90,000. How much of the distribution is treated as a return of capital?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, current E&P of $50,000 is distributed. Then, accumulated E&P of $25,000 is distributed. This leaves $90,000 - $50,000 - $25,000 = $15,000 to be treated as a return of capital.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Follow the correct order for E&P distribution.

title: Tax Credits: Business and Individual
description: Understand major tax credits and their application

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Credits are more valuable than deductions—they reduce tax dollar-for-dollar! Understanding major credits, limitations, and refundability is essential for tax planning!

sections.title: Credit vs Deduction
sections.content:
**Key difference:**

**Deduction:** Reduces taxable income
• Value = Amount × Marginal rate

**Credit:** Reduces tax directly
• Value = Dollar for dollar

**Example:** $1,000 at 24% bracket
• Deduction saves: $240
• Credit saves: $1,000

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Refundable Credits
sections.content:
**"RACE" for refundable:**

**R**efundable = Get money even if no tax
**A**OTC (40% refundable)
**C**hild Tax Credit (partially)
**E**ITC (fully refundable)

**Nonrefundable = Only reduces tax to zero**

sections.title: Business Tax Credits
sections.content:
**Common business credits:**

• **R&D Credit:** Qualified research expenses
• **Work Opportunity Credit:** Targeted group hiring
• **Disabled Access Credit:** Small business accessibility
• **Low-Income Housing Credit:** Affordable housing
• **General Business Credit:** Carryback 1, Forward 20

**Subject to ordering and limitation rules**

sections.title: Foreign Tax Credit
sections.content:
**For taxes paid to foreign countries:**

**Limitation:**
FTC = US Tax × (Foreign source income / Worldwide income)

**Cannot exceed US tax on foreign income**

**Excess credits:**
• Carryback 1 year
• Carryforward 10 years

**Alternative: Deduct instead of credit**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Phase-outs
sections.content:
**Many credits phase out at higher incomes:**

**Child Tax Credit:** Starts at $200K/$400K
**EITC:** Varies by filing status
**Education credits:** ~$80K-90K range

**Always check income limits!**

**Phase-out can make marginal rate very high**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Credits vs. Deductions: The Real Deal
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Credits and deductions? They sound boring, but they're HUGE. Think of deductions like getting a discount coupon – it's nice, but the credit? That's like finding a twenty in your old jeans. Dollar for dollar, baby! Don't gloss over this stuff. Knowing the difference between an R&D credit and simply deducting research expenses can save your client (and your career) a ton of money. Seriously, nail this down. Your clients will love you, and your wallet will too.

sections.title: Forgetting the Phase-Outs!
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming a taxpayer is eligible for the full credit amount. Many credits, like the Low-Income Housing Credit, have complex phase-out rules based on income or other factors. Students often calculate the maximum credit without checking if the taxpayer's circumstances allow for it. Always remember to verify eligibility requirements and phase-out thresholds! Failing to do so leads to incorrect tax calculations and potential penalties for your clients. Double-check the fine print!

sections.title: Sarah's Small Business Woes
sections.content: Sarah owns a bakery and recently renovated her entrance to be wheelchair accessible. She spent $15,000. She's excited about the Disabled Access Credit, but she's confused. The learning point? The Disabled Access Credit is for small businesses (less than $1 million in revenue or fewer than 30 employees) and only covers expenses exceeding $250, capped at $10,250. Sarah can only claim a credit based on $9,750 (15,000 - 250 = 14,750 limited to 10,250, 50% of that is 5,125). It's crucial to understand the credit's limitations.

sections.title: Landing the Big Client with R&D
sections.content: In practice, you'll often use the R&D credit as a selling point when pitching to tech startups or manufacturing companies. They often don't realize they qualify for this credit because they think 'research' means lab coats and test tubes. Emphasize that activities like developing new software, improving manufacturing processes, or even designing a new product feature can qualify. Identifying and claiming this credit can result in significant tax savings, making you a hero in your client's eyes and securing long-term business.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credits reduce tax dollar-for-dollar
Refundable credits can create refund
CTC: $2,000, partially refundable
EITC: Fully refundable, low income
Education: AOTC ($2,500) vs LLC ($2,000)
FTC limited to US tax on foreign income
Watch for phase-outs at higher incomes

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer in the 32% marginal tax bracket has $5,000 in qualified expenses. If the taxpayer can choose between a $5,000 deduction and a $500 tax credit, which option will result in the greatest tax savings?
options: A $5,000 deduction
A $500 tax credit
Both options will result in the same tax savings
More information is needed to determine the greatest tax savings
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A $5,000 deduction will result in tax savings of $5,000 * 0.32 = $1,600. A $500 tax credit will result in tax savings of $500. Therefore, the deduction provides greater tax savings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember how deductions and credits impact tax liability differently.

reveal.question: What are the carryback and carryforward rules for the General Business Credit?
reveal.answer: The General Business Credit can be carried back one year and carried forward 20 years.

values: R&D Credit
Qualified research expenses

values: Work Opportunity Credit

values: Disabled Access Credit

values: Low-Income Housing Credit

title: Qualified Business Income Deduction (Section 199A)
description: Master the 20% pass-through deduction rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The QBI deduction can be worth 20% of qualified business income! Understanding the limitations, phase-outs, and planning opportunities is essential for pass-through entity planning!

sections.title: Basic QBI Deduction
sections.content:
**20% deduction for:**

• Sole proprietors (Schedule C)
• Partners (Schedule K-1)
• S corp shareholders (Schedule K-1)
• REIT dividends
• PTP income

**Deducted from AGI (not itemized)**

**Maximum: 20% of QBI**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SSTB
sections.content:
**Specified Service Trade or Business:**

**"HELP!"**
**H**ealth
**E**ngineering... wait no, not included!
**L**aw
**P**erforming arts

**Also:** Accounting, consulting, financial services, athletics

**Engineering and Architecture are NOT SSTB!**

sections.title: W-2 Wage Limitation
sections.content:
**Above threshold, limited to greater of:**

**Option 1:** 50% of W-2 wages
**Option 2:** 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA

**UBIA:** Unadjusted Basis Immediately After Acquisition (property)

**Below threshold:** No W-2/property limit applies

sections.title: Planning Strategies
sections.content:
**Maximize QBI deduction:**

• Aggregate related businesses
• Increase W-2 wages (S corps especially)
• Consider REIT investments
• Manage taxable income around thresholds
• Evaluate entity structure

**SSTB owners:** Stay below threshold if possible

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: What's NOT QBI
sections.content:
**Excluded from QBI:**

• Wages/salary (employee income)
• Investment income (portfolio)
• Guaranteed payments for services
• Reasonable compensation (S corp)
• Short-term capital gains

**QBI = Business PROFIT, not all income!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: 199A: The Deduction That Keeps on Giving (Maybe)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, Section 199A can feel like a tax code version of Inception. Deductions within deductions! As a practitioner, I've seen so many small business owners miss out on this because they get bogged down in the W-2 wage and UBIA limitations. Don't just blindly input numbers into your tax software. Actually understand the calculation. Is your client's income above the threshold? Do they even have W-2 wages? A little planning can make a HUGE difference in their tax liability. Treat it like a puzzle, not a chore!

sections.title: Threshold Traps: Above, Below, and the Gray Area
sections.content: A common mistake is applying the W-2/UBIA limitation even when the taxpayer's income is *below* the threshold. Remember, those limitations only kick in when taxable income exceeds the threshold. Students often memorize the formulas without understanding the context. For example, someone with $100,000 of QBI and taxable income of $150,000 can take the full 20% QBI deduction ($20,000) regardless of W-2 wages or UBIA. Always check the income level first! Ignoring this simple step could cost your clients thousands.

sections.title: Sarah's S Corp Struggle: W-2 Wages to the Rescue!
sections.content: Sarah owns a successful marketing agency structured as an S corp. Her QBI is $200,000, but her taxable income is $350,000, putting her above the threshold. Initially, she was disappointed to learn her 199A deduction would be limited. However, after realizing she could significantly increase her W-2 wages paid, she restructured her compensation, boosting her deduction. The takeaway: strategic planning around W-2 wages can unlock significant tax savings for S corp owners in this situation.

sections.title: QBI in the Real World: Planning is Key
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter the QBI deduction when working with small business clients, especially sole proprietors, partners, and S corp owners. You'll need to gather information on their QBI, W-2 wages paid, and UBIA of qualified property. This is where good client communication is essential. Proactive planning, such as adjusting W-2 wages or considering investments in qualified property, can significantly impact the deduction. Understanding the nuances of Section 199A is crucial for providing valuable tax advice and helping clients minimize their tax liabilities.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 20% deduction on qualified business income
Available to pass-through owners and REIT investors
W-2 wage/property limit above threshold
SSTB completely excluded above threshold
Engineering and architecture NOT SSTB
Excludes wages, investment income, guaranteed payments
Below AGI deduction (not itemized)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following business structures is NOT directly eligible for the Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction under Section 199A?
options: A sole proprietorship operating a retail store.
A partner receiving a Schedule K-1 from a partnership.
A C corporation shareholder receiving dividends.
An S corporation shareholder receiving a Schedule K-1 from the S corporation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: C corporations are not pass-through entities. The QBI deduction applies to income from pass-through entities like sole proprietorships, partnerships, and S corporations. Dividends from REITs are also eligible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which entities are considered 'pass-through' businesses.

reveal.question: Define UBIA in the context of the QBI deduction.
reveal.answer: UBIA stands for Unadjusted Basis Immediately After Acquisition. It refers to the original cost basis of qualified property placed in service and still used in the qualified business. It is used in the W-2 wage and UBIA limitation calculation when a taxpayer's taxable income exceeds the threshold.

knowledgeCheck.question: For a taxpayer above the taxable income threshold for the QBI deduction, the deduction is limited to the GREATER of which of the following?
options: 50% of W-2 wages or 20% of QBI
25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA or 20% of QBI
50% of W-2 wages or 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA
20% of QBI or 2.5% of UBIA
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When taxable income exceeds the threshold, the QBI deduction is limited to the GREATER of: (1) 50% of W-2 wages or (2) 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA. This is then compared to 20% of QBI, and the SMALLER of the two amounts is the deduction.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the wording of the question and what the limitation is based on.

sections.title: QBI Deduction: Above vs. Below Threshold

rows.label: W-2 Wage/UBIA Limitation
values: No limitation applies.
Limited to the GREATER of 50% of W-2 wages OR 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA.

rows.label: QBI Deduction Amount
values: Up to 20% of QBI is deductible.
Up to 20% of QBI is deductible, but cannot exceed the W-2 wage/UBIA limitation if applicable.
